    🚀 GitHub Actions Complete Reference Guide

    This comprehensive guide covers everything you need to master GitHub Actions for your Django applications. From workflow events and commands to variables, expressions, contexts, and deployment strategies — all with real-world examples based on the JobPost application.

    📚 What This Guide Covers

    Master these 9 essential GitHub Actions topics to build robust CI/CD pipelines:

    🔄

    Workflow Events

    32 events that trigger workflows — from push and pull_request to schedule, workflow_dispatch, and repository_dispatch.

    32 Events
    ⚙️

    Workflow Commands

    15 commands for controlling workflow behavior — debug messages, grouping, masking, environment files, and job summaries.

    15 Commands
    📦

    Variables

    Environment variables, configuration variables, naming conventions, precedence rules, and scope management.

    6 Topics
    🧮

    Expressions

    Literals, operators, functions, status checks, and object filters for dynamic workflow configuration.

    4 Topics
    🎯

    Contexts

    14 context objects providing runtime information — github, env, vars, job, steps, runner, secrets, matrix, and more.

    14 Contexts
    🚀

    Deployments

    Environment management, protection rules, deployment secrets, and environment-specific variables.

    3 Topics
    💾

    Dependency Caching

    Cache action usage, key matching strategies, setup actions for package managers, and cache policies.

    6 Topics
    ♻️

    Reusable Workflows

    Workflow templates, reusable workflow patterns, YAML anchors and aliases for DRY configurations.

    3 Topics
    📝

    Metadata Syntax

    Action metadata for custom actions — name, author, description, inputs, outputs, and branding.

    2 Topics

    🏗️ Project Context: Django JobPost Application

    All examples in this guide are based on a real Django project structure:

    📁

    anihpj/

    Django Project
    • 📄 __init__.py — Package marker
    • 📄 settings.py — Project settings
    • 📄 urls.py — URL configuration
    • 📄 wsgi.py — WSGI application
    • 📄 asgi.py — ASGI application
    📁

    jobpost/

    Django App
    • 📄 models.py — Skills, Author, Location, JobPost
    • 📄 views.py — View functions
    • 📄 urls.py — App URL patterns
    • 📄 admin.py — Admin configuration
    • 📄 tests.py — Unit tests
    • 📁 migrations/ — Database migrations

    🛠️ How to Implement GitHub Actions

    Follow these steps to add GitHub Actions to your Django JobPost project:

    1

    Create Workflow Directory

    Create .github/workflows/ folder in your repository root. This is where all your workflow YAML files will live.

    2

    Define Your Workflow File

    Create a .yml or .yaml file (e.g., ci.yml) with your workflow configuration including name, triggers, jobs, and steps.

    3

    Configure Event Triggers

    Use the on: key to specify which events trigger your workflow — push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch, or any of the 32 supported events.

    4

    Set Up Jobs and Steps

    Define jobs under jobs: with a runner and sequential steps. Each step can run commands or use pre-built actions from the GitHub Marketplace.

    5

    Use Contexts and Expressions

    Leverage contexts like ${{ github.* }}, ${{ secrets.* }}, and expressions for dynamic configuration and conditional execution.

    6

    Configure Secrets and Variables

    Store sensitive data in GitHub Secrets and use configuration variables for environment-specific settings. Access them via ${{ secrets.NAME }} and ${{ vars.NAME }}.

    ⚡ Quick Reference — Workflow File Location

    📁 .github/workflows/
    📄 ci.yml or *.yaml
    🔧 YAML syntax required
    🌐 Runs on GitHub servers

    📝 Sample Django CI Workflow

    Here's a complete example for the JobPost application:

    # .github/workflows/django-ci.yml
name: Django JobPost CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'  # Weekly on Monday at 6 AM

env:
  PYTHON_VERSION: '3.11'
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: 'anihpj.settings'

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:15
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: jobpost_test
          POSTGRES_USER: postgres
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: --health-cmd pg_isready --health-interval 10s

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ env.PYTHON_VERSION }}

      - name: Cache pip dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache/pip
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-${{ hashFiles('requirements.txt') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-pip-

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
          pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run migrations
        run: python manage.py migrate

      - name: Run tests
        run: python manage.py test jobpost --verbosity=2

      - name: Upload coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          token: ${{ secrets.CODECOV_TOKEN }}

    💡 Pro Tip

    Start with a simple workflow and gradually add complexity. Use the examples in each section of this guide to build production-ready CI/CD pipelines for your Django applications.

    ⚖️ GitHub Actions vs Other CI/CD Tools

    How GitHub Actions compares to popular CI/CD alternatives:

    Feature GitHub Actions Jenkins GitLab CI CircleCI
    Native GitHub Integration ✓ ~ ✗ ~
    Free for Public Repos ✓ ✓ ✓ ~
    YAML Configuration ✓ ~ ✓ ✓
    Marketplace Actions ✓ 15K+ ~ Plugins ~ ~ Orbs
    Self-Hosted Runners ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓
    Matrix Builds ✓ ~ ✓ ✓
    Container Services ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓

    🔄 Common Workflow Patterns

    Popular CI/CD patterns you'll implement with this guide:

    🧪
    Test on Push

    Run Django tests automatically on every push to main or feature branches.

    🔍
    PR Validation

    Validate pull requests with linting, tests, and code coverage checks before merge.

    📦
    Build & Deploy

    Build Docker images and deploy to staging/production on release tags.

    ⏰
    Scheduled Tasks

    Run database backups, cleanup jobs, or security scans on a schedule.

    🔄
    Matrix Testing

    Test JobPost across multiple Python versions and Django versions simultaneously.

    🛡️
    Security Scanning

    Automated dependency scanning and vulnerability checks on every commit.

    🎬 Most Used Actions for Django

    Essential marketplace actions for Django JobPost workflows:

    Action Purpose Type
    actions/checkout@v4 Clone repository code into runner Official
    actions/setup-python@v5 Install and configure Python version Official
    actions/cache@v4 Cache pip dependencies for faster builds Official
    codecov/codecov-action@v4 Upload test coverage reports Verified
    docker/build-push-action@v5 Build and push Docker images Verified
    actions/upload-artifact@v4 Upload build artifacts and test reports Official

    🔒 Security Best Practices

    🔐 Never hardcode secrets in workflows
    🔐 Use secrets.* for sensitive data
    🔐 Pin action versions with SHA
    🔐 Limit GITHUB_TOKEN permissions
    🔐 Review third-party actions carefully
    🔐 Use environment protection rules

    🎖️ Skills You'll Master

    📝 YAML Workflows 🔔 Event Triggers ⚙️ Workflow Commands 📦 Variables & Secrets 🎭 Contexts & Expressions 🚀 Deployments 💾 Caching Strategies ♻️ Reusable Workflows 🔧 Custom Actions 🧪 Matrix Testing 🐳 Container Services 🛡️ Security Hardening

    ✅ Best Practices Checklist

    Follow these guidelines for production-ready workflows:

    ✅ Use specific action versions — Pin to @v4 or SHA for stability
    ✅ Cache dependencies — Speed up builds with pip/npm caching
    ✅ Use matrix testing — Test across Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12
    ✅ Add status badges — Show build status in README
    ✅ Use environment protection — Require approvals for production
    ✅ Set timeout limits — Prevent runaway jobs with timeout-minutes
    ✅ Use concurrency groups — Cancel redundant workflow runs
    ✅ Add job summaries — Create readable workflow reports

    Context Properties & Event Payloads

    Docs: Contexts reference | Webhooks: Event payloads

    Understanding how to access event data, user information, and repository details using dot notation property accessors like .title, .body, and .user.login.

    Overview & Concepts

    When a GitHub event triggers your workflow, GitHub sends a JSON payload containing detailed information about the event. You access this data using dot notation property accessors within the ${{ }} expression syntax. These accessors let you navigate nested objects to retrieve specific values.

    How Property Access Works

    🎯
    Event Triggers
    push, PR, issue...
    →
    📦
    JSON Payload
    github.event
    →
    🔑
    Dot Notation
    .property.nested
    →
    ✨
    Value Retrieved
    Use in workflow
    📝 Key Concepts:

    • Contexts — Named objects containing workflow data: github, env, job, steps, runner, secrets, strategy, matrix, needs, inputs
    • Dot Notation — Access nested properties: github.event.pull_request.title
    • Bracket Notation — Alternative syntax: github.event['pull_request']['title'] (useful for special characters)
    • Object Filters — Wildcard access to array properties: github.event.issue.labels.*.name

    Expression Syntax

    # Basic property access
${{ github.event.action }}

# Nested property access
${{ github.event.pull_request.user.login }}

# Bracket notation (for special characters or variables)
${{ github.event['pull_request']['head']['ref'] }}

# Object filter (get all names from labels array)
${{ github.event.issue.labels.*.name }}

    Available Contexts

    GitHub Actions provides several contexts, each containing different types of workflow data:

    Context Description Common Properties
    github Information about the workflow run and event that triggered it .event, .sha, .ref, .actor
    env Environment variables set in workflow, job, or step .MY_VAR, .NODE_ENV
    vars Configuration variables set at repository, environment, or org level .DEPLOY_URL, .API_KEY
    job Information about the currently running job .status, .container, .services
    steps Outputs and status of steps in the current job .<step_id>.outputs, .<step_id>.outcome
    runner Information about the runner executing the job .os, .arch, .name, .temp
    secrets Secrets configured for the repository or organization .GITHUB_TOKEN, .MY_SECRET
    strategy Matrix strategy configuration for the current job .fail-fast, .max-parallel
    matrix Current matrix values for the job .os, .node-version
    needs Outputs from jobs this job depends on .<job_id>.outputs, .<job_id>.result
    inputs Inputs passed to reusable workflows or manual triggers .environment, .version

    github.event Properties

    Docs: github context

    The github.event object contains the full webhook payload for the event that triggered the workflow.

    Core github Context Properties

    These properties are available on the github context regardless of which event triggered the workflow:

    Property Description Example Value
    github.event Full webhook event payload (varies by event type) JSON object
    github.event_name Name of the event that triggered the workflow push, pull_request
    github.action Unique ID of the current action or step name __run
    github.actor Username who initiated the workflow octocat
    github.actor_id User ID who initiated the workflow 583231
    github.repository Repository owner and name owner/repo
    github.repository_owner Repository owner (user or org) owner
    github.sha Commit SHA that triggered the workflow ffac537e6cbb...
    github.ref Branch or tag ref that triggered the workflow refs/heads/main
    github.ref_name Short ref name (branch or tag) main, v1.0.0
    github.ref_type Type of ref branch, tag
    github.head_ref Source branch (PR events only) feature-branch
    github.base_ref Target branch (PR events only) main
    github.workflow Name of the workflow CI Pipeline
    github.run_id Unique ID for the workflow run 1658821493
    github.run_number Sequential number for workflow runs 42
    github.job Job ID of the current job build
    github.token Automatically generated GITHUB_TOKEN ghs_xxxx...
    github.server_url GitHub server URL https://github.com
    github.api_url GitHub API URL https://api.github.com

    Common github.event.action Values

    The github.event.action property indicates what activity type triggered the event:

    📝

    Issues & PRs

    opened, closed, reopened, edited, assigned, unassigned, labeled, unlabeled

    💬

    Comments

    created, edited, deleted

    👀

    Reviews

    submitted, edited, dismissed

    🏷️

    Releases

    published, unpublished, created, edited, deleted, prereleased, released

    Common Payload Properties

    Docs: Webhook events and payloads

    Properties that appear across multiple event types with consistent structure.

    Sender (Who Triggered the Event)

    The sender object identifies who performed the action that triggered the event:

    Property Description Example
    github.event.sender.login Username of the user who triggered the event octocat
    github.event.sender.id Numeric ID of the user 583231
    github.event.sender.type Type of account User, Bot
    github.event.sender.avatar_url URL to user's avatar image https://avatar...
    github.event.sender.html_url URL to user's GitHub profile https://github.com/octocat

    Repository Information

    The repository object contains details about the repository:

    Property Description Example
    github.event.repository.name Repository name (without owner) my-repo
    github.event.repository.full_name Full repository name with owner owner/my-repo
    github.event.repository.private Whether the repository is private true, false
    github.event.repository.owner.login Repository owner username owner
    github.event.repository.default_branch Default branch name main
    github.event.repository.html_url URL to the repository on GitHub https://github.com/...
    github.event.repository.clone_url HTTPS clone URL https://github.com/....git
    github.event.repository.ssh_url SSH clone URL git@github.com:...
    github.event.repository.language Primary programming language Python
    github.event.repository.topics Array of repository topics ["django", "api"]

    Organization (if applicable)

    Property Description Example
    github.event.organization.login Organization username my-org
    github.event.organization.id Organization ID 123456
    github.event.organization.description Organization description Our team...

    Event-Specific Properties

    Docs: Webhook payloads reference

    Properties unique to specific event types, organized by event category.

    Pull Request Properties

    Available when pull_request or pull_request_target events trigger:

    Property Description
    github.event.pull_request.number PR number (e.g., 42)
    github.event.pull_request.title PR title text
    github.event.pull_request.body PR description/body text
    github.event.pull_request.state PR state: open or closed
    github.event.pull_request.merged Whether the PR was merged (true/false)
    github.event.pull_request.draft Whether the PR is a draft
    github.event.pull_request.user.login Username who created the PR
    github.event.pull_request.head.ref Source branch name
    github.event.pull_request.head.sha Source branch HEAD commit SHA
    github.event.pull_request.base.ref Target branch name
    github.event.pull_request.base.sha Target branch commit SHA
    github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name Array of label names (use object filter)
    github.event.pull_request.assignees.*.login Array of assignee usernames
    github.event.pull_request.requested_reviewers.*.login Array of requested reviewer usernames
    github.event.pull_request.milestone.title Associated milestone title
    github.event.pull_request.html_url URL to the PR on GitHub
    github.event.pull_request.additions Number of lines added
    github.event.pull_request.deletions Number of lines deleted
    github.event.pull_request.changed_files Number of files changed

    Issue Properties

    Available when issues or issue_comment events trigger:

    Property Description
    github.event.issue.number Issue number (e.g., 123)
    github.event.issue.title Issue title text
    github.event.issue.body Issue description/body text
    github.event.issue.state Issue state: open or closed
    github.event.issue.user.login Username who created the issue
    github.event.issue.labels.*.name Array of label names
    github.event.issue.assignees.*.login Array of assignee usernames
    github.event.issue.milestone.title Associated milestone title
    github.event.issue.html_url URL to the issue on GitHub
    github.event.issue.comments Number of comments on the issue
    github.event.issue.locked Whether the issue is locked

    Comment Properties

    Available when issue_comment, pull_request_review_comment, or discussion_comment events trigger:

    Property Description
    github.event.comment.id Unique comment ID
    github.event.comment.body Comment text content
    github.event.comment.user.login Username who wrote the comment
    github.event.comment.created_at Timestamp when comment was created
    github.event.comment.updated_at Timestamp when comment was last updated
    github.event.comment.html_url URL to the comment on GitHub

    Push Event Properties

    Available when push events trigger:

    Property Description
    github.event.before SHA of the previous HEAD commit
    github.event.after SHA of the new HEAD commit
    github.event.ref Full ref (e.g., refs/heads/main)
    github.event.pusher.name Name of the user who pushed
    github.event.pusher.email Email of the user who pushed
    github.event.forced Whether the push was a force push
    github.event.created Whether this push created the ref
    github.event.deleted Whether this push deleted the ref
    github.event.commits Array of commit objects in the push
    github.event.commits.*.id SHA of each commit
    github.event.commits.*.message Commit message text
    github.event.commits.*.author.name Commit author name
    github.event.head_commit.message Message of the HEAD commit
    github.event.compare URL to compare changes

    Release Properties

    Available when release events trigger:

    Property Description
    github.event.release.tag_name Release tag (e.g., v1.0.0)
    github.event.release.name Release title/name
    github.event.release.body Release notes text
    github.event.release.draft Whether it's a draft release
    github.event.release.prerelease Whether it's a pre-release
    github.event.release.target_commitish Branch or commit the release targets
    github.event.release.author.login Username who created the release
    github.event.release.html_url URL to the release on GitHub
    github.event.release.assets Array of release assets

    Review Properties

    Available when pull_request_review events trigger:

    Property Description
    github.event.review.id Review ID
    github.event.review.body Review comment text
    github.event.review.state approved, changes_requested, commented, dismissed
    github.event.review.user.login Username of the reviewer
    github.event.review.submitted_at Timestamp when review was submitted
    github.event.review.html_url URL to the review

    Label & Milestone Properties

    Property Description
    github.event.label.name Label name (on label events)
    github.event.label.color Label color hex code
    github.event.label.description Label description
    github.event.milestone.title Milestone title
    github.event.milestone.description Milestone description
    github.event.milestone.due_on Milestone due date
    github.event.milestone.state open or closed

    User & Actor Properties

    Docs: User objects in payloads

    Understanding the different user objects and when each is available.

    User Object Locations

    User information appears in multiple places within event payloads. Here's where to find it:

    User Property Description When Available
    github.actor User who triggered the workflow run Always available
    github.event.sender.login User who performed the triggering action All webhook events
    github.event.pull_request.user.login User who created the PR PR events
    github.event.issue.user.login User who created the issue Issue events
    github.event.comment.user.login User who wrote the comment Comment events
    github.event.review.user.login User who submitted the review PR review events
    github.event.release.author.login User who created the release Release events
    github.event.pusher.name User who pushed commits Push events
    github.event.repository.owner.login Owner of the repository All events with repository

    User Object Properties

    Each user object (like .user, .sender, .author) contains these properties:

    👤

    Identity

    .login — Username
    .id — Numeric ID
    .node_id — GraphQL ID

    🖼️

    Profile

    .avatar_url — Avatar image
    .html_url — Profile URL
    .type — User/Bot/Organization

    🔗

    API URLs

    .url — API endpoint
    .repos_url — User's repos
    .events_url — User's events

    ⭐

    Status

    .site_admin — Is GitHub staff
    .gravatar_id — Gravatar ID

    💡 Tip: actor vs sender

    github.actor and github.event.sender.login usually return the same value — the user who triggered the workflow.

    However, they can differ in some cases:
    • Re-runs: github.actor may be the user who triggered the re-run
    • Scheduled workflows: github.actor is the last user who modified the workflow file
    • workflow_dispatch: github.actor is the user who manually triggered the workflow

    Repository Properties

    Docs: Repository object reference

    Complete reference for repository information available in event payloads.

    Full Repository Object

    The github.event.repository object contains comprehensive repository information:

    Cat. Property Description
    ID .id Numeric repository ID
    .node_id GraphQL node ID
    .name Repository name
    .full_name Full name (owner/repo)
    .description Repository description
    Owner .owner.login Owner username
    .owner.id Owner numeric ID
    .owner.type User or Organization
    .owner.avatar_url Owner avatar URL
    URLs .html_url GitHub web URL
    .clone_url HTTPS clone URL
    .ssh_url SSH clone URL
    .git_url Git protocol URL
    Settings .private Is private repository
    .fork Is a fork
    .archived Is archived
    .disabled Is disabled
    .visibility public, private, internal
    Branches .default_branch Default branch name
    .master_branch Master branch (legacy)
    .allow_forking Forking allowed
    .is_template Is template repository
    Stats .stargazers_count Number of stars
    .watchers_count Number of watchers
    .forks_count Number of forks
    .open_issues_count Open issues count
    .size Repository size (KB)
    Code .language Primary language
    .topics Array of topics
    .license.key License identifier (mit, apache-2.0)

    Object Filters & Wildcards

    Docs: Object filters reference

    Using the * wildcard to extract values from arrays of objects.

    Object Filter Syntax

    The * syntax extracts a specific property from all objects in an array, returning a new array of those values:

    # Get all label names from an issue
${{ github.event.issue.labels.*.name }}
# Returns: ["bug", "priority-high", "needs-review"]

# Get all assignee usernames
${{ github.event.pull_request.assignees.*.login }}
# Returns: ["alice", "bob"]

# Get all commit messages from a push
${{ github.event.commits.*.message }}
# Returns: ["Fix bug", "Add feature", "Update docs"]

    Common Object Filter Patterns

    Pattern Returns
    github.event.issue.labels.*.name Array of all label names on an issue
    github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name Array of all label names on a PR
    github.event.issue.assignees.*.login Array of all assignee usernames
    github.event.pull_request.assignees.*.login Array of PR assignee usernames
    github.event.pull_request.requested_reviewers.*.login Array of requested reviewer usernames
    github.event.commits.*.id Array of commit SHAs from push
    github.event.commits.*.message Array of commit messages
    github.event.commits.*.author.name Array of commit author names
    github.event.release.assets.*.name Array of release asset filenames
    github.event.repository.topics.* Array of repository topics (strings)

    Using Object Filters with Functions

    Object filters are most powerful when combined with expression functions:

    Check if label exists

    # Run only if issue has 'bug' label
if: contains(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, 'bug')

# Run only if PR has 'ready-for-review' label
if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'ready-for-review')

    Join array values

    # Create comma-separated list of labels
- name: Show labels
  run: echo "Labels: ${{ join(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, ', ') }}"
  # Output: Labels: bug, priority-high, needs-review

# List all assignees
- name: Show assignees
  run: echo "Assigned to: ${{ join(github.event.pull_request.assignees.*.login, ', ') }}"

    Convert to JSON

    # Pass labels as JSON to a script
- name: Process labels
  env:
    LABELS: ${{ toJSON(github.event.issue.labels.*.name) }}
  run: |
    echo "Labels JSON: $LABELS"
    # Output: Labels JSON: ["bug","priority-high"]
    ⚠️ Important Notes:
    • Object filters return arrays, not single values
    • Empty arrays are returned if no objects match
    • Use contains() to check if a value exists in the result
    • Use join() to convert arrays to strings
    • Use toJSON() to serialize arrays for shell scripts

    Practical Examples

    Complete workflow examples demonstrating context property usage

    Real-world examples showing how to use .title, .body, .user.login and other properties in your JobPost workflows.

    Django JobPost Examples

    1. Display PR details from event payload

    on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  show-info:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Show PR details
        run: |
          echo "PR #${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
          echo "Title: ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.pull_request.user.login }}"
          echo "Source: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}"
          echo "Target: ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.ref }}"
          echo "Files changed: ${{ github.event.pull_request.changed_files }}"
    📋 Displays comprehensive PR information from event payload

    2. Respond to issue comments

    on:
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  process-comment:
    if: github.event.comment.user.login != 'github-actions[bot]'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check for deploy command
        if: contains(github.event.comment.body, '/deploy')
        run: |
          echo "Deploy requested by @${{ github.event.comment.user.login }}"
          echo "On issue #${{ github.event.issue.number }}: ${{ github.event.issue.title }}"
          
      - name: Check for help command
        if: contains(github.event.comment.body, '/help')
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              body: 'Available commands: /deploy, /test, /approve'
            })
    💬 Processes slash commands from issue comments

    3. Conditional deployment based on labels

    on:
  pull_request:
    types: [closed]

jobs:
  deploy:
    if: |
      github.event.pull_request.merged == true &&
      contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'deploy-production')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost to production
        run: |
          echo "Deploying PR #${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
          echo "Merged by: ${{ github.event.pull_request.merged_by.login }}"
          echo "Merge commit: ${{ github.event.pull_request.merge_commit_sha }}"
    🚀 Deploys only when merged PR has specific label

    4. Release notification workflow

    on:
  release:
    types: [published]

jobs:
  notify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Send release notification
        run: |
          echo "🎉 New release: ${{ github.event.release.name }}"
          echo "Tag: ${{ github.event.release.tag_name }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.release.author.login }}"
          echo "Prerelease: ${{ github.event.release.prerelease }}"
          echo ""
          echo "Release notes:"
          echo "${{ github.event.release.body }}"
          echo ""
          echo "Download: ${{ github.event.release.html_url }}"
    📢 Sends notifications with full release details

    5. Push event with commit information

    on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  log-push:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Log push details
        run: |
          echo "Pushed to: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
          echo "Pusher: ${{ github.event.pusher.name }} <${{ github.event.pusher.email }}>"
          echo "Before: ${{ github.event.before }}"
          echo "After: ${{ github.event.after }}"
          echo "Force push: ${{ github.event.forced }}"
          echo "Compare: ${{ github.event.compare }}"
          
      - name: Show head commit
        run: |
          echo "Latest commit: ${{ github.event.head_commit.id }}"
          echo "Message: ${{ github.event.head_commit.message }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.head_commit.author.name }}"
          echo "Timestamp: ${{ github.event.head_commit.timestamp }}"
    📝 Logs comprehensive push and commit information

    6. Matrix strategy with context

    on: push

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
        python: ['3.10', '3.11', '3.12']
    steps:
      - name: Show matrix context
        run: |
          echo "OS: ${{ matrix.os }}"
          echo "Python: ${{ matrix.python }}"
          echo "Runner: ${{ runner.os }} (${{ runner.arch }})"
          echo "Job: ${{ github.job }}"
          echo "Strategy fail-fast: ${{ strategy.fail-fast }}"
    🔀 Demonstrates matrix and runner context properties

    7. Using needs context for job dependencies

    jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      version: ${{ steps.version.outputs.value }}
    steps:
      - id: version
        run: echo "value=1.2.3" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        
  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Use output from build
        run: |
          echo "Deploying version: ${{ needs.build.outputs.version }}"
          echo "Build job result: ${{ needs.build.result }}"
    🔗 Passes data between jobs using needs context

    Quick Reference Cheat Sheet

    📋 Most Used Property Accessors

    👤 github.actor
    📝 .title / .body
    🔢 .number
    🏷️ .labels.*.name
    👥 .user.login
    🌿 .head.ref / .base.ref
    📊 .state / .merged
    🔗 .html_url
    📦 github.sha / github.ref
    🏢 .repository.name
    💬 .comment.body
    🚀 .release.tag_name

    💡 Pro Tips

    1. Always check if a property exists before using it — use github.event.pull_request.title || 'N/A' pattern.
    2. Use toJSON() to debug and see full payload structure: echo '${{ toJSON(github.event) }}'
    3. Object filters (.*.name) return arrays — combine with contains() or join().
    4. For complex nested access, use bracket notation: github.event['pull_request']['head']['ref']

    branch_protection_rule

    Docs: branch_protection_rule event | Webhook: branch_protection_rule payload

    Runs your workflow when branch protection rules in the workflow repository are changed.

    Full description

    The branch_protection_rule event triggers workflows when branch protection rules change in your repository. This is useful for automating security compliance checks, updating CI/CD configurations, or notifying teams about protection changes that affect your JobPost deployment workflow.

    For more information about branch protection rules, see About protected branches. For information about the branch protection rule APIs, see Objects in the GraphQL API documentation or REST API endpoints for branches.

    ⚠️ Important Notes:
    • More than one activity type triggers this event. By default, all activity types trigger workflows that run on this event.
    • You can limit your workflow runs to specific activity types using the types keyword.
    • This event will only trigger a workflow run if the workflow file exists on the default branch.

    Webhook event payload

    GITHUB_SHA: Last commit on default branch
    GITHUB_REF: Default branch

    Syntax

    on:
  branch_protection_rule:
    types: [created, edited, deleted]

    Activity types

    created: A new branch protection rule was created.
    edited: An existing branch protection rule was modified.
    deleted: A branch protection rule was removed.
    Payload: Contains rule details in github.event.rule.

    Workflow payload

    When this event triggers, you can access the webhook event payload in github.event. The payload includes information about the rule that was created, edited, or deleted, including pattern, required approvals, and enforcement settings.

    Official Example

    Basic branch protection rule trigger

    You can run a workflow when a branch protection rule has been created or deleted:

    on:
  branch_protection_rule:
    types: [created, deleted]
    📋 Workflow runs when protection rules are created or deleted

    Django JobPost Examples

    1. JobPost protection monitoring

    on:
  branch_protection_rule:
    types: [created, edited, deleted]

jobs:
  notify-team:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Notify JobPost team
        run: |
          echo "Branch protection changed: ${{ github.event.action }}"
          echo "Rule: ${{ github.event.rule.pattern }}"
    🛡️ Notifies JobPost team of protection rule changes

    2. Security compliance check

    on:
  branch_protection_rule:
    types: [edited, deleted]

jobs:
  compliance-check:
    if: github.event.rule.pattern == 'main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Validate JobPost main branch protection
        run: |
          if [ "${{ github.event.action }}" = "deleted" ]; then
            echo "❌ Main branch protection removed - JobPost security compromised"
            exit 1
          fi
    🔒 Validates JobPost main branch remains protected

    3. Update deployment workflow

    on:
  branch_protection_rule:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  update-deployment:
    if: contains(github.event.rule.pattern, 'release')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Update JobPost deployment rules
        run: |
          echo "New protected branch: ${{ github.event.rule.pattern }}"
          # Update deployment configuration
    📝 Updates JobPost deployment config for new protected branches

    4. Audit logging

    on:
  branch_protection_rule

jobs:
  audit-log:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Log JobPost protection changes
        run: |
          echo "$(date): ${{ github.actor }} ${{ github.event.action }} protection rule"
          echo "Pattern: ${{ github.event.rule.pattern }}"
          echo "Required reviews: ${{ github.event.rule.required_pull_request_reviews.required_approving_review_count }}"
    📋 Creates audit log of JobPost branch protection changes

    5. CI configuration sync

    on:
  branch_protection_rule:
    types: [created, edited]

jobs:
  sync-ci:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Sync JobPost CI with protection rules
        run: |
          PATTERN="${{ github.event.rule.pattern }}"
          if [[ "$PATTERN" == *"jobpost"* ]]; then
            echo "Updating CI for JobPost-related branch: $PATTERN"
            # Update CI configuration
          fi
    ⚙️ Syncs JobPost CI configuration with new protection rules

    check_run

    Docs: check_run event | Webhook: check_run payload

    Runs your workflow when activity related to a check run occurs.

    Full description

    The check_run event triggers workflows when check runs are created, completed, re-requested, or requested for action. A check run is an individual test that is part of a check suite. This is useful for creating workflows that respond to the results of other CI checks, implement complex approval processes, or perform follow-up actions based on JobPost test results.

    For more information, see Using the REST API to interact with checks. For information about the check run APIs, see Objects in the GraphQL API documentation or REST API endpoints for check runs.

    ⚠️ Important Notes:
    • More than one activity type triggers this event. By default, all activity types trigger workflows that run on this event.
    • You can limit your workflow runs to specific activity types using the types keyword.
    • This event will only trigger a workflow run if the workflow file exists on the default branch.
    • To prevent recursive workflows: This event does not trigger workflows if the check run's check suite was created by GitHub Actions or if the check suite's head SHA is associated with GitHub Actions.

    Webhook event payload

    GITHUB_SHA: Last commit on default branch
    GITHUB_REF: Default branch

    Syntax

    on:
  check_run:
    types: [completed]  # most common

    Activity types

    created: A new check run was created.
    rerequested: Someone requested to re-run the check.
    completed: A check run has completed (success, failure, neutral, cancelled).
    requested_action: An action button in the check run was clicked.
    Payload: Check run details available in github.event.check_run.

    Official Example

    Basic check_run trigger

    You can run a workflow when a check run has been rerequested or completed:

    on:
  check_run:
    types: [rerequested, completed]
    📋 Workflow runs when checks are rerequested or completed

    Django JobPost Examples

    1. JobPost test completion handler

    on:
  check_run:
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  handle-test-completion:
    if: github.event.check_run.name == 'JobPost Tests'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Handle JobPost test results
        run: |
          echo "Test status: ${{ github.event.check_run.conclusion }}"
          if [ "${{ github.event.check_run.conclusion }}" = "success" ]; then
            echo "✅ JobPost tests passed - triggering deployment"
          fi
    ✅ Handles JobPost test completion and triggers follow-up actions

    2. Failed check notification

    on:
  check_run:
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  notify-failure:
    if: github.event.check_run.conclusion == 'failure'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Notify JobPost team of failure
        run: |
          echo "❌ Check failed: ${{ github.event.check_run.name }}"
          echo "Details: ${{ github.event.check_run.details_url }}"
          echo "Output: ${{ github.event.check_run.output.summary }}"
    🚨 Notifies JobPost team when critical checks fail

    3. Re-run trigger automation

    on:
  check_run:
    types: [rerequested]

jobs:
  auto-rerun:
    if: contains(github.event.check_run.name, 'JobPost')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Auto re-run JobPost checks
        run: |
          echo "Re-running JobPost check: ${{ github.event.check_run.name }}"
          echo "Requested by: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          # Trigger re-run logic
    🔄 Automatically handles JobPost check re-run requests

    4. Custom action button handler

    on:
  check_run:
    types: [requested_action]

jobs:
  handle-action:
    if: github.event.requested_action.identifier == 'deploy-jobpost'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost on action
        run: |
          echo "Deploying JobPost via check run action"
          echo "Action: ${{ github.event.requested_action.identifier }}"
          # Deploy JobPost application
    🚀 Deploys JobPost when custom action button is clicked

    5. Check run aggregation

    on:
  check_run:
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  aggregate-results:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Aggregate JobPost check results
        run: |
          echo "Check: ${{ github.event.check_run.name }}"
          echo "Status: ${{ github.event.check_run.status }}"
          echo "Conclusion: ${{ github.event.check_run.conclusion }}"
          echo "Started: ${{ github.event.check_run.started_at }}"
          echo "Completed: ${{ github.event.check_run.completed_at }}"
    📊 Aggregates and reports JobPost check run results

    check_suite

    Docs: check_suite event | Webhook: check_suite payload

    Runs your workflow when check suite activity occurs.

    Full description

    The check_suite event triggers when check suites are created, completed, requested, or re-requested. A check suite is a collection of the check runs created for a specific commit. Check suites summarize the status and conclusion of the check runs that are in the suite. This enables coordination of multiple related checks, batch processing of JobPost validation workflows, and comprehensive quality gate management for your Django application.

    For more information, see Using the REST API to interact with checks. For information about the check suite APIs, see Objects in the GraphQL API documentation or REST API endpoints for check suites.

    ⚠️ Important Notes:
    • More than one activity type triggers this event. Although only the completed activity type is supported, specifying the activity type will keep your workflow specific if more activity types are added in the future.
    • By default, all activity types trigger workflows that run on this event.
    • You can limit your workflow runs to specific activity types using the types keyword.
    • This event will only trigger a workflow run if the workflow file exists on the default branch.
    • To prevent recursive workflows: This event does not trigger workflows if the check suite was created by GitHub Actions or if the check suite's head SHA is associated with GitHub Actions.

    Webhook event payload

    GITHUB_SHA: Last commit on default branch
    GITHUB_REF: Default branch

    Syntax

    on:
  check_suite:
    types: [completed]  # default

    Activity types

    completed: All checks in the suite have finished running.
    requested: A new check suite has been requested (triggered by new commit).
    rerequested: Someone requested to re-run all checks in the suite.
    Note: Although only the completed activity type is commonly used, specifying activity types keeps your workflow specific.

    Official Example

    Basic check_suite trigger

    You can run a workflow when a check suite has been completed:

    on:
  check_suite:
    types: [completed]
    📋 Workflow runs when check suite completes

    Django JobPost Examples

    1. JobPost quality gate orchestration

    on:
  check_suite:
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  quality-gate:
    if: github.event.check_suite.conclusion == 'success'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: JobPost quality gate passed
        run: |
          echo "✅ JobPost quality gate completed successfully"
          echo "Suite: ${{ github.event.check_suite.head_branch }}"
          echo "Commit: ${{ github.event.check_suite.head_sha }}"
          echo "App: ${{ github.event.check_suite.app.name }}"
          
          # All checks passed - proceed with deployment
          echo "All JobPost checks passed - approving for deployment"
          echo "Triggering production deployment pipeline"
    ✅ Orchestrates JobPost quality gates and approves deployment

    2. Failed check suite notification

    on:
  check_suite:
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  notify-failures:
    if: github.event.check_suite.conclusion == 'failure'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Notify JobPost check failures
        run: |
          echo "❌ JobPost check suite failed"
          echo "Failed checks in suite: ${{ github.event.check_suite.id }}"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.event.check_suite.head_branch }}"
          echo "Commit: ${{ github.event.check_suite.head_sha }}"
          
          # Get details of failed checks
          echo "Analyzing failed JobPost checks:"
          echo "- Check suite conclusion: ${{ github.event.check_suite.conclusion }}"
          echo "- Pull requests: ${{ github.event.check_suite.pull_requests }}"
          
          # Send notification to development team
          echo "Sending failure notification to JobPost development team"
    ❌ Notifies JobPost team when check suites fail

    3. Re-run automation

    on:
  check_suite:
    types: [rerequested]

jobs:
  handle-rerun:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Handle JobPost check re-run
        run: |
          echo "🔄 JobPost check suite re-run requested"
          echo "Suite ID: ${{ github.event.check_suite.id }}"
          echo "Requested by: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.event.check_suite.head_branch }}"
          
          # Log re-run for analysis
          echo "Logging JobPost check re-run for pattern analysis"
          echo "Previous conclusion: ${{ github.event.check_suite.conclusion }}"
          
          # Clear any cached results
          echo "Clearing JobPost check caches for fresh run"
    🔄 Handles JobPost check suite re-run requests

    4. Deployment readiness check

    on:
  check_suite:
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  deployment-readiness:
    if: github.event.check_suite.head_branch == 'main' && github.event.check_suite.conclusion == 'success'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check JobPost deployment readiness
        run: |
          echo "🚀 JobPost deployment readiness check"
          echo "All checks passed for main branch"
          echo "Suite status: ${{ github.event.check_suite.status }}"
          echo "Suite conclusion: ${{ github.event.check_suite.conclusion }}"
          
          # Verify deployment prerequisites
          echo "Verifying JobPost deployment prerequisites:"
          echo "- Code quality: ✅"
          echo "- Security scans: ✅"
          echo "- Tests: ✅"
          echo "- Performance: ✅"
          
          echo "JobPost is ready for production deployment"
    🚀 Verifies JobPost deployment readiness after all checks pass

    5. Check suite analytics

    on:
  check_suite:
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  suite-analytics:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Collect JobPost check suite metrics
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost check suite analytics"
          echo "Suite ID: ${{ github.event.check_suite.id }}"
          echo "Status: ${{ github.event.check_suite.status }}"
          echo "Conclusion: ${{ github.event.check_suite.conclusion }}"
          echo "Created: ${{ github.event.check_suite.created_at }}"
          echo "Updated: ${{ github.event.check_suite.updated_at }}"
          
          # Calculate duration and send metrics
          echo "Calculating JobPost check suite duration"
          echo "Sending metrics to monitoring dashboard"
          
          # Track success rates
          if [ "${{ github.event.check_suite.conclusion }}" = "success" ]; then
            echo "Recording successful JobPost check suite"
          fi
    📊 Collects JobPost check suite analytics and performance metrics

    create

    Docs: create event

    Triggers when a Git reference (branch or tag) is created in the repository.

    Full description

    The create event triggers when someone creates a branch or tag in your JobPost repository. This is useful for automating branch setup, initializing new feature environments, or responding to new release tags with deployment workflows.

    Syntax

    on:
  create

    Parameters description

    ref_type: Either 'branch' or 'tag' to identify what was created.
    ref: The name of the created reference.
    master_branch: The default branch of the repository.
    Auto-triggering: No additional configuration needed.
    Context access: Full access to repository and reference information.

    Return value

    Workflow runs with information about the newly created branch or tag.

    Examples

    1. Feature branch setup

    on:
  create

jobs:
  setup-feature-branch:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'branch' && startsWith(github.event.ref, 'feature/')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Setup JobPost feature branch
        run: |
          echo "🌿 Setting up JobPost feature branch"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Created by: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          echo "Default branch: ${{ github.event.master_branch }}"
          
          # Create development environment
          echo "Creating development environment for JobPost feature"
          echo "Branch name: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          
          # Setup branch protection if needed
          echo "Configuring branch settings for JobPost development"
    🌿 Automatically sets up JobPost feature branch environments

    2. Release tag automation

    on:
  create

jobs:
  handle-release-tag:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'tag' && startsWith(github.event.ref, 'v')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Handle JobPost release tag
        run: |
          echo "🏷️ JobPost release tag created"
          echo "Tag: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Creator: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          
          # Extract version number
          VERSION="${{ github.event.ref }}"
          VERSION=${VERSION#v}  # Remove 'v' prefix
          echo "JobPost version: $VERSION"
          
          # Trigger release processes
          echo "Starting JobPost release pipeline for $VERSION"
          echo "Building release artifacts"
          echo "Preparing deployment packages"
    🏷️ Handles JobPost release tag creation and triggers release pipeline

    3. Hotfix branch notification

    on:
  create

jobs:
  hotfix-notification:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'branch' && startsWith(github.event.ref, 'hotfix/')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: JobPost hotfix branch notification
        run: |
          echo "🚨 JobPost hotfix branch created"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Creator: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          
          # Extract issue number if present
          ISSUE_NUM=$(echo "${{ github.event.ref }}" | grep -oE '[0-9]+' | head -1)
          if [ ! -z "$ISSUE_NUM" ]; then
            echo "Related issue: #$ISSUE_NUM"
          fi
          
          # Notify team about urgent hotfix
          echo "Sending urgent notification to JobPost team"
          echo "Hotfix requires immediate attention"
    🚨 Notifies JobPost team when hotfix branches are created

    4. Beta tag deployment

    on:
  create

jobs:
  beta-deployment:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'tag' && contains(github.event.ref, 'beta')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost beta
        run: |
          echo "🧪 JobPost beta deployment"
          echo "Beta tag: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Target environment: staging"
          
          # Deploy to staging environment
          echo "Deploying JobPost beta to staging"
          echo "Running smoke tests"
          echo "Notifying QA team about new beta"
          
          # Create beta release notes
          echo "Generating beta release notes for JobPost"
    🧪 Automatically deploys JobPost beta versions to staging

    5. Branch analytics tracking

    on:
  create

jobs:
  track-branch-creation:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'branch'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Track JobPost branch creation
        run: |
          echo "📈 JobPost branch analytics"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Type: ${{ github.event.ref_type }}"
          echo "Creator: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          echo "Timestamp: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
          
          # Categorize branch type
          if [[ "${{ github.event.ref }}" == feature/* ]]; then
            BRANCH_TYPE="feature"
          elif [[ "${{ github.event.ref }}" == hotfix/* ]]; then
            BRANCH_TYPE="hotfix"
          elif [[ "${{ github.event.ref }}" == release/* ]]; then
            BRANCH_TYPE="release"
          else
            BRANCH_TYPE="other"
          fi
          
          echo "JobPost branch type: $BRANCH_TYPE"
          # Send to analytics
    📈 Tracks JobPost branch creation analytics and patterns

    delete

    Docs: delete event

    Triggers when a Git reference (branch or tag) is deleted from the repository.

    Full description

    The delete event triggers when someone deletes a branch or tag from your JobPost repository. This is useful for cleanup operations, removing associated resources, notifying teams about deleted features, or maintaining environment hygiene.

    Syntax

    on:
  delete

    Parameters description

    ref_type: Either 'branch' or 'tag' to identify what was deleted.
    ref: The name of the deleted reference.
    Cleanup opportunity: Perfect for resource cleanup automation.
    Safety checks: Implement safeguards against accidental deletions.
    Audit trail: Track deletions for compliance and debugging.

    Return value

    Workflow runs with information about the deleted branch or tag.

    Examples

    1. Feature branch cleanup

    on:
  delete

jobs:
  cleanup-feature-resources:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'branch' && startsWith(github.event.ref, 'feature/')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Cleanup JobPost feature resources
        run: |
          echo "🧹 Cleaning up JobPost feature branch resources"
          echo "Deleted branch: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Deleted by: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          
          # Clean up development environment
          BRANCH_NAME="${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Removing development environment for: $BRANCH_NAME"
          
          # Remove associated resources
          echo "Cleaning up JobPost preview deployments"
          echo "Removing database snapshots"
          echo "Cleaning up storage buckets"
    🧹 Automatically cleanups JobPost feature branch resources

    2. Critical branch protection

    on:
  delete

jobs:
  protect-critical-branches:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'branch' && (github.event.ref == 'main' || github.event.ref == 'production')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Alert on critical JobPost branch deletion
        run: |
          echo "🚨 CRITICAL: JobPost critical branch deleted!"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Deleted by: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          echo "Time: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
          
          # Send immediate alert
          echo "Sending URGENT alert to JobPost team"
          echo "Critical branch deletion requires immediate attention"
          
          # Log for audit
          echo "Logging critical deletion event for JobPost audit trail"
    🚨 Sends urgent alerts when critical JobPost branches are deleted

    3. Tag deletion audit

    on:
  delete

jobs:
  audit-tag-deletion:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'tag'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Audit JobPost tag deletion
        run: |
          echo "📋 JobPost tag deletion audit"
          echo "Deleted tag: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Deleted by: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          echo "Actor ID: ${{ github.event.sender.id }}"
          
          # Check if it was a release tag
          if [[ "${{ github.event.ref }}" == v* ]]; then
            echo "⚠️ Release tag deleted: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
            echo "Checking for associated releases"
          fi
          
          # Log to audit system
          curl -X POST "https://audit.jobpost.com/api/deletion" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{
              "type": "tag",
              "ref": "${{ github.event.ref }}",
              "actor": "${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
            }'
    📋 Audits JobPost tag deletions for compliance tracking

    4. Environment decommissioning

    on:
  delete

jobs:
  decommission-environment:
    if: github.event.ref_type == 'branch' && (startsWith(github.event.ref, 'release/') || startsWith(github.event.ref, 'staging/'))
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Decommission JobPost environment
        run: |
          echo "🏗️ Decommissioning JobPost environment"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          
          # Extract environment name
          ENV_NAME="${{ github.event.ref }}"
          ENV_NAME=${ENV_NAME//\//-}  # Replace / with -
          echo "Environment: $ENV_NAME"
          
          # Decommission resources
          echo "Stopping JobPost services for $ENV_NAME"
          echo "Backing up data before deletion"
          echo "Removing infrastructure resources"
          echo "Updating DNS records"
    🏗️ Decommissions JobPost environments when branches are deleted

    5. Deletion analytics and recovery

    on:
  delete

jobs:
  track-deletions:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Track JobPost deletions
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost deletion analytics"
          echo "Type: ${{ github.event.ref_type }}"
          echo "Reference: ${{ github.event.ref }}"
          echo "Actor: ${{ github.event.sender.login }}"
          
          # Store deletion metadata for potential recovery
          echo "Storing deletion metadata for potential recovery"
          
          # Check if deletion was accidental (quick create/delete)
          echo "Analyzing deletion patterns for JobPost"
          
          # Provide recovery instructions
          echo "Recovery instructions logged for JobPost team"
          echo "Use 'git push origin ${{ github.event.ref }}' to restore if needed"
    📊 Tracks JobPost deletions and provides recovery guidance

    deployment

    Docs: deployment event

    Triggers workflows when deployments are created via the GitHub API.

    Full description

    The deployment event triggers workflows when deployments are created through the GitHub Deployments API. This event is useful for handling deployment requests from external systems, implementing custom deployment orchestration, and coordinating complex deployment processes for your JobPost application.

    Syntax

    on:
  deployment

    Parameters description

    No activity types: The deployment event doesn't use activity types.
    API-triggered only: Only triggered via GitHub Deployments API, not by pushes or releases.
    Environment info: Contains deployment environment and configuration.
    Payload access: Deployment details available in github.event.deployment.

    Return value

    Workflow runs with access to deployment environment, payload, and configuration.

    Examples

    1. JobPost deployment orchestration

    on:
  deployment

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost application
        run: |
          echo "🚀 JobPost deployment requested"
          echo "Environment: ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
          echo "Ref: ${{ github.event.deployment.ref }}"
          echo "Task: ${{ github.event.deployment.task }}"
          echo "Payload: ${{ toJson(github.event.deployment.payload) }}"
          
          # Execute deployment based on environment
          if [ "${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}" = "production" ]; then
            echo "Deploying JobPost to production"
          fi
    🚀 Orchestrates JobPost deployments based on API requests

    2. Environment-specific deployment

    on:
  deployment

jobs:
  deploy-jobpost:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to specific environment
        run: |
          ENV="${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
          echo "🎯 Deploying JobPost to $ENV"
          
          case "$ENV" in
            "staging")
              echo "Setting up JobPost staging environment"
              ;;
            "production")
              echo "Deploying JobPost to production with blue-green strategy"
              ;;
            "development")
              echo "Deploying JobPost to development environment"
              ;;
          esac
    🎯 Deploys JobPost to specific environments with custom strategies

    3. Deployment status reporting

    on:
  deployment

jobs:
  deploy-with-status:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Report JobPost deployment status
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost deployment status reporting"
          echo "Deployment ID: ${{ github.event.deployment.id }}"
          echo "Creator: ${{ github.event.deployment.creator.login }}"
          echo "Created at: ${{ github.event.deployment.created_at }}"
          
          # Update deployment status via API
          echo "Updating deployment status to in_progress"
          # Deploy JobPost
          echo "JobPost deployment completed successfully"
          echo "Updating deployment status to success"
    📊 Reports JobPost deployment status throughout the process

    4. Multi-service deployment

    on:
  deployment

jobs:
  deploy-services:
    if: contains(github.event.deployment.payload.services, 'jobpost')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost services
        run: |
          echo "🔧 Multi-service JobPost deployment"
          echo "Services: ${{ join(github.event.deployment.payload.services, ', ') }}"
          
          # Deploy each service
          for service in ${{ join(github.event.deployment.payload.services, ' ') }}; do
            echo "Deploying $service service"
            if [ "$service" = "jobpost" ]; then
              echo "Deploying main JobPost application"
            fi
          done
    🔧 Deploys multiple JobPost services based on deployment payload

    5. Rollback deployment

    on:
  deployment

jobs:
  handle-rollback:
    if: github.event.deployment.task == 'rollback'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Rollback JobPost deployment
        run: |
          echo "🔄 JobPost rollback deployment"
          echo "Rolling back to: ${{ github.event.deployment.ref }}"
          echo "Environment: ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
          echo "Reason: ${{ github.event.deployment.description }}"
          
          # Execute rollback procedure
          echo "Rolling back JobPost to previous stable version"
    🔄 Handles JobPost rollback deployments when requested

    deployment_status

    Docs: deployment_status event

    Triggers when the status of a deployment changes, enabling post-deployment automation.

    Full description

    The deployment_status event triggers when deployment status changes via GitHub's Deployments API. This enables post-deployment workflows, monitoring setup, rollback automation, and integration with external deployment systems for your JobPost application.

    Syntax

    on:
  deployment_status

    Parameters description

    deployment: Information about the deployment that changed status.
    deployment_status: The new status (pending, success, failure, error).
    environment: The deployment environment (production, staging, etc.).
    target_url: URL where the deployment can be accessed.
    External integration: Works with deployment tools and services.

    Return value

    Workflow runs with detailed deployment status and environment information.

    Examples

    1. Post-deployment testing

    on:
  deployment_status

jobs:
  post-deployment-tests:
    if: github.event.deployment_status.state == 'success'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Run JobPost post-deployment tests
        run: |
          echo "🧪 JobPost post-deployment testing"
          echo "Environment: ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
          echo "URL: ${{ github.event.deployment_status.target_url }}"
          echo "State: ${{ github.event.deployment_status.state }}"
          
          # Wait for deployment to be ready
          sleep 30
          
          # Run smoke tests
          echo "Running JobPost smoke tests"
          curl -f "${{ github.event.deployment_status.target_url }}/health" || exit 1
          echo "Testing JobPost API endpoints"
          echo "Validating JobPost database connectivity"
    🧪 Runs JobPost smoke tests after successful deployment

    2. Deployment failure rollback

    on:
  deployment_status

jobs:
  handle-failure:
    if: github.event.deployment_status.state == 'failure'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Handle JobPost deployment failure
        run: |
          echo "❌ JobPost deployment failed"
          echo "Environment: ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
          echo "Failed deployment ID: ${{ github.event.deployment.id }}"
          echo "Error: ${{ github.event.deployment_status.description }}"
          
          # Trigger automatic rollback for production
          if [ "${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}" = "production" ]; then
            echo "🔄 Triggering JobPost production rollback"
            echo "Rolling back to previous stable version"
          fi
          
          # Notify team
          echo "Sending JobPost deployment failure alert"
    ❌ Handles JobPost deployment failures and triggers rollback

    3. Environment monitoring setup

    on:
  deployment_status

jobs:
  setup-monitoring:
    if: github.event.deployment_status.state == 'success' && github.event.deployment.environment == 'production'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Setup JobPost production monitoring
        run: |
          echo "📊 Setting up JobPost production monitoring"
          echo "Target URL: ${{ github.event.deployment_status.target_url }}"
          echo "Deployment ref: ${{ github.event.deployment.ref }}"
          
          # Configure monitoring
          echo "Configuring JobPost health checks"
          echo "Setting up JobPost performance monitoring"
          echo "Enabling JobPost error tracking"
          echo "Configuring JobPost alert thresholds"
          
          # Register with monitoring service
          curl -X POST "https://monitoring.jobpost.com/api/register" \
            -d "url=${{ github.event.deployment_status.target_url }}"
    📊 Sets up JobPost production monitoring after successful deployment

    4. Deployment notification system

    on:
  deployment_status

jobs:
  notify-deployment:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Notify JobPost deployment status
        run: |
          echo "📢 JobPost deployment notification"
          echo "Environment: ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
          echo "Status: ${{ github.event.deployment_status.state }}"
          echo "Deployer: ${{ github.event.deployment.creator.login }}"
          echo "Ref: ${{ github.event.deployment.ref }}"
          
          # Send appropriate notification based on status
          case "${{ github.event.deployment_status.state }}" in
            "success")
              echo "✅ JobPost deployed successfully to ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
              ;;
            "failure")
              echo "❌ JobPost deployment failed in ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
              ;;
            "pending")
              echo "⏳ JobPost deployment in progress for ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
              ;;
          esac
    📢 Sends JobPost deployment status notifications to team

    5. Performance baseline setup

    on:
  deployment_status

jobs:
  performance-baseline:
    if: github.event.deployment_status.state == 'success'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Establish JobPost performance baseline
        run: |
          echo "⚡ JobPost performance baseline establishment"
          echo "Environment: ${{ github.event.deployment.environment }}"
          echo "Target: ${{ github.event.deployment_status.target_url }}"
          
          # Wait for application to stabilize
          sleep 60
          
          # Run performance benchmarks
          echo "Running JobPost performance benchmarks"
          echo "Testing JobPost API response times"
          echo "Measuring JobPost database query performance"
          echo "Checking JobPost memory usage"
          
          # Store baseline metrics
          echo "Storing JobPost performance baseline for future comparisons"
    ⚡ Establishes JobPost performance baseline after deployment

    discussion

    Docs: discussion event

    Triggers when team discussions are created, edited, or answered in GitHub Discussions.

    Full description

    The discussion event triggers when GitHub Discussions activities occur in your JobPost repository. This enables community management automation, content moderation, expert assignment, and integration with external knowledge systems for your Django job board project.

    Syntax

    on:
  discussion:
    types: [created, answered]  # common types

    Activity types

    created: A new discussion was started.
    edited: Discussion title or body was modified.
    deleted: A discussion was deleted.
    transferred: A discussion was transferred to another repository.
    pinned: A discussion was pinned to the discussions list.
    unpinned: A discussion was unpinned from the discussions list.
    labeled: A label was added to the discussion.
    unlabeled: A label was removed from the discussion.
    locked: Discussion conversation was locked.
    unlocked: Discussion conversation was unlocked.
    category_changed: Discussion was moved to a different category.
    answered: Discussion was marked as answered (Q&A category).
    unanswered: Discussion answer was unmarked.

    Return value

    Workflow runs with access to discussion content, category, and participant information.

    Examples

    1. Auto-categorize JobPost discussions

    on:
  discussion:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  auto-categorize:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Auto-categorize JobPost discussion
        run: |
          echo "🏷️ Auto-categorizing JobPost discussion"
          echo "Title: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.discussion.user.login }}"
          echo "Category: ${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}"
          
          # Analyze discussion content for auto-categorization
          TITLE="${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          if [[ "$TITLE" == *"bug"* ]] || [[ "$TITLE" == *"error"* ]]; then
            echo "Suggesting 'Bug Reports' category for JobPost discussion"
          elif [[ "$TITLE" == *"feature"* ]] || [[ "$TITLE" == *"enhancement"* ]]; then
            echo "Suggesting 'Feature Requests' category for JobPost discussion"
          fi
    🏷️ Auto-categorizes JobPost discussions based on content analysis

    2. Expert assignment system

    on:
  discussion:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  assign-expert:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Assign JobPost expert
        run: |
          echo "👨‍💼 JobPost expert assignment"
          echo "Discussion: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          echo "Category: ${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}"
          
          # Assign experts based on discussion category
          case "${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}" in
            "Django Development")
              echo "Assigning Django expert to JobPost discussion"
              EXPERT="django-team"
              ;;
            "Database")
              echo "Assigning database expert to JobPost discussion"
              EXPERT="db-team"
              ;;
            "Deployment")
              echo "Assigning DevOps expert to JobPost discussion"
              EXPERT="devops-team"
              ;;
          esac
          
          echo "Expert assigned: $EXPERT"
    👨‍💼 Assigns JobPost experts based on discussion categories

    3. Knowledge base integration

    on:
  discussion:
    types: [answered]

jobs:
  update-knowledge-base:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Update JobPost knowledge base
        run: |
          echo "📚 Updating JobPost knowledge base"
          echo "Answered discussion: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          echo "Answer by: ${{ github.event.discussion.answer_chosen_by.login }}"
          
          # Extract Q&A for knowledge base
          echo "Extracting JobPost Q&A for knowledge base"
          echo "Question: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          echo "Category: ${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}"
          
          # Add to searchable knowledge base
          curl -X POST "https://kb.jobpost.com/api/add" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{
              "question": "${{ github.event.discussion.title }}",
              "category": "${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}",
              "url": "${{ github.event.discussion.html_url }}"
            }'
    📚 Updates JobPost knowledge base with answered discussions

    4. Community engagement rewards

    on:
  discussion:
    types: [answered]

jobs:
  reward-contributor:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Reward JobPost community contributor
        run: |
          echo "🏆 JobPost community rewards"
          echo "Helpful contributor: ${{ github.event.discussion.answer_chosen_by.login }}"
          echo "Discussion: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          
          # Track community contributions
          echo "Recording contribution to JobPost community leaderboard"
          echo "Contributor: ${{ github.event.discussion.answer_chosen_by.login }}"
          echo "Type: Helpful Answer"
          echo "Topic: ${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}"
          
          # Award points or badges
          echo "Awarding JobPost community points"
          echo "Checking for milestone achievements"
    🏆 Rewards JobPost community contributors for helpful answers

    5. Discussion analytics

    on:
  discussion:
    types: [created, answered]

jobs:
  track-analytics:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Track JobPost discussion analytics
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost discussion analytics"
          echo "Event type: ${{ github.event.action }}"
          echo "Category: ${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.discussion.user.login }}"
          
          # Track discussion metrics
          echo "Tracking JobPost community engagement"
          echo "Category popularity: ${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}"
          echo "User activity: ${{ github.event.discussion.user.login }}"
          
          # Send to analytics
          curl -X POST "https://analytics.jobpost.com/api/discussion" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d '{
              "action": "${{ github.event.action }}",
              "category": "${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}",
              "timestamp": "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
            }'
    📊 Tracks JobPost discussion analytics and community engagement

    discussion_comment

    Docs: discussion_comment event

    Triggers when comments are added, edited, or deleted in GitHub Discussions.

    Full description

    The discussion_comment event triggers when someone interacts with comments in GitHub Discussions. This enables real-time moderation, automated responses, sentiment analysis, and community engagement tracking for your JobPost project discussions.

    Syntax

    on:
  discussion_comment:
    types: [created, edited, deleted]

    Activity types

    created: A new comment was added to a discussion.
    edited: An existing comment was modified.
    deleted: A comment was removed from the discussion.

    Return value

    Workflow runs with access to comment content, author, and discussion context.

    Examples

    1. Auto-moderation system

    on:
  discussion_comment:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  auto-moderate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Moderate JobPost discussion comment
        run: |
          echo "🛡️ JobPost comment moderation"
          echo "Comment by: ${{ github.event.comment.user.login }}"
          echo "Discussion: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          echo "Comment ID: ${{ github.event.comment.id }}"
          
          # Check for inappropriate content
          COMMENT_BODY="${{ github.event.comment.body }}"
          echo "Analyzing JobPost comment for policy violations"
          
          # Flag potential issues
          if [[ "$COMMENT_BODY" == *"spam"* ]] || [[ "$COMMENT_BODY" == *"promotional"* ]]; then
            echo "⚠️ Potential spam detected in JobPost discussion"
            echo "Flagging for human review"
          fi
    🛡️ Automatically moderates JobPost discussion comments

    2. Expert notification system

    on:
  discussion_comment:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  notify-experts:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Notify JobPost experts
        run: |
          echo "🔔 JobPost expert notification"
          echo "New comment in: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          echo "Category: ${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}"
          echo "Comment by: ${{ github.event.comment.user.login }}"
          
          # Notify relevant experts based on category
          case "${{ github.event.discussion.category.name }}" in
            "Django Development")
              echo "Notifying Django experts about JobPost discussion update"
              ;;
            "API Integration")
              echo "Notifying API experts about JobPost discussion update"
              ;;
            "Performance")
              echo "Notifying performance experts about JobPost discussion update"
              ;;
          esac
    🔔 Notifies JobPost experts when relevant discussions are updated

    3. Sentiment analysis tracking

    on:
  discussion_comment:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  analyze-sentiment:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Analyze JobPost comment sentiment
        run: |
          echo "😊 JobPost sentiment analysis"
          echo "Analyzing comment: ${{ github.event.comment.id }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.comment.user.login }}"
          echo "Discussion: ${{ github.event.discussion.title }}"
          
          # Basic sentiment detection
          COMMENT="${{ github.event.comment.body }}"
          if [[ "$COMMENT" == *"thank"* ]] || [[ "$COMMENT" == *"great"* ]] || [[ "$COMMENT" == *"awesome"* ]]; then
            SENTIMENT="positive"
