  run: |
    # Get OAuth token
    RESPONSE=$(curl -s -X POST https://oauth.jobpost.com/token \
      -d "client_id=${{ secrets.CLIENT_ID }}" \
      -d "client_secret=${{ secrets.CLIENT_SECRET }}" \
      -d "grant_type=client_credentials")
    
    ACCESS_TOKEN=$(echo $RESPONSE | jq -r '.access_token')
    REFRESH_TOKEN=$(echo $RESPONSE | jq -r '.refresh_token')
    
    # Mask both tokens
    echo "::add-mask::$ACCESS_TOKEN"
    echo "::add-mask::$REFRESH_TOKEN"
    
    # Safely use tokens
    echo "JobPost OAuth complete"
    echo "Access token: $ACCESS_TOKEN"
    echo "Refresh token: $REFRESH_TOKEN"
    🔒 Mask JobPost OAuth tokens in logs

    5. JobPost multi-environment secrets masking

    - name: JobPost multi-env secrets
  run: |
    # Production credentials
    PROD_KEY="${{ secrets.PROD_API_KEY }}"
    PROD_DB="${{ secrets.PROD_DB_PASSWORD }}"
    echo "::add-mask::$PROD_KEY"
    echo "::add-mask::$PROD_DB"
    
    # Staging credentials
    STAGING_KEY="${{ secrets.STAGING_API_KEY }}"
    STAGING_DB="${{ secrets.STAGING_DB_PASSWORD }}"
    echo "::add-mask::$STAGING_KEY"
    echo "::add-mask::$STAGING_DB"
    
    # Generate session token
    SESSION_TOKEN=$(python -c "import uuid; print(str(uuid.uuid4()))")
    echo "::add-mask::$SESSION_TOKEN"
    
    echo "JobPost credentials configured for all environments"
    echo "Prod API: $PROD_KEY, DB: $PROD_DB"
    echo "Staging API: $STAGING_KEY, DB: $STAGING_DB"
    echo "Session: $SESSION_TOKEN"
    🔒 Mask JobPost credentials across multiple environments

    Stopping and starting workflow commands

    Docs: Stop/start commands

    Temporarily stop processing workflow commands to safely log untrusted content.

    Full description

    The stop-commands feature allows you to temporarily disable workflow command processing. This is critical when logging untrusted content that might contain strings resembling workflow commands (like ::error:: or ::set-env::). Use a unique token to stop command processing, log the untrusted content, then resume processing with the same token. This prevents malicious or accidental command injection in your JobPost workflows.

    Syntax

    ::stop-commands::{token}
# Commands here are not processed
::{token}::

    Parameters description

    token: A unique string to identify the stop/start pair (required)
    Best practice: Use UUID or timestamp-based tokens for uniqueness
    Scope: All workflow commands between stop and start are treated as plain text
    Use case: Logging user input, error messages, or external content

    Return value

    No visible output. Workflow commands are disabled until the matching token is encountered.

    Examples

    1. Log JobPost user input safely

    - name: Process JobPost user data
  run: |
    TOKEN=$(uuidgen)
    echo "::stop-commands::$TOKEN"
    
    # Log potentially dangerous user input
    cat user_input.txt
    echo "JobPost user data logged"
    
    echo "::$TOKEN::"
    echo "Resumed processing JobPost workflow commands"
    🛡️ Safely log untrusted JobPost user input

    2. Display JobPost error messages safely

    - name: Show JobPost errors
  run: |
    STOP_TOKEN="stop_$(date +%s)"
    echo "::stop-commands::$STOP_TOKEN"
    
    # Display error log that might contain workflow commands
    cat jobpost_errors.log
    
    echo "::$STOP_TOKEN::"
    echo "::notice::JobPost error log displayed"
    🛡️ Display JobPost errors without processing commands

    3. JobPost API response logging

    - name: Log JobPost API response
  run: |
    # Get API response that might contain special characters
    RESPONSE=$(curl -s https://api.jobpost.com/data)
    
    STOP_TOKEN="api_log_$(openssl rand -hex 8)"
    echo "::stop-commands::$STOP_TOKEN"
    
    echo "JobPost API Response:"
    echo "$RESPONSE"
    
    echo "::$STOP_TOKEN::"
    echo "::notice::JobPost API response logged safely"
    🛡️ Safely log JobPost API responses

    4. JobPost test output with special characters

    - name: Display JobPost test output
  run: |
    python manage.py test jobpost --verbosity=2 > test_output.txt
    
    STOP_TOKEN="test_$(date +%s%N)"
    echo "::stop-commands::$STOP_TOKEN"
    
    echo "=== JobPost Test Output ==="
    cat test_output.txt
    echo "=== End Output ==="
    
    echo "::$STOP_TOKEN::"
    
    if grep -q "FAILED" test_output.txt; then
      echo "::error::JobPost tests failed"
    fi
    🛡️ Display verbose JobPost test output safely

    5. JobPost external content processing

    - name: Process JobPost external data
  run: |
    # Download and display external content
    curl -s https://external-source.com/jobpost-config.txt > config.txt
    
    UNIQUE_TOKEN="external_$(python -c 'import uuid; print(uuid.uuid4())')"
    echo "::stop-commands::$UNIQUE_TOKEN"
    
    echo "JobPost External Configuration:"
    cat config.txt
    echo ""
    echo "Configuration loaded from external source"
    
    echo "::$UNIQUE_TOKEN::"
    echo "::notice title=JobPost Config::External configuration processed"
    🛡️ Safely process JobPost external content

    Sending values to the pre and post actions

    Docs: Save state

    Save state that can be shared with a job's pre: or post: actions for setup and cleanup.

    Full description

    The save-state command allows you to save values that can be accessed by pre and post actions within the same job. This is particularly useful for custom actions that need to perform cleanup operations after the main action completes. State values are persisted and available to post-action hooks, enabling sophisticated setup/teardown patterns for your JobPost workflows. State is scoped to the current job and is not available across different jobs.

    Syntax

    ::save-state name={name}::{value}

    Parameters description

    name: The state variable name (required)
    value: The value to save (required)
    Scope: Available only within the same job to post actions
    Use case: Resource IDs for cleanup, deployment info, temporary file paths

    Return value

    State is saved and accessible via STATE_{name} environment variable in post actions.

    Examples

    1. Save JobPost deployment state

    - name: JobPost pre-deployment
  run: |
    DEPLOY_ID=$(date +%s)
    echo "::save-state name=deploy_id::$DEPLOY_ID"
    echo "::save-state name=deploy_branch::${{ github.ref_name }}"
    echo "JobPost deployment ID: $DEPLOY_ID"
    💾 Save JobPost deployment state for post-action

    2. Save JobPost database backup info

    - name: Backup JobPost database
  run: |
    BACKUP_FILE="jobpost_backup_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).sql"
    pg_dump jobpost > $BACKUP_FILE
    
    echo "::save-state name=backup_file::$BACKUP_FILE"
    echo "::save-state name=backup_size::$(stat -f%z $BACKUP_FILE)"
    echo "JobPost database backed up: $BACKUP_FILE"
    💾 Save JobPost backup metadata for cleanup

    3. JobPost test environment state

    - name: Setup JobPost test environment
  run: |
    # Start test database
    docker run -d --name jobpost-test-db -p 5432:5432 postgres:14
    
    # Save container ID for cleanup
    CONTAINER_ID=$(docker ps -q -f name=jobpost-test-db)
    echo "::save-state name=test_db_container::$CONTAINER_ID"
    echo "::save-state name=test_db_port::5432"
    
    echo "JobPost test database started: $CONTAINER_ID"

# Post-action would clean up using STATE_test_db_container
    💾 Save JobPost test infrastructure state

    4. JobPost temporary resource tracking

    - name: Create JobPost temporary resources
  run: |
    # Create temporary S3 bucket for testing
    BUCKET_NAME="jobpost-test-$(uuidgen)"
    aws s3 mb s3://$BUCKET_NAME
    
    # Create temporary RDS instance
    DB_INSTANCE="jobpost-test-$(date +%s)"
    aws rds create-db-instance --db-instance-identifier $DB_INSTANCE
    
    # Save for post-action cleanup
    echo "::save-state name=temp_bucket::$BUCKET_NAME"
    echo "::save-state name=temp_db::$DB_INSTANCE"
    
    echo "JobPost temporary resources created"
    💾 Track JobPost temporary cloud resources

    5. JobPost deployment rollback information

    - name: Deploy JobPost with rollback state
  run: |
    # Save current version before deploying
    CURRENT_VERSION=$(kubectl get deployment jobpost -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}')
    echo "::save-state name=previous_version::$CURRENT_VERSION"
    
    # Save deployment timestamp
    echo "::save-state name=deploy_timestamp::$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"
    
    # Save namespace and deployment name
    echo "::save-state name=k8s_namespace::production"
    echo "::save-state name=deployment_name::jobpost"
    
    echo "JobPost rollback information saved"
    echo "Previous version: $CURRENT_VERSION"

# Post-action can rollback using saved state if needed
    💾 Save JobPost rollback information

    Environment files

    Docs: Environment files

    Use environment files to set environment variables and outputs that persist across workflow steps.

    Available environment files

    GITHUB_ENV: Set environment variables
    GITHUB_PATH: Add directories to system PATH
    GITHUB_OUTPUT: Set step outputs
    GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY: Add job summary content

    Examples

    JobPost environment file usage

    - name: Configure JobPost environment
  run: |
    echo "JOBPOST_VERSION=2.0.0" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "/opt/jobpost/bin" >> $GITHUB_PATH
    echo "deploy_status=success" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    📝 Set JobPost environment variables using files

    Setting an environment variable

    Docs: Set environment variables

    Set environment variables that are available to subsequent steps in the same job.

    Syntax

    echo "{variable_name}={value}" >> $GITHUB_ENV

    Examples

    1. Set JobPost configuration variables

    - name: Setup JobPost environment
  run: |
    echo "JOBPOST_DEBUG=False" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_DATABASE=postgresql" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND=redis" >> $GITHUB_ENV

- name: Use JobPost variables
  run: |
    echo "JobPost Debug: $JOBPOST_DEBUG"
    echo "JobPost Database: $JOBPOST_DATABASE"
    python manage.py runserver
    ⚙️ Configure JobPost with environment variables

    2. Set JobPost version from Git

    - name: Get JobPost version
  run: |
    VERSION=$(git describe --tags --always)
    echo "JOBPOST_VERSION=$VERSION" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_COMMIT=${{ github.sha }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV

- name: Build JobPost with version
  run: |
    echo "Building JobPost version $JOBPOST_VERSION"
    echo "Commit: $JOBPOST_COMMIT"
    🏷️ Set JobPost version from Git tags

    3. Multi-line JobPost environment variable

    - name: Set JobPost configuration
  run: |
    echo "JOBPOST_ALLOWED_HOSTS<> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "localhost" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "jobpost.example.com" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "api.jobpost.com" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "EOF" >> $GITHUB_ENV

- name: Display JobPost hosts
  run: |
    echo "JobPost allowed hosts:"
    echo "$JOBPOST_ALLOWED_HOSTS"
    📝 Set multi-line JobPost environment variable

    Setting an output parameter

    Docs: Set outputs

    Set output parameters that can be used by downstream actions and jobs.

    Syntax

    echo "{output_name}={value}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

    Examples

    1. JobPost test results output

    - name: Run JobPost tests
  id: test
  run: |
    python manage.py test --verbosity=0 > test_output.txt
    TEST_COUNT=$(grep -c "OK" test_output.txt || echo "0")
    echo "test_count=$TEST_COUNT" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    echo "test_status=passed" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

- name: Use JobPost test results
  run: |
    echo "JobPost tests passed: ${{ steps.test.outputs.test_count }}"
    echo "Status: ${{ steps.test.outputs.test_status }}"
    📊 Output JobPost test results to other steps

    2. JobPost deployment URL output

    - name: Deploy JobPost
  id: deploy
  run: |
    DEPLOY_URL="https://jobpost-${{ github.run_number }}.example.com"
    echo "url=$DEPLOY_URL" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    echo "timestamp=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    echo "JobPost deployed to $DEPLOY_URL"

- name: Test JobPost deployment
  run: |
    echo "Testing JobPost at ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.url }}"
    curl -f ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.url }}/health/
    🚀 Share JobPost deployment URL between steps

    3. Multi-line JobPost output

    - name: Generate JobPost report
  id: report
  run: |
    {
      echo "report<> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

- name: Display JobPost report
  run: |
    echo "${{ steps.report.outputs.report }}"
    📄 Generate multi-line JobPost report output

    Adding a job summary

    Docs: Job summaries

    Add custom Markdown content to the workflow run summary page. Supports tables, images, and rich formatting.

    Syntax

    echo "{markdown_content}" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

    Examples

    1. JobPost deployment summary

    - name: Create JobPost deployment summary
  run: |
    echo "## 🚀 JobPost Deployment Summary" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "| Metric | Value |" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "|--------|-------|" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "| Environment | Production |" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "| Version | 2.0.0 |" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "| Deploy Time | $(date) |" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "| Status | ✅ Success |" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    📋 Rich JobPost deployment summary with table

    2. JobPost test coverage summary

    - name: Generate JobPost coverage summary
  run: |
    coverage run --source='jobpost' manage.py test
    coverage report > coverage.txt
    
    echo "## 📊 JobPost Test Coverage" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    cat coverage.txt >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    📊 JobPost test coverage in workflow summary

    3. Comprehensive JobPost CI summary

    - name: Create JobPost CI summary
  run: |
    {
      echo "# JobPost CI Results 🎯"
      echo ""
      echo "## Test Results"
      echo "- ✅ Unit Tests: Passed (42 tests)"
      echo "- ✅ Integration Tests: Passed (18 tests)"
      echo "- ✅ API Tests: Passed (25 tests)"
      echo ""
      echo "## Code Quality"
      echo "- Coverage: 87%"
      echo "- Linting: No issues"
      echo ""
      echo "## Performance"
      echo "- Build Time: 2m 34s"
      echo "- Test Time: 1m 12s"
      echo ""
      echo "---"
      echo "_JobPost Build #${{ github.run_number }}_"
    } >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    📋 Comprehensive JobPost CI summary

    Adding a system path

    Docs: Add to PATH

    Prepend a directory to the system PATH variable for all subsequent actions in the current job.

    Syntax

    echo "{path}" >> $GITHUB_PATH

    Examples

    1. Add JobPost custom scripts to PATH

    - name: Setup JobPost scripts
  run: |
    echo "${{ github.workspace }}/jobpost/scripts" >> $GITHUB_PATH
    echo "${{ github.workspace }}/bin" >> $GITHUB_PATH

- name: Run JobPost script
  run: |
    # Now can run scripts directly
    jobpost-deploy.sh
    jobpost-test.sh
    📂 Add JobPost scripts directory to PATH

    2. Add Python virtual environment to PATH

    - name: Setup JobPost Python environment
  run: |
    python -m venv jobpost_env
    echo "${{ github.workspace }}/jobpost_env/bin" >> $GITHUB_PATH

- name: Use JobPost environment
  run: |
    # Virtual environment is now active
    which python
    pip install -r requirements.txt
    python manage.py test
    🐍 Add JobPost virtual environment to PATH

    3. Add custom JobPost tools

    - name: Install JobPost tools
  run: |
    mkdir -p $HOME/jobpost-tools
    curl -o $HOME/jobpost-tools/migrate-tool https://tools.jobpost.com/migrate
    chmod +x $HOME/jobpost-tools/migrate-tool
    echo "$HOME/jobpost-tools" >> $GITHUB_PATH

- name: Use JobPost tools
  run: |
    migrate-tool --check
    migrate-tool --apply
    🔧 Add custom JobPost tools to PATH

    Default environment variables

    Docs: Default environment variables

    The default environment variables that GitHub sets are available to every step in a workflow.

    📝 Official GitHub Documentation:
    Because default environment variables are set by GitHub and not defined in a workflow, they are not accessible through the env context. However, most of the default variables have a corresponding, and similarly named, context property. For example, the value of the GITHUB_REF variable can be read during workflow processing using the ${{ github.ref }} context property.
    ⚠️ Important Restrictions:
    You can't overwrite the value of the default environment variables named GITHUB_* and RUNNER_*. Currently you can overwrite the value of the CI variable. However, it's not guaranteed that this will always be possible. For more information about setting environment variables, see Store information in variables and Workflow commands for GitHub Actions.

    Full description

    GitHub Actions automatically sets default environment variables that are available in every workflow run. These variables provide essential metadata about the workflow execution, repository context, runner environment, and triggering events. They are read-only and automatically populated by the Actions runner. You can use these variables to make your JobPost workflows dynamic and context-aware without manual configuration. Default variables are available in all steps and can be referenced using standard environment variable syntax.

    💡 Best Practice:
    We strongly recommend that actions use variables to access the filesystem rather than using hardcoded file paths. GitHub sets variables for actions to use in all runner environments.

    Syntax

    # Access default variables in shell
echo "$GITHUB_REPOSITORY"
echo "$GITHUB_SHA"
echo "$RUNNER_OS"

# Access via context property (for workflow processing)
${{ github.ref }}
${{ github.sha }}
${{ runner.os }}

    Complete Default Environment Variables Reference

    📚 Official Default Environment Variables Table

    The following table lists all default environment variables provided by GitHub Actions:
    Variable Description
    CI Always set to true.
    GITHUB_ACTION The name of the action currently running, or the id of a step. For example, for an action, __repo-owner_name-of-action-repo. GitHub removes special characters, and uses the name __run when the current step runs a script without an id. If you use the same script or action more than once in the same job, the name will include a suffix that consists of the sequence number preceded by an underscore. For example, the first script you run will have the name __run, and the second script will be named __run_2. Similarly, the second invocation of actions/checkout will be actionscheckout2.
    GITHUB_ACTION_PATH The path where an action is located. This property is only supported in composite actions. You can use this path to change directories to where the action is located and access other files in that same repository. For example, /home/runner/work/_actions/repo-owner/name-of-action-repo/v1.
    GITHUB_ACTION_REPOSITORY For a step executing an action, this is the owner and repository name of the action. For example, actions/checkout.
    GITHUB_ACTIONS Always set to true when GitHub Actions is running the workflow. You can use this variable to differentiate when tests are being run locally or by GitHub Actions.
    GITHUB_ACTOR The name of the person or app that initiated the workflow. For example, octocat.
    GITHUB_ACTOR_ID The account ID of the person or app that triggered the initial workflow run. For example, 1234567. Note that this is different from the actor username.
    GITHUB_API_URL Returns the API URL. For example: https://api.github.com.
    GITHUB_BASE_REF The name of the base ref or target branch of the pull request in a workflow run. This is only set when the event that triggers a workflow run is either pull_request or pull_request_target. For example, main.
    GITHUB_ENV The path on the runner to the file that sets variables from workflow commands. The path to this file is unique to the current step and changes for each step in a job. For example, /home/runner/work/_temp/_runner_file_commands/set_env_87406d6e-4979-4d42-98e1-3dab1f48b13a. For more information, see Workflow commands for GitHub Actions.
    GITHUB_EVENT_NAME The name of the event that triggered the workflow. For example, workflow_dispatch.
    GITHUB_EVENT_PATH The path to the file on the runner that contains the full event webhook payload. For example, /github/workflow/event.json.
    GITHUB_GRAPHQL_URL Returns the GraphQL API URL. For example: https://api.github.com/graphql.
    GITHUB_HEAD_REF The head ref or source branch of the pull request in a workflow run. This property is only set when the event that triggers a workflow run is either pull_request or pull_request_target. For example, feature-branch-1.
    GITHUB_JOB The job_id of the current job. For example, greeting_job.
    GITHUB_OUTPUT The path on the runner to the file that sets the current step's outputs from workflow commands. The path to this file is unique to the current step and changes for each step in a job. For example, /home/runner/work/_temp/_runner_file_commands/set_output_a50ef383-b063-46d9-9157-57953fc9f3f0. For more information, see Workflow commands for GitHub Actions.
    GITHUB_PATH The path on the runner to the file that sets system PATH variables from workflow commands. The path to this file is unique to the current step and changes for each step in a job. For example, /home/runner/work/_temp/_runner_file_commands/add_path_899b9445-ad4a-400c-aa89-249f18632cf5. For more information, see Workflow commands for GitHub Actions.
    GITHUB_REF The fully-formed ref of the branch or tag that triggered the workflow run. For workflows triggered by push, this is the branch or tag ref that was pushed. For workflows triggered by pull_request, this is the pull request merge branch. For workflows triggered by release, this is the release tag created. For other triggers, this is the branch or tag ref that triggered the workflow run. This is only set if a branch or tag is available for the event type. The ref given is fully-formed, meaning that for branches the format is refs/heads/<branch_name>. For pull requests events except pull_request_target, it is refs/pull/<pr_number>/merge. pull_request_target events have the ref from the base branch. For tags it is refs/tags/<tag_name>. For example, refs/heads/feature-branch-1.
    GITHUB_REF_NAME The short ref name of the branch or tag that triggered the workflow run. This value matches the branch or tag name shown on GitHub. For example, feature-branch-1. For pull requests, the format is <pr_number>/merge.
    GITHUB_REF_PROTECTED true if branch protections or rulesets are configured for the ref that triggered the workflow run.
    GITHUB_REF_TYPE The type of ref that triggered the workflow run. Valid values are branch or tag.
    GITHUB_REPOSITORY The owner and repository name. For example, octocat/Hello-World.
    GITHUB_REPOSITORY_ID The ID of the repository. For example, 123456789. Note that this is different from the repository name.
    GITHUB_REPOSITORY_OWNER The repository owner's name. For example, octocat.
    GITHUB_REPOSITORY_OWNER_ID The repository owner's account ID. For example, 1234567. Note that this is different from the owner's name.
    GITHUB_RETENTION_DAYS The number of days that workflow run logs and artifacts are kept. For example, 90.
    GITHUB_RUN_ATTEMPT A unique number for each attempt of a particular workflow run in a repository. This number begins at 1 for the workflow run's first attempt, and increments with each re-run. For example, 3.
    GITHUB_RUN_ID A unique number for each workflow run within a repository. This number does not change if you re-run the workflow run. For example, 1658821493.
    GITHUB_RUN_NUMBER A unique number for each run of a particular workflow in a repository. This number begins at 1 for the workflow's first run, and increments with each new run. This number does not change if you re-run the workflow run. For example, 3.
    GITHUB_SERVER_URL The URL of the GitHub server. For example: https://github.com.
    GITHUB_SHA The commit SHA that triggered the workflow. The value of this commit SHA depends on the event that triggered the workflow. For more information, see Events that trigger workflows. For example, ffac537e6cbbf934b08745a378932722df287a53.
    GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY The path on the runner to the file that contains job summaries from workflow commands. The path to this file is unique to the current step and changes for each step in a job. For example, /home/runner/_layout/_work/_temp/_runner_file_commands/step_summary_1cb22d7f-5663-41a8-9ffc-13472605c76c. For more information, see Workflow commands for GitHub Actions.
    GITHUB_TRIGGERING_ACTOR The username of the user that initiated the workflow run. If the workflow run is a re-run, this value may differ from github.actor. Any workflow re-runs will use the privileges of github.actor, even if the actor initiating the re-run (github.triggering_actor) has different privileges.
    GITHUB_WORKFLOW The name of the workflow. For example, My test workflow. If the workflow file doesn't specify a name, the value of this variable is the full path of the workflow file in the repository.
    GITHUB_WORKFLOW_REF The ref path to the workflow. For example, octocat/hello-world/.github/workflows/my-workflow.yml@refs/heads/my_branch.
    GITHUB_WORKFLOW_SHA The commit SHA for the workflow file.
    GITHUB_WORKSPACE The default working directory on the runner for steps, and the default location of your repository when using the checkout action. For example, /home/runner/work/my-repo-name/my-repo-name.
    RUNNER_ARCH The architecture of the runner executing the job. Possible values are X86, X64, ARM, or ARM64.
    RUNNER_DEBUG This is set only if debug logging is enabled, and always has the value of 1. It can be useful as an indicator to enable additional debugging or verbose logging in your own job steps.
    RUNNER_ENVIRONMENT The environment of the runner executing the job. Possible values are: github-hosted for GitHub-hosted runners provided by GitHub, and self-hosted for self-hosted runners configured by the repository owner.
    RUNNER_NAME The name of the runner executing the job. This name may not be unique in a workflow run as runners at the repository and organization levels could use the same name. For example, Hosted Agent
    RUNNER_OS The operating system of the runner executing the job. Possible values are Linux, Windows, or macOS. For example, Windows
    RUNNER_TEMP The path to a temporary directory on the runner. This directory is emptied at the beginning and end of each job. Note that files will not be removed if the runner's user account does not have permission to delete them. For example, D:\a\_temp
    RUNNER_TOOL_CACHE The path to the directory containing preinstalled tools for GitHub-hosted runners. For more information, see GitHub-hosted runners. For example, C:\hostedtoolcache\windows
    📝 Note:
    If you need to use a workflow run's URL from within a job, you can combine these variables: $GITHUB_SERVER_URL/$GITHUB_REPOSITORY/actions/runs/$GITHUB_RUN_ID

    Return value

    Default environment variables are read-only strings automatically set by GitHub Actions. They persist throughout the entire job.

    Examples

    1. Display JobPost build metadata

    name: JobPost CI

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Display JobPost build info
        run: |
          echo "🚀 JobPost CI Pipeline"
          echo "Workflow: $GITHUB_WORKFLOW"
          echo "Repository: $GITHUB_REPOSITORY"
          echo "Branch: ${GITHUB_REF#refs/heads/}"
          echo "Commit: ${GITHUB_SHA::7}"
          echo "Actor: $GITHUB_ACTOR"
          echo "Run ID: $GITHUB_RUN_ID"
          echo "Run Number: $GITHUB_RUN_NUMBER"
          echo "Runner OS: $RUNNER_OS"
          echo "Workspace: $GITHUB_WORKSPACE"
    📋 Display JobPost build metadata using default variables

    2. JobPost deployment with environment info

    - name: Deploy JobPost
  run: |
    echo "Deploying JobPost from $GITHUB_REPOSITORY"
    echo "Commit SHA: $GITHUB_SHA"
    echo "Branch: $GITHUB_REF_NAME"
    echo "Triggered by: $GITHUB_ACTOR"
    
    # Create deployment tag with run number
    DEPLOY_TAG="deploy-$GITHUB_RUN_NUMBER-$(date +%Y%m%d)"
    echo "Deployment tag: $DEPLOY_TAG"
    
    # Use workspace path
    cd $GITHUB_WORKSPACE
    python manage.py migrate
    python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
    
    # Tag deployment
    git tag -a "$DEPLOY_TAG" -m "JobPost deployment from run $GITHUB_RUN_ID"
    🚀 JobPost deployment using default environment variables

    3. JobPost conditional logic based on runner

    - name: Install JobPost dependencies
  run: |
    echo "Setting up JobPost on $RUNNER_OS ($RUNNER_ARCH)"
    
    if [ "$RUNNER_OS" == "Linux" ]; then
      echo "Installing JobPost on Linux"
      sudo apt-get update
      sudo apt-get install -y postgresql-client libpq-dev
    elif [ "$RUNNER_OS" == "macOS" ]; then
      echo "Installing JobPost on macOS"
      brew install postgresql
    elif [ "$RUNNER_OS" == "Windows" ]; then
      echo "Installing JobPost on Windows"
      choco install postgresql
    fi
    
    pip install -r requirements.txt
    echo "JobPost dependencies installed successfully"
    💻 OS-specific JobPost setup using RUNNER_OS

    4. JobPost build artifacts with metadata

    - name: Create JobPost build artifact
  run: |
    # Create build info file with all metadata
    cat > build-info.json << EOF
    {
      "application": "JobPost",
      "repository": "$GITHUB_REPOSITORY",
      "commit": "$GITHUB_SHA",
      "branch": "$GITHUB_REF_NAME",
      "run_id": "$GITHUB_RUN_ID",
      "run_number": "$GITHUB_RUN_NUMBER",
      "workflow": "$GITHUB_WORKFLOW",
      "actor": "$GITHUB_ACTOR",
      "runner_os": "$RUNNER_OS",
      "runner_arch": "$RUNNER_ARCH",
      "timestamp": "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)",
      "workspace": "$GITHUB_WORKSPACE"
    }
    EOF
    
    echo "JobPost build info created"
    cat build-info.json
    
    # Include in build artifact
    cp build-info.json dist/
    📦 Create JobPost build metadata file with all default variables

    5. JobPost dynamic configuration using CI variable

    - name: Configure JobPost for CI/CD
  run: |
    # Check if running in CI environment
    if [ "$CI" = "true" ]; then
      echo "Running JobPost in CI mode"
      
      # CI-specific configuration
      export JOBPOST_DEBUG=False
      export JOBPOST_USE_TEST_DB=True
      export JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND=memory
      
      # Use temporary directory for logs
      export JOBPOST_LOG_DIR=$RUNNER_TEMP/jobpost-logs
      mkdir -p $JOBPOST_LOG_DIR
      
      # Set build identifier
      export JOBPOST_BUILD_ID="$GITHUB_RUN_NUMBER-$GITHUB_SHA"
      
      echo "JobPost CI configuration:"
      echo "  Debug: $JOBPOST_DEBUG"
      echo "  Test DB: $JOBPOST_USE_TEST_DB"
      echo "  Log Dir: $JOBPOST_LOG_DIR"
      echo "  Build ID: $JOBPOST_BUILD_ID"
    else
      echo "Running JobPost in local development mode"
    fi
    
    # Run JobPost tests
    python manage.py test jobpost
    ⚙️ Dynamic JobPost configuration based on CI environment

    Naming conventions for configuration variables

    Docs: Configuration variable naming

    The following rules apply to configuration variable names.

    Full description

    Configuration variables have specific naming requirements to ensure they work correctly and don't conflict with system variables. These variables are set at the repository, environment, or organization level and accessed using the vars context. Names are case-insensitive and automatically converted to uppercase. Following proper naming conventions prevents errors and makes your JobPost workflows more maintainable.

    📋 Official Naming Rules for Configuration Variables

    • Can only contain alphanumeric characters ([a-z], [A-Z], [0-9]) or underscores (_). Spaces are not allowed.
    • Must not start with the GITHUB_ prefix.
    • Must not start with a number.
    • Are case insensitive when referenced. GitHub stores secret names as uppercase regardless of how they are entered.
    • Must be unique to the repository, organization, or enterprise where they are created.

    Syntax

    # Access configuration variables using vars context
${{ vars.VARIABLE_NAME }}

    Parameters description

    ✅ Allowed characters and rules:
    • Must start with a letter or underscore (_)
    • Can contain only alphanumeric characters and underscores
    • Case-insensitive (automatically converted to uppercase)
    • Maximum length: 255 characters
    ❌ Not allowed:
    • Cannot start with GITHUB_ prefix (reserved for system variables)
    • Cannot start with a number
    • Cannot contain spaces, hyphens, or special characters (except underscore)
    • Cannot shadow default environment variables
    • Cannot be empty or contain only underscores

    Return value

    Configuration variable names are stored as uppercase strings. Accessing them is case-insensitive, so vars.my_var and vars.MY_VAR reference the same variable.

    Examples

    1. JobPost configuration variables

    name: JobPost Deploy

on: [push]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost with config
        run: |
          # ✅ Good naming conventions
          echo "JobPost API URL: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
          echo "Environment: ${{ vars.DEPLOYMENT_ENVIRONMENT }}"
          echo "Database Host: ${{ vars.DB_HOST }}"
          echo "Cache Backend: ${{ vars.CACHE_BACKEND }}"
          
          # Variables are case-insensitive
          echo "Version: ${{ vars.jobpost_version }}"  # Same as JOBPOST_VERSION
          echo "Version: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_VERSION }}"  # Same result
    ✅ Well-named JobPost configuration variables

    2. JobPost naming best practices

    - name: Use JobPost configuration
  run: |
    # ✅ Good names - descriptive and prefixed
    echo "API Key: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_KEY }}"
    echo "Max Workers: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_MAX_WORKERS }}"
    echo "Enable Cache: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_ENABLE_CACHE }}"
    echo "Log Level: ${{ vars.LOG_LEVEL }}"
    echo "Timeout: ${{ vars.REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS }}"
    
    # ❌ Avoid these patterns (shown as comments)
    # GITHUB_CUSTOM_VAR  # Cannot start with GITHUB_
    # 123_VAR            # Cannot start with number
    # MY-VAR             # Cannot contain hyphens
    # MY VAR             # Cannot contain spaces
    # _ONLY_UNDERSCORES_ # Not descriptive
    📝 JobPost variable naming best practices and anti-patterns

    3. JobPost environment-specific variables

    - name: Configure JobPost by environment
  run: |
    # Use descriptive prefixes for clarity
    echo "Production DB: ${{ vars.PROD_JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    echo "Staging DB: ${{ vars.STAGING_JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    echo "Dev DB: ${{ vars.DEV_JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    
    # Feature flags with clear naming
    echo "New UI: ${{ vars.FEATURE_JOBPOST_NEW_UI }}"
    echo "Beta API: ${{ vars.FEATURE_JOBPOST_BETA_API }}"
    echo "Search V2: ${{ vars.FEATURE_JOBPOST_SEARCH_V2 }}"
    
    # Version-specific configurations
    echo "API Version: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_VERSION }}"
    🏷️ Organized JobPost configuration with environment prefixes

    4. JobPost valid variable name patterns

    - name: Valid JobPost variable names
  run: |
    # ✅ All these are valid naming patterns
    
    # Application-specific prefix
    echo "App: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_APPLICATION_NAME }}"
    
    # Environment indicator
    echo "Env: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_ENV_TYPE }}"
    
    # Service configuration
    echo "Redis: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_REDIS_URL }}"
    echo "Celery: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_CELERY_BROKER }}"
    echo "Email: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_EMAIL_BACKEND }}"
    
    # Starts with underscore (valid)
    echo "Private: ${{ vars._JOBPOST_INTERNAL_KEY }}"
    
    # Numbers after first character (valid)
    echo "DB: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_V2_HOST }}"
    ✅ Examples of valid JobPost variable naming patterns

    5. JobPost variable naming validation

    - name: Validate JobPost variable names
  run: |
    # Check if variables follow naming conventions
    VARS_TO_CHECK=(
      "JOBPOST_API_URL"
      "JOBPOST_MAX_WORKERS"
      "DB_CONNECTION_STRING"
      "_INTERNAL_CONFIG"
      "ENABLE_FEATURE_FLAGS"
    )
    
    for var_name in "${VARS_TO_CHECK[@]}"; do
      # Check if variable name starts with GITHUB_ (reserved)
      if [[ $var_name == GITHUB_* ]]; then
        echo "❌ Invalid: $var_name (reserved prefix)"
      # Check if starts with number
      elif [[ $var_name =~ ^[0-9] ]]; then
        echo "❌ Invalid: $var_name (starts with number)"
      # Check if contains invalid characters
      elif [[ ! $var_name =~ ^[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$ ]]; then
        echo "❌ Invalid: $var_name (invalid characters)"
      else
        echo "✅ Valid: $var_name"
      fi
    done
    🔍 Validate JobPost configuration variable naming conventions

    Naming conventions for environment variables

    Docs: Environment variable naming

    When you set an environment variable, you cannot use any of the default environment variable names.

    Full description

    When you set custom environment variables in your JobPost workflows, they must follow specific naming rules. Environment variables are case-sensitive on Linux and macOS runners, but case-insensitive on Windows runners. To ensure cross-platform compatibility, always use uppercase for environment variable names. These variables can be set at workflow, job, or step level and are accessed differently than configuration variables.

    ⚠️ Official GitHub Restriction:
    When you set an environment variable, you cannot use any of the default environment variable names. For a complete list of default environment variables, see Variables reference above. If you attempt to override the value of one of these default variables, the assignment is ignored.
    📝 Note:
    You can list the entire set of environment variables that are available to a workflow step by using run: env in a step and then examining the output for the step.

    Syntax

    # Set environment variables
echo "VARIABLE_NAME=value" >> $GITHUB_ENV

# Access in shell
echo "$VARIABLE_NAME"

# Access in expressions
${{ env.VARIABLE_NAME }}

    Parameters description

    ✅ Best practices:
    • Use UPPERCASE for all environment variable names
    • Use underscores (_) to separate words
    • Make names descriptive and meaningful
    • Prefix with project name (e.g., JOBPOST_) to avoid conflicts
    • Use consistent naming patterns across workflows
    ⚠️ Avoid:
    • Overriding default GitHub variables (GITHUB_*, RUNNER_*, CI)
    • Using names that conflict with system variables (PATH, HOME, etc.)
    • Using special characters except underscore
    • Using lowercase on Linux/macOS (Windows compatibility)
    • Creating ambiguous or abbreviated names

    Return value

    Environment variables are string values accessible throughout the job after they are set. They persist across steps but not across jobs.

    Examples

    1. JobPost environment variables at multiple levels

    name: JobPost Build

on: [push]

env:
  JOBPOST_VERSION: "2.0.0"
  JOBPOST_BUILD_TYPE: "release"
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      JOBPOST_ENV: "production"
      DATABASE_URL: "postgresql://localhost/jobpost"
      JOBPOST_LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"
    
    steps:
      - name: Build JobPost
        env:
          JOBPOST_DEBUG: "false"
          SECRET_KEY: ${{ secrets.DJANGO_SECRET_KEY }}
          JOBPOST_MAX_WORKERS: "4"
        run: |
          echo "Building JobPost $JOBPOST_VERSION"
          echo "Environment: $JOBPOST_ENV"
          echo "Build type: $JOBPOST_BUILD_TYPE"
          echo "Log level: $JOBPOST_LOG_LEVEL"
          echo "Max workers: $JOBPOST_MAX_WORKERS"
          python manage.py check
    ⚙️ JobPost environment variables at workflow, job, and step levels

    2. JobPost dynamic environment variables

    - name: Set JobPost environment
  run: |
    # Create descriptive environment variables with proper naming
    echo "JOBPOST_DEPLOY_TIME=$(date -u +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_COMMIT_SHORT=${GITHUB_SHA::7}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_BRANCH_NAME=${GITHUB_REF_NAME}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_BUILD_NUMBER=$GITHUB_RUN_NUMBER" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_RUNNER_OS=$RUNNER_OS" >> $GITHUB_ENV

- name: Use JobPost variables
  run: |
    echo "Deploying JobPost"
    echo "Time: $JOBPOST_DEPLOY_TIME"
    echo "Commit: $JOBPOST_COMMIT_SHORT"
    echo "Branch: $JOBPOST_BRANCH_NAME"
    echo "Build: $JOBPOST_BUILD_NUMBER"
    echo "OS: $JOBPOST_RUNNER_OS"
    🔧 Dynamic JobPost environment variables with consistent naming

    3. JobPost environment variable precedence

    name: JobPost Variable Precedence

env:
  JOBPOST_LEVEL: "workflow"     # Workflow level (lowest precedence)
  JOBPOST_TIMEOUT: "30"

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      JOBPOST_LEVEL: "job"      # Job level (overrides workflow)
      JOBPOST_RETRIES: "3"
    
    steps:
      - name: Step level override
        env:
          JOBPOST_LEVEL: "step"  # Step level (highest precedence)
          JOBPOST_VERBOSE: "true"
        run: |
          echo "JobPost level: $JOBPOST_LEVEL"      # Outputs: step
          echo "Timeout: $JOBPOST_TIMEOUT"          # From workflow
          echo "Retries: $JOBPOST_RETRIES"          # From job
          echo "Verbose: $JOBPOST_VERBOSE"          # From step
      
      - name: Job level variable
        run: |
          echo "JobPost level: $JOBPOST_LEVEL"      # Outputs: job
    🎯 JobPost environment variable precedence demonstration

    4. JobPost cross-platform environment variables

    - name: Set cross-platform JobPost variables
  run: |
    # ✅ Use UPPERCASE for cross-platform compatibility
    echo "JOBPOST_DATA_DIR=/opt/jobpost/data" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_CONFIG_FILE=/etc/jobpost/config.yml" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_ENABLE_SSL=true" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    
    # Platform-specific adjustments
    if [ "$RUNNER_OS" == "Windows" ]; then
      echo "JOBPOST_DATA_DIR=C:/JobPost/data" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      echo "JOBPOST_CONFIG_FILE=C:/JobPost/config.yml" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    fi

- name: Use JobPost variables
  run: |
    echo "Data directory: $JOBPOST_DATA_DIR"
    echo "Config file: $JOBPOST_CONFIG_FILE"
    echo "SSL enabled: $JOBPOST_ENABLE_SSL"
    
    # Variables work consistently across platforms
    mkdir -p "$JOBPOST_DATA_DIR"
    🖥️ Cross-platform JobPost environment variable usage

    5. JobPost environment variable organization

    - name: Organize JobPost environment variables
  run: |
    # Group related variables with consistent prefixes
    
    # Database configuration
    echo "JOBPOST_DB_HOST=localhost" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_DB_PORT=5432" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_DB_NAME=jobpost_prod" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_DB_USER=postgres" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    
    # Cache configuration
    echo "JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND=redis" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_CACHE_HOST=localhost" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_CACHE_PORT=6379" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    
    # Application settings
    echo "JOBPOST_APP_NAME=JobPost" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_APP_VERSION=2.0.0" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    echo "JOBPOST_APP_ENV=production" >> $GITHUB_ENV

- name: Display JobPost configuration
  run: |
    echo "📊 JobPost Database Configuration:"
    echo "  Host: $JOBPOST_DB_HOST:$JOBPOST_DB_PORT"
    echo "  Database: $JOBPOST_DB_NAME"
    echo ""
    echo "🔄 JobPost Cache Configuration:"
    echo "  Backend: $JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND"
    echo "  Host: $JOBPOST_CACHE_HOST:$JOBPOST_CACHE_PORT"
    echo ""
    echo "🚀 JobPost Application:"
    echo "  Name: $JOBPOST_APP_NAME"
    echo "  Version: $JOBPOST_APP_VERSION"
    echo "  Environment: $JOBPOST_APP_ENV"
    📋 Organized JobPost environment variables with logical grouping

    Configuration variable precedence

    Docs: Variable precedence

    If a variable with the same name exists at multiple levels, the variable at the lowest level takes precedence.

    Full description

    When you define a configuration variable with the same name at multiple levels (organization, repository, and environment), GitHub Actions uses a precedence hierarchy to determine which value to use. The variable at the most specific level takes precedence, allowing you to set organization-wide defaults while overriding them for specific repositories or environments. This is particularly useful for JobPost deployments across different environments (development, staging, production) where certain settings need to vary.

    📊 Official GitHub Precedence Rules

    If a variable with the same name exists at multiple levels, the variable at the lowest level takes precedence. For example, if an organization-level variable has the same name as a repository-level variable, then the repository-level variable takes precedence. Similarly, if an organization, repository, and environment all have a variable with the same name, the environment-level variable takes precedence.
    📝 Note:
    Environment-level variables are only available on the runner after the job starts executing. This means that environment-level variables won't overwrite variables in the env and vars contexts.
    💡 Reusable Workflows:
    For reusable workflows, the variables from the caller workflow's repository are used. Variables from the repository that contains the called workflow are not made available to the caller workflow.

    Syntax

    # Access configuration variables (automatically resolves precedence)
${{ vars.VARIABLE_NAME }}

# Use in environment-specific job
jobs:
  deploy:
    environment: production  # Uses production environment variables first
    steps:
      - run: echo "${{ vars.API_URL }}"

    Parameters description

    Precedence order (highest to lowest):
    1. Environment variable - Defined in a specific environment (production, staging, etc.)
    2. Repository variable - Defined for the specific repository
    3. Organization variable - Defined at organization level (lowest priority)
    Rules:
    • More specific levels override less specific levels
    • If variable not found at environment level, checks repository level
    • If variable not found at repository level, checks organization level
    • Variables are resolved at workflow execution time

    Return value

    Returns the value from the highest precedence level where the variable is defined. If not found at any level, the expression evaluates to an empty string.

    Examples

    1. JobPost multi-level configuration

    name: JobPost Deploy

on: [push]

jobs:
  deploy-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to staging
        run: |
          # Uses environment-level variables (highest precedence)
          # staging environment: JOBPOST_DEPLOY_URL = "https://staging.jobpost.com"
          # repository: JOBPOST_DEPLOY_URL = "https://jobpost.com"
          # organization: JOBPOST_DEPLOY_URL = "https://default.jobpost.com"
          echo "Deploying JobPost to: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DEPLOY_URL }}"
          # Output: https://staging.jobpost.com
          
          echo "API Key: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_KEY }}"
          echo "Max Connections: ${{ vars.DB_MAX_CONNECTIONS }}"
  
  deploy-production:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to production
        run: |
          # Uses production environment variables
          echo "Deploying JobPost to: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DEPLOY_URL }}"
          # Output: https://production.jobpost.com (from production env)
    🎯 JobPost environment-specific variable precedence

    2. JobPost fallback configuration

    - name: Configure JobPost with fallbacks
  run: |
    # Environment variable takes precedence if set
    # Falls back to repository variable
    # Falls back to organization variable
    DEPLOY_URL="${{ vars.JOBPOST_DEPLOY_URL }}"
    API_KEY="${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_KEY }}"
    
    echo "JobPost Deploy URL: $DEPLOY_URL"
    echo "JobPost API configured"
    
    # Provide defaults if not set at any level
    MAX_WORKERS="${{ vars.JOBPOST_MAX_WORKERS || '4' }}"
    TIMEOUT="${{ vars.JOBPOST_TIMEOUT || '30' }}"
    RETRY_COUNT="${{ vars.JOBPOST_RETRY_COUNT || '3' }}"
    
    echo "Max workers: $MAX_WORKERS"
    echo "Timeout: $TIMEOUT seconds"
    echo "Retries: $RETRY_COUNT"
    🔄 JobPost configuration with fallback values and defaults

    3. JobPost precedence example scenario

    # Variable defined at three levels:
# Organization: JOBPOST_API_URL = "https://api.example.com"
# Repository: JOBPOST_API_URL = "https://jobpost-api.example.com"
# Environment (prod): JOBPOST_API_URL = "https://prod.jobpost-api.example.com"
# Environment (staging): Not defined

- name: Show JobPost API precedence in production
  environment: production
  run: |
    # This will use the production environment value (highest precedence)
    # Result: https://prod.jobpost-api.example.com
    echo "JobPost API: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    
- name: Show JobPost API precedence in staging
  environment: staging
  run: |
    # Environment level not defined, falls back to repository level
    # Result: https://jobpost-api.example.com
    echo "JobPost API: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    📊 JobPost variable precedence with multiple environment levels

    4. JobPost repository-level override

    name: JobPost Repo-Specific Config

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # No environment specified, uses repository-level variables
    steps:
      - name: Build JobPost with repo config
        run: |
          # Organization: JOBPOST_BUILD_WORKERS = "2" (org default)
          # Repository: JOBPOST_BUILD_WORKERS = "8" (repo override)
          echo "Build workers: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_BUILD_WORKERS }}"
          # Output: 8 (repository level takes precedence)
          
          # Organization: JOBPOST_ENABLE_CACHE = "false"
          # Repository: JOBPOST_ENABLE_CACHE = "true"
          echo "Cache enabled: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_ENABLE_CACHE }}"
          # Output: true (repository level)
          
          # Only at organization level
          echo "Org setting: ${{ vars.ORGANIZATION_WIDE_SETTING }}"
          # Output: value from organization (no repository override)
    🏢 JobPost repository-level configuration overriding organization defaults

    5. JobPost dynamic precedence handling

    - name: Handle JobPost variable precedence dynamically
  environment: ${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && 'production' || 'staging' }}
  run: |
    # Environment is selected dynamically based on branch
    # main branch uses 'production' environment variables
    # other branches use 'staging' environment variables
    
    echo "� JobPost Deployment Configuration"
    echo "Environment: ${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && 'production' || 'staging' }}"
    echo "API URL: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    echo "Database: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    echo "Cache: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND }}"
    
    # Check which level variable came from (for debugging)
    echo ""
    echo "📋 Variable Sources:"
    echo "  JOBPOST_API_URL: Resolved from current environment or repository"
    echo "  JOBPOST_DB_HOST: Resolved from current environment or repository"
    
    # Display precedence information
    if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
      echo "  Priority: production env > repository > organization"
    else
      echo "  Priority: staging env > repository > organization"
    fi
    🔀 Dynamic JobPost environment selection with precedence handling

    Limits for configuration variables

    Docs: Variable limits

    Individual variables are limited to 48 KB in size. You can store up to 1,000 organization variables, 500 variables per repository, and 100 variables per environment.

    Full description

    GitHub Actions enforces specific limits on configuration variables to ensure optimal performance and prevent abuse. These limits apply to the number of variables you can create at different levels and the size of variable names and values. Understanding these constraints is essential for planning your JobPost workflow configuration strategy. When approaching these limits, consider consolidating variables or using alternative storage methods like artifacts or repository files.

    📏 Official Variable Limits

    • Individual variable size: Limited to 48 KB
    • Organization variables: Up to 1,000 variables
    • Repository variables: 500 variables per repository
    • Environment variables: 100 variables per environment
    • Total combined size: 256 KB per workflow run
    📋 Workflow Variable Availability

    A workflow created in a repository can access the following number of variables:
    • Up to 500 repository variables, if the total size of repository variables is less than 256 KB. If the total size of repository variables exceeds 256 KB, only the repository variables that fall below the limit will be available (as sorted alphabetically by variable name).
    • Up to 1,000 organization variables, if the total combined size of repository and organization variables is less than 256 KB. If the total combined size of organization and repository variables exceeds 256 KB, only the organization variables that fall below that limit will be available (after accounting for repository variables and as sorted alphabetically by variable name).
    • Up to 100 environment-level variables.
    📝 Note:
    Environment-level variables do not count toward the 256 KB total size limit. If you exceed the combined size limit for repository and organization variables and still need additional variables, you can use an environment and define additional variables in the environment.

    Syntax

    # Configuration variables are accessed via vars context
${{ vars.VARIABLE_NAME }}

# Subject to these limits:
# - Max variables per level
# - Max name length: 255 characters
# - Max value size: 48 KB

    Parameters description

    Number of variables:
    • Up to 1,000 organization variables per organization
    • Up to 500 repository variables per repository
    • Up to 100 environment variables per environment
    • Total across all levels: 1,600 variables maximum
    Size limits:
    • Variable names: Maximum 255 characters
    • Variable values: Maximum 48 KB (49,152 characters) per variable
    • Total workflow size: Up to 256 KB for all variables combined in a single workflow run
    • API rate limits: Standard GitHub API rate limits apply when creating/updating variables

    Return value

    Configuration variables return string values up to 48 KB in size. Attempting to create variables exceeding these limits will result in an error.

    Examples

    1. JobPost within variable limits

    - name: Check JobPost variable sizes
  run: |
    # ✅ Good: Concise variable names (well under 255 chars)
    API_URL="${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    DB_HOST="${{ vars.DB_HOST }}"
    CACHE_BACKEND="${{ vars.JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND }}"
    
    # ✅ Good: Reasonable value sizes
    CONFIG="${{ vars.JOBPOST_CONFIG }}"
    
    # Display variable sizes for monitoring
    echo "JobPost variable sizes:"
    echo "  API_URL: ${#API_URL} characters"
    echo "  DB_HOST: ${#DB_HOST} characters"
    echo "  CONFIG: ${#CONFIG} characters"
    
    # Warn if approaching size limit (48 KB = 49,152 chars)
    if [ ${#CONFIG} -gt 45000 ]; then
      echo "⚠️ WARNING: Config variable approaching 48 KB limit"
      echo "Consider splitting into multiple variables"
    elif [ ${#CONFIG} -gt 10000 ]; then
      echo "ℹ️ INFO: Config variable is large (${#CONFIG} chars)"
    fi
    📏 Monitor JobPost variable sizes to stay within limits

    2. JobPost variable optimization

    - name: Optimize JobPost configuration
  run: |
    # ❌ Bad: Storing large JSON configuration in single variable
    # JOBPOST_CONFIG: '{"database": {...1000s of lines...}, "cache": {...}, ...}'
    # Problem: Approaches 48 KB limit, hard to maintain
    
    # ✅ Good: Split into multiple smaller, focused variables
    echo "Database Host: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    echo "Database Port: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_PORT }}"
    echo "Database Name: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_NAME }}"
    echo "Cache Host: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_CACHE_HOST }}"
    echo "Cache Backend: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND }}"
    echo "API Endpoint: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    echo "API Version: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_VERSION }}"
    
    # ✅ Good: Store large configs as repository files
    if [ -f config/jobpost-production.json ]; then
      echo "Loading JobPost production config from file"
      CONFIG_DATA=$(cat config/jobpost-production.json)
      echo "Config loaded: ${#CONFIG_DATA} characters"
    fi
    
    # ✅ Good: Use artifacts for generated large data
    echo "Generating build metadata..."
    python scripts/generate_build_info.py > build-info.json
    ⚡ Optimized JobPost variable usage to avoid size limits

    3. JobPost variable count management

    - name: Manage JobPost variables efficiently
  run: |
    # Strategy: Group related settings to stay within count limits
    # Repository limit: 500 variables
    # Environment limit: 100 variables per environment
    
    # ✅ Good: Organized with prefixes (counts as separate variables)
    echo "Production DB: ${{ vars.PROD_JOBPOST_DB }}"
    echo "Staging DB: ${{ vars.STAGING_JOBPOST_DB }}"
    echo "Dev DB: ${{ vars.DEV_JOBPOST_DB }}"
    
    # ✅ Good: Use JSON for complex configs (single variable, multiple values)
    JOBPOST_FEATURES='${{ vars.JOBPOST_FEATURES_JSON }}'
    echo "Features JSON: $JOBPOST_FEATURES"
    
    # Parse JSON to extract multiple values from one variable
    ENABLE_CACHE=$(echo $JOBPOST_FEATURES | jq -r '.cache_enabled')
    ENABLE_API=$(echo $JOBPOST_FEATURES | jq -r '.api_v2_enabled')
    ENABLE_SEARCH=$(echo $JOBPOST_FEATURES | jq -r '.search_enabled')
    MAX_UPLOAD=$(echo $JOBPOST_FEATURES | jq -r '.max_upload_size')
    
    echo "Cache enabled: $ENABLE_CACHE"
    echo "API v2 enabled: $ENABLE_API"
    echo "Search enabled: $ENABLE_SEARCH"
    echo "Max upload: $MAX_UPLOAD MB"
    
    # This approach uses 1 variable instead of 4
    🎛️ Efficient JobPost variable management using JSON consolidation

    4. JobPost variable limit monitoring

    - name: Monitor JobPost variable usage
  run: |
    echo "📊 JobPost Variable Usage Report"
    echo ""
    
    # Count variables (example - actual count requires API)
    echo "Repository Variables:"
    echo "  Total used: ~50 / 500 (10%)"
    echo "  Available: ~450"
    echo ""
    
    # List key JobPost variables with sizes
    VARS=(
      "JOBPOST_API_URL:${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
      "JOBPOST_DB_HOST:${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
      "JOBPOST_CONFIG:${{ vars.JOBPOST_CONFIG }}"
      "JOBPOST_FEATURES:${{ vars.JOBPOST_FEATURES_JSON }}"
    )
    
    echo "Variable Sizes:"
    for var_entry in "${VARS[@]}"; do
      var_name="${var_entry%%:*}"
      var_value="${var_entry#*:}"
      echo "  $var_name: ${#var_value} characters"
    done
    echo ""
    
    # Recommendations
    echo "💡 Recommendations:"
    echo "  - Stay under 400 repository variables for safety margin"
    echo "  - Keep individual values under 40 KB (83% of limit)"
    echo "  - Use JSON consolidation for related settings"
    echo "  - Store large data in repository files or artifacts"
    📈 JobPost variable usage monitoring and recommendations

    5. JobPost alternative storage strategies

    - name: JobPost alternative storage for large data
  run: |
    # When approaching variable limits, use alternatives:
    
    # Strategy 1: Repository files for static configs
    echo "Loading JobPost config from repository file..."
    if [ -f configs/production.env ]; then
      source configs/production.env
      echo "✅ Loaded config from file"
    fi
    
    # Strategy 2: Secrets for sensitive data (same limits as vars)
    SECRET_KEY="${{ secrets.DJANGO_SECRET_KEY }}"
    echo "✅ Using secrets for sensitive data"
    
    # Strategy 3: Artifacts for generated/dynamic data
    echo "Generating large JobPost deployment manifest..."
    python scripts/generate_manifest.py --output manifest.json
    echo "✅ Generated manifest (not stored in variables)"
    
    # Strategy 4: External configuration service
    echo "Fetching JobPost config from external service..."
    curl -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.CONFIG_API_TOKEN }}" \
         https://config.example.com/jobpost/production > external-config.json
    echo "✅ Loaded config from external service"
    
    # Strategy 5: Build-time code generation
    echo "Generating JobPost settings at build time..."
    cat > anihpj/generated_settings.py << EOF
    # Auto-generated settings
    API_URL = "${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    DB_HOST = "${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    # ... more settings ...
    EOF
    echo "✅ Generated Python settings file"
    🔄 Alternative JobPost storage strategies to work around variable limits

    Supported contexts

    Docs: Context availability

    You will commonly use either the env or github context to access variable values in parts of the workflow that are processed before jobs are sent to runners.

    Full description

    Variables can be accessed through different contexts depending on where they're defined and where you're accessing them in your workflow. Each context has specific availability rules within workflows. The vars context provides access to configuration variables and is available in most workflow locations. The env context provides access to environment variables but only after they are set. Understanding context availability is crucial for building robust JobPost workflows that reference variables correctly at each stage.

    ⚠️ Warning:
    Do not print the github context to logs. It contains sensitive information.
    📋 Official Supported Contexts Table

    Context Use case Example
    env Reference custom variables defined in the workflow. ${{ env.MY_VARIABLE }}
    github Reference information about the workflow run and the event that triggered the run. ${{ github.repository }}

    Syntax

    # vars context - available throughout workflow
${{ vars.VARIABLE_NAME }}

# env context - available after environment variables are set
${{ env.VARIABLE_NAME }}

# Access in shell (after variables are defined)
echo "$VARIABLE_NAME"

    Parameters description

    vars context:
    • Available in: workflow-level env, job-level if, step-level run
    • Available in: conditionals, expressions, action inputs
    • Not available in: workflow triggers (on), workflow name
    env context:
    • Available in: step-level run commands (after env is set)
    • Available in: expressions within steps (after variable is defined)
    • Not available in: step-level if conditionals (use shell conditionals instead)
    secrets context:
    • Available in: workflow file, limited in log outputs (masked)
    • Similar availability to vars context
    github context:
    • Available throughout: entire workflow at all levels
    • Automatically populated with workflow metadata

    Return value

    Contexts return string values when accessed via expressions. Availability depends on context type and workflow location. Accessing unavailable contexts results in empty strings or errors.

    Examples

    1. JobPost context usage locations

    name: JobPost Context Examples

on: [push]

# ✅ vars context available at workflow level
env:
  JOBPOST_API: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}
  JOBPOST_VERSION: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_VERSION }}

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # ✅ vars context available in job-level conditional
    if: vars.JOBPOST_ENABLE_BUILD == 'true'
    
    # ✅ vars context available in job-level env
    env:
      JOBPOST_DB: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}
    
    steps:
      - name: Setup
        # ✅ vars context available in step run
        run: |
          echo "API: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
          echo "Version: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_VERSION }}"
      
      - name: Use workflow-level env
        # ✅ env context available (from workflow level)
        run: |
          echo "API from env: $JOBPOST_API"
          echo "API from context: ${{ env.JOBPOST_API }}"
      
      - name: Create dynamic env
        run: echo "JOBPOST_BUILD_TIME=$(date)" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Use dynamic env
        # ✅ env context includes dynamically set variables
        run: echo "Build time: ${{ env.JOBPOST_BUILD_TIME }}"
    🎯 JobPost contexts at different workflow levels

    2. JobPost conditional with contexts

    jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # ✅ vars context in job-level conditionals
    if: vars.JOBPOST_DEPLOY_ENABLED == 'true'
    
    steps:
      - name: Check environment
        # ✅ Multiple contexts in step conditionals
        if: vars.ENVIRONMENT == 'production' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: echo "Deploying JobPost to production"
      
      - name: Set deploy time
        run: echo "DEPLOY_TIME=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Check deploy time
        # ❌ Cannot use env context in step-level if
        # Must use shell conditional instead
        run: |
          if [ -n "$DEPLOY_TIME" ]; then
            echo "✅ JobPost deployment at $DEPLOY_TIME"
          else
            echo "❌ DEPLOY_TIME not set"
          fi
      
      - name: Combine contexts
        # ✅ vars, secrets, github contexts all work in conditionals
        if: vars.ENABLE_NOTIFICATIONS == 'true' && github.event_name == 'push'
        run: |
          echo "Sending JobPost deployment notification"
          curl -X POST ${{ vars.WEBHOOK_URL }} \
               -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.WEBHOOK_TOKEN }}" \
               -d '{"status": "deployed", "commit": "${{ github.sha }}"}'
    🔀 JobPost conditional execution with multiple contexts

    3. JobPost context combinations

    - name: Combine JobPost contexts
  run: |
    echo "📋 JobPost Configuration from Multiple Contexts"
    echo ""
    
    # vars context - configuration variables
    echo "🔧 Configuration Variables (vars):"
    echo "  API URL: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    echo "  DB Host: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    echo "  Cache: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_CACHE_BACKEND }}"
    echo ""
    
    # secrets context - sensitive data (masked in logs)
    echo "🔐 Secrets (secrets):"
    echo "  API Key: ${{ secrets.JOBPOST_API_KEY }}"
    echo "  DB Password: ${{ secrets.DB_PASSWORD }}"
    echo ""
    
    # github context - workflow metadata
    echo "📦 GitHub Context:"
    echo "  Repository: ${{ github.repository }}"
    echo "  Branch: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
    echo "  Commit: ${{ github.sha }}"
    echo "  Actor: ${{ github.actor }}"
    echo "  Run ID: ${{ github.run_id }}"
    echo ""
    
    # env context - environment variables (if set earlier)
    echo "🌍 Environment Variables (env):"
    echo "  Custom: ${{ env.JOBPOST_CUSTOM_VAR }}"
    echo ""
    
    # Combine contexts in expressions
    FULL_URL="${{ vars.JOBPOST_PROTOCOL }}://${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}/api/v${{ vars.API_VERSION }}"
    echo "🔗 Combined URL: $FULL_URL"
    🔗 Combining multiple JobPost contexts in one workflow

    4. JobPost context syntax variations

    - name: JobPost context syntax examples
  env:
    # ✅ Expression syntax (evaluated by GitHub Actions)
    API_URL: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}
    DB_HOST: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}
    API_VERSION: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_VERSION }}
    # ✅ Combine contexts in env
    GITHUB_BRANCH: ${{ github.ref_name }}
  run: |
    echo "🔤 JobPost Context Syntax Variations"
    echo ""
    
    # ✅ Environment variable syntax (evaluated by shell)
    echo "Method 1 - Shell env vars:"
    echo "  API: $API_URL"
    echo "  DB: $DB_HOST"
    echo "  Branch: $GITHUB_BRANCH"
    echo ""
    
    # ✅ Direct expression syntax in run (evaluated by GitHub)
    echo "Method 2 - Direct expressions:"
    echo "  API: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
    echo "  DB: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
    echo "  Commit: ${{ github.sha }}"
    echo ""
    
    # ✅ Mixed approach - best for complex strings
    echo "Method 3 - Mixed syntax:"
    FULL_URL="${{ vars.JOBPOST_PROTOCOL }}://$DB_HOST:${{ vars.JOBPOST_PORT }}"
    CONNECTION="${{ github.actor }}@$DB_HOST"
    echo "  URL: $FULL_URL"
    echo "  Connection: $CONNECTION"
    📝 JobPost context syntax variations and best practices

    5. JobPost context availability troubleshooting

    name: JobPost Context Availability

on: [push]

# ❌ Cannot use vars in workflow name
# name: Deploy ${{ vars.JOBPOST_VERSION }}  # Won't work

# ✅ Can use vars in workflow-level env
env:
  VERSION: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_VERSION }}

jobs:
  test-contexts:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # ✅ Can use vars in job conditionals
    if: vars.JOBPOST_ENABLE_TESTS == 'true'
    
    steps:
      - name: Test context availability
        run: |
          echo "🧪 Testing JobPost Context Availability"
          echo ""
          
          # ✅ vars context - always available
          echo "✅ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
          
          # ✅ github context - always available
          echo "✅ github.repository: ${{ github.repository }}"
          
          # ⚠️ env context - only after set
          # This works because VERSION was set at workflow level
          echo "✅ env.VERSION: ${{ env.VERSION }}"
      
      - name: Set dynamic variable
        run: echo "DYNAMIC_VAR=test123" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Use dynamic variable
        # ❌ Cannot use env.DYNAMIC_VAR in step if
        # if: env.DYNAMIC_VAR == 'test123'  # Won't work
        run: |
          # ✅ Use shell conditional instead
          if [ "$DYNAMIC_VAR" == "test123" ]; then
            echo "✅ Dynamic variable accessible in shell"
          fi
          
          # ✅ Can use in expression within run
          echo "Dynamic var: ${{ env.DYNAMIC_VAR }}"
      
      - name: Context availability summary
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost Context Availability Summary"
          echo ""
          echo "✅ Available everywhere:"
          echo "   - vars context"
          echo "   - github context"
          echo "   - secrets context"
          echo ""
          echo "⚠️ Limited availability:"
          echo "   - env context (only after variables are set)"
          echo "   - Cannot use env in step-level if"
          echo ""
          echo "❌ Not available:"
          echo "   - vars in workflow name"
          echo "   - contexts in trigger configuration (on:)"
    � JobPost context availability rules and troubleshooting guide

    Literals Operators

    Docs: Literals and operators

    As part of an expression, you can use boolean, null, number, or string data types.

    Official Literals Documentation

    📋 Data Types and Literal Values

    Data type Literal value
    boolean true or false
    null null
    number Any number format supported by JSON.
    string You don't need to enclose strings in ${{ }}. However, if you do, you must use single quotes (') around the string. To use a literal single quote, escape the literal single quote using an additional single quote (''). Wrapping with double quotes (") will throw an error.
    📝 Note on Truthiness:
    In conditionals, falsy values (false, 0, -0, "", '', null) are coerced to false and truthy (true and other non-falsy values) are coerced to true.

    Official Example of Literals

    env:
  myNull: ${{ null }}
  myBoolean: ${{ false }}
  myIntegerNumber: ${{ 711 }}
  myFloatNumber: ${{ -9.2 }}
  myHexNumber: ${{ 0xff }}
  myExponentialNumber: ${{ -2.99e-2 }}
  myString: Mona the Octocat
  myStringInBraces: ${{ 'It''s open source!' }}

    Official Operators Documentation

    📋 Operators Reference Table

    Operator Description
    ( ) Logical grouping
    [ ] Index
    . Property de-reference
    ! Not
    < Less than
    <= Less than or equal
    > Greater than
    >= Greater than or equal
    == Equal
    != Not equal
    && And
    || Or
    📝 Important Notes:
    • GitHub ignores case when comparing strings.
    • steps.<step_id>.outputs.<output_name> evaluates as a string. You need to use specific syntax to tell GitHub to evaluate an expression rather than treat it as a string. For more information, see Contexts reference.
    • For numerical comparison, the fromJSON() function can be used to convert a string to a number.
    ⚠️ Type Coercion:

    GitHub performs loose equality comparisons. If the types do not match, GitHub coerces the type to a number. GitHub casts data types to a number using these conversions:

    Type Result
    Null 0
    Boolean true returns 1
    false returns 0
    String Parsed from any legal JSON number format, otherwise NaN. Note: empty string returns 0.
    Array NaN
    Object NaN

    When NaN is one of the operands of any relational comparison (>, <, >=, <=), the result is always false. For more information, see the NaN Mozilla docs.

    GitHub ignores case when comparing strings.

    Objects and arrays are only considered equal when they are the same instance.
    💡 Ternary-like Pattern with Binary Operators:

    GitHub provides a way to create conditional logic in expressions using binary logical operators (&& and ||). This pattern can be used to achieve similar functionality to the ternary operator (?:) found in many programming languages, while actually using only binary operators.

    Example: 
    env:
  MY_ENV_VAR: ${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && 'value_for_main_branch' || 'value_for_other_branches' }}
    In this example, we're using a combination of && and || operators to set the value of the MY_ENV_VAR environment variable based on whether the GitHub reference is set to refs/heads/main or not. If it is, the variable is set to value_for_main_branch. Otherwise, it is set to value_for_other_branches. It is important to note that the first value after the && must be truthy. Otherwise, the value after the || will always be returned.

    Full description

    GitHub Actions expressions allow you to programmatically set environment variables and access contexts using literals and operators. Literals include boolean, null, number, and string types. Operators enable comparisons, logical operations, and data type checking. Expressions are evaluated using ${{ }} syntax and are particularly useful for JobPost workflow conditionals, dynamic values, and complex logic. All operators follow JavaScript-like syntax with type coercion rules.

    Syntax

    # Expression syntax
${{ expression }}

# Comparison operators: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=
${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' }}

# Logical operators: &&, ||, !
${{ success() && vars.DEPLOY_ENABLED == 'true' }}

# Other operators: contains(), startsWith(), endsWith(), format(), join()

    Parameters description

    Literals:
    • true / false - Boolean values
    • null - Null value
    • Numbers - Integer or floating-point (e.g., 42, 3.14)
    • Strings - Single or double quotes (e.g., 'main', "production")
    Comparison operators:
    • == - Equal (loose equality with type coercion)
    • != - Not equal
    • < - Less than
    • <= - Less than or equal
    • > - Greater than
    • >= - Greater than or equal
    Logical operators:
    • && - Logical AND (both must be true)
    • || - Logical OR (at least one must be true)
    • ! - Logical NOT (negation)
    Other operators:
    • () - Grouping for precedence
    • [ ] - Array/object index access

    Return value

    Expressions return boolean values for conditionals, or the evaluated result (string, number, boolean, array, object) for assignments and other contexts.

    Examples

    1. JobPost comparison operators

    name: JobPost Comparison

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # String equality comparison
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - name: Production deployment
        run: echo "Deploying JobPost to production"
  
  staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # String inequality comparison
    if: github.ref != 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - name: Staging deployment
        run: echo "Deploying JobPost to staging"
  
  version-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Check JobPost version
        # Numeric comparisons
        if: vars.JOBPOST_VERSION_MAJOR >= 2
        run: |
          echo "JobPost version is 2.0 or higher"
          echo "Using new API endpoints"
      
      - name: Check worker count
        # Greater than comparison
        if: vars.JOBPOST_MAX_WORKERS > 4
        run: echo "High-performance JobPost configuration detected"
    ⚖️ JobPost workflow using comparison operators

    2. JobPost logical operators

    name: JobPost Logical Operations

on: [push]

jobs:
  conditional-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # AND operator - both conditions must be true
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && vars.JOBPOST_DEPLOY_ENABLED == 'true'
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost
        run: echo "Deploying JobPost (main branch + enabled)"
  
  notify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # OR operator - at least one must be true
    if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    steps:
      - name: Send notification
        run: echo "JobPost notification: triggered by push or manual"
  
  skip-ci:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # NOT operator - negation
    if: "!contains(github.event.head_commit.message, '[skip ci]')"
    steps:
      - name: Run JobPost tests
        run: |
          echo "Running JobPost test suite"
          python manage.py test jobpost
  
  complex-condition:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Combined logical operators with grouping
    if: |
      (github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' || github.ref == 'refs/heads/develop') &&
      vars.JOBPOST_CI_ENABLED == 'true' &&
      !cancelled()
    steps:
      - name: Complex JobPost workflow
        run: echo "JobPost CI on main/develop branches"
    🔗 JobPost workflow using logical AND, OR, NOT operators

    3. JobPost literals in expressions

    name: JobPost Literals

on: [push]

env:
  # String literal
  JOBPOST_ENV: 'production'
  # Number literal
  JOBPOST_TIMEOUT: 30
  # Boolean literal
  JOBPOST_DEBUG: false

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: String comparison with literals
        if: vars.ENVIRONMENT == 'production'
        run: echo "JobPost production environment"
      
      - name: Number comparison with literals
        if: vars.JOBPOST_MAX_WORKERS >= 4
        run: echo "JobPost has 4+ workers"
      
      - name: Boolean literal comparison
        if: vars.JOBPOST_ENABLE_CACHE == true
        run: echo "JobPost caching enabled"
      
      - name: Null check
        if: env.OPTIONAL_SETTING != null
        run: echo "Optional JobPost setting is configured"
      
      - name: Mixed literals
        run: |
          # Use literals in variable assignments
          ENABLED="${{ true }}"
          COUNT="${{ 10 }}"
          NAME="${{ 'JobPost' }}"
          
          echo "Enabled: $ENABLED"
          echo "Count: $COUNT"
          echo "Name: $NAME"
    📝 JobPost workflow using different literal types
