    4. JobPost operator precedence and grouping

    name: JobPost Operator Precedence

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  precedence-example:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Without grouping - AND has higher precedence than OR
    # Evaluates as: (github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && vars.DEPLOY == 'true') || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && vars.DEPLOY == 'true' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    steps:
      - name: Deploy without grouping
        run: echo "JobPost deployment (precedence)"
  
  grouped-example:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # With grouping - explicit precedence control
    # Evaluates as: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && (vars.DEPLOY == 'true' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch')
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && (vars.DEPLOY == 'true' || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch')
    steps:
      - name: Deploy with grouping
        run: echo "JobPost deployment (grouped)"
  
  complex-precedence:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Complex JobPost condition
        # Multiple levels of grouping
        if: |
          (github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main') ||
          (github.event_name == 'pull_request' && vars.TEST_PR == 'true') ||
          (github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' && !cancelled())
        run: echo "JobPost triggered by complex condition"
      
      - name: Negation with grouping
        # NOT operator with grouped conditions
        if: "!(github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && vars.SKIP_MAIN == 'true')"
        run: echo "JobPost: not skipping main branch"
    🔢 JobPost operator precedence and grouping examples

    5. JobPost type coercion in comparisons

    name: JobPost Type Coercion

on: [push]

jobs:
  coercion-examples:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: String to boolean coercion
        # String 'true' compared with boolean true
        if: vars.JOBPOST_ENABLED == true
        run: echo "JobPost enabled (string 'true' == boolean true)"
      
      - name: Number to string coercion
        # Number compared with string
        if: vars.JOBPOST_VERSION == '2'
        run: echo "JobPost version 2 (number 2 == string '2')"
      
      - name: Empty string is falsy
        if: vars.OPTIONAL_VAR == ''
        run: echo "JobPost optional variable is empty"
      
      - name: Null vs undefined
        # Check if variable exists
        if: vars.MAYBE_UNDEFINED != null
        run: echo "JobPost variable is defined"
      
      - name: Truthiness in conditions
        run: |
          echo "🔍 JobPost Type Coercion Examples:"
          echo ""
          echo "String 'true' == boolean true: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_ENABLED == true }}"
          echo "Number 2 == string '2': ${{ 2 == '2' }}"
          echo "Empty string is falsy: ${{ '' == false }}"
          echo "Non-empty string is truthy: ${{ 'value' != false }}"
          echo ""
          
          # Explicit type comparisons
          if [ "${{ vars.JOBPOST_WORKERS }}" -eq "4" ]; then
            echo "JobPost has exactly 4 workers"
          fi
    🔄 JobPost type coercion behavior in expressions

    Functions

    Docs: Expression functions

    GitHub offers a set of built-in functions that you can use in expressions.

    Full description

    Expression functions enable powerful data transformation and manipulation within your JobPost workflows. GitHub Actions includes functions for string operations (contains, startsWith, endsWith), formatting (format), data conversion (toJSON, fromJSON), array operations (join), and hash generation (hashFiles). These functions can be combined to create complex logic for conditionals, dynamic values, and workflow automation. All functions are case-sensitive and follow JavaScript-like syntax.

    📝 Type Casting:
    Some functions cast values to a string to perform comparisons. GitHub casts data types to a string using these conversions:

    Type Result
    Null ''
    Boolean 'true' or 'false'
    Number Decimal format, exponential for large numbers
    Array Arrays are not converted to a string
    Object Objects are not converted to a string

    Official Function Reference

    🔍 contains
    contains( search, item )

    Returns true if search contains item. If search is an array, this function returns true if the item is an element in the array. If search is a string, this function returns true if the item is a substring of search. This function is not case sensitive. Casts values to a string.

    Example using a string:
    contains('Hello world', 'llo') returns true.

    Example using an object filter:
    contains(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, 'bug') returns true if the issue related to the event has a label "bug".

    Example matching an array of strings:
    Instead of writing github.event_name == "push" || github.event_name == "pull_request", you can use contains() with fromJSON() to check if an array of strings contains an item. For example, contains(fromJSON('["push", "pull_request"]'), github.event_name) returns true if github.event_name is "push" or "pull_request".
    ▶️ startsWith
    startsWith( searchString, searchValue )

    Returns true when searchString starts with searchValue. This function is not case sensitive. Casts values to a string.

    Example:
    startsWith('Hello world', 'He') returns true.
    ◀️ endsWith
    endsWith( searchString, searchValue )

    Returns true if searchString ends with searchValue. This function is not case sensitive. Casts values to a string.

    Example:
    endsWith('Hello world', 'ld') returns true.
    📝 format
    format( string, replaceValue0, replaceValue1, ..., replaceValueN)

    Replaces values in the string, with the variable replaceValueN. Variables in the string are specified using the {N} syntax, where N is an integer. You must specify at least one replaceValue and string. There is no maximum for the number of variables (replaceValueN) you can use. Escape curly braces using double braces.

    Example of format:
    format('Hello {0} {1} {2}', 'Mona', 'the', 'Octocat')
    Returns 'Hello Mona the Octocat'.

    Example escaping braces:
    format('{{Hello {0} {1} {2}!}}', 'Mona', 'the', 'Octocat')
    Returns '{Hello Mona the Octocat!}'.
    🔗 join
    join( array, optionalSeparator )

    The value for array can be an array or a string. All values in array are concatenated into a string. If you provide optionalSeparator, it is inserted between the concatenated values. Otherwise, the default separator , is used. Casts values to a string.

    Example:
    join(github.event.issue.labels.*.name, ', ') may return 'bug, help wanted'
    📤 toJSON
    toJSON(value)

    Returns a pretty-print JSON representation of value. You can use this function to debug the information provided in contexts.

    Example:
    toJSON(job) might return { "status": "success" }
    📥 fromJSON
    fromJSON(value)

    Returns a JSON object or JSON data type for value. You can use this function to provide a JSON object as an evaluated expression or to convert any data type that can be represented in JSON or JavaScript, such as strings, booleans, null values, arrays, and objects.

    Example returning a JSON object:
    This workflow sets a JSON matrix in one job, and passes it to the next job using an output and fromJSON. 
    name: build
on: push
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      matrix: ${{ steps.set-matrix.outputs.matrix }}
    steps:
      - id: set-matrix
        run: echo "matrix={\"include\":[{\"project\":\"foo\",\"config\":\"Debug\"},{\"project\":\"bar\",\"config\":\"Release\"}]}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
  job2:
    needs: job1
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix: ${{ fromJSON(needs.job1.outputs.matrix) }}
    steps:
      - run: echo "Matrix - Project ${{ matrix.project }}, Config ${{ matrix.config }}"
    Example returning a JSON data type:
    This workflow uses fromJSON to convert environment variables from a string to a Boolean or integer. 
    name: print
on: push
env:
  continue: true
  time: 3
jobs:
  job1:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - continue-on-error: ${{ fromJSON(env.continue) }}
        timeout-minutes: ${{ fromJSON(env.time) }}
        run: echo ...
    The workflow uses the fromJSON() function to convert the environment variable continue from a string to a boolean, allowing it to determine whether to continue-on-error or not. Similarly, it converts the time environment variable from a string to an integer, setting the timeout for the job in minutes.
    #️⃣ hashFiles
    hashFiles(path)

    Returns a single hash for the set of files that matches the path pattern. You can provide a single path pattern or multiple path patterns separated by commas. The path is relative to the GITHUB_WORKSPACE directory and can only include files inside of the GITHUB_WORKSPACE. This function calculates an individual SHA-256 hash for each matched file, and then uses those hashes to calculate a final SHA-256 hash for the set of files. If the path pattern does not match any files, this returns an empty string. For more information about SHA-256, see SHA-2.

    You can use pattern matching characters to match file names. Pattern matching for hashFiles follows glob pattern matching and is case-insensitive on Windows. For more information about supported pattern matching characters, see the Patterns section in the @actions/glob documentation.

    Examples with a single pattern:
    • Matches any package-lock.json file in the repository: hashFiles('**/package-lock.json')
    • Matches all .js files in the src directory at root level, but ignores any subdirectories of src: hashFiles('/src/*.js')
    • Matches all .rb files in the lib directory at root level, including any subdirectories of lib: hashFiles('/lib/**/*.rb')

    Examples with multiple patterns:
    • Creates a hash for any package-lock.json and Gemfile.lock files in the repository: hashFiles('**/package-lock.json', '**/Gemfile.lock')
    • Creates a hash for all .rb files in the lib directory at root level, including any subdirectories of lib, but excluding .rb files in the foo subdirectory: hashFiles('/lib/**/*.rb', '!/lib/foo/*.rb')

    Syntax

    # String functions
${{ contains(value, searchString) }}
${{ startsWith(value, searchString) }}
${{ endsWith(value, searchString) }}

# Formatting
${{ format('pattern {0} {1}', arg0, arg1) }}

# JSON operations
${{ toJSON(value) }}
${{ fromJSON(jsonString) }}

# Array operations
${{ join(array, separator) }}

# Hash generation
${{ hashFiles('path/pattern') }}

    Parameters description

    String functions:
    • contains(haystack, needle) - Returns true if haystack contains needle (case-insensitive)
    • startsWith(string, prefix) - Returns true if string starts with prefix (case-insensitive)
    • endsWith(string, suffix) - Returns true if string ends with suffix (case-insensitive)
    Formatting:
    • format(template, ...args) - Format string with placeholders {0}, {1}, etc.
    JSON:
    • toJSON(value) - Convert value to JSON string
    • fromJSON(json) - Parse JSON string to object
    Array:
    • join(array, separator) - Join array elements with separator
    Hashing:
    • hashFiles(pattern) - Generate SHA-256 hash of files matching pattern

    Return value

    Functions return values appropriate to their operation: boolean for comparison functions, string for formatting and JSON operations, hash string for hashFiles.

    Examples

    1. JobPost string functions

    name: JobPost String Functions

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  string-operations:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Check commit message contains skip
        if: contains(github.event.head_commit.message, '[skip ci]')
        run: echo "⏭️ JobPost CI skipped per commit message"
      
      - name: Check if feature branch
        if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/heads/feature/')
        run: |
          echo "🌿 JobPost feature branch detected"
          echo "Branch: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
      
      - name: Check if hotfix branch
        if: startsWith(github.ref_name, 'hotfix-')
        run: echo "🔥 JobPost hotfix branch"
      
      - name: Check Python files changed
        if: contains(github.event.head_commit.message, '.py')
        run: |
          echo "🐍 Python changes detected in JobPost"
          echo "Running Python-specific tests"
      
      - name: Check if release tag
        if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')
        run: |
          echo "🏷️ JobPost release tag detected"
          TAG="${{ github.ref_name }}"
          echo "Release version: $TAG"
    🔤 JobPost string functions: contains, startsWith, endsWith

    2. JobPost format function

    name: JobPost Format

on: [push]

jobs:
  formatting:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Format JobPost deployment message
        run: |
          MESSAGE="${{ format('Deploying JobPost {0} to {1} environment', vars.JOBPOST_VERSION, vars.ENVIRONMENT) }}"
          echo "$MESSAGE"
      
      - name: Format JobPost build info
        run: |
          BUILD_INFO="${{ format('JobPost build #{0} - commit {1} by {2}', github.run_number, github.sha, github.actor) }}"
          echo "$BUILD_INFO"
      
      - name: Format JobPost error message
        if: failure()
        run: |
          ERROR="${{ format('❌ JobPost deployment failed on {0} at {1}', runner.os, github.ref_name) }}"
          echo "$ERROR"
      
      - name: Format JobPost notification
        run: |
          # Multiple placeholders with different types
          NOTIFY="${{ format('JobPost {0}: {1} files changed in commit {2}', github.event_name, github.event.commits[0].modified.length, github.sha) }}"
          echo "$NOTIFY"
      
      - name: Format JobPost connection string
        env:
          CONNECTION: ${{ format('postgresql://{0}:{1}@{2}:{3}/{4}', vars.DB_USER, secrets.DB_PASSWORD, vars.DB_HOST, vars.DB_PORT, vars.DB_NAME) }}
        run: |
          echo "JobPost database connection configured"
          # Connection string is masked in logs if it contains secrets
    📝 JobPost format function for string templates

    3. JobPost JSON functions

    name: JobPost JSON Operations

on: [push]

jobs:
  json-operations:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Convert JobPost config to JSON
        run: |
          # Create JobPost config object and convert to JSON
          CONFIG='${{ toJSON(vars) }}'
          echo "JobPost configuration:"
          echo "$CONFIG" | jq '.'
      
      - name: Parse JobPost settings JSON
        env:
          # Assume JOBPOST_SETTINGS contains JSON string
          SETTINGS: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_SETTINGS_JSON }}
        run: |
          # Parse JSON in workflow
          DB_HOST='${{ fromJSON(vars.JOBPOST_SETTINGS_JSON).database.host }}'
          CACHE_ENABLED='${{ fromJSON(vars.JOBPOST_SETTINGS_JSON).cache.enabled }}'
          
          echo "JobPost DB Host: $DB_HOST"
          echo "Cache enabled: $CACHE_ENABLED"
      
      - name: Convert JobPost context to JSON
        run: |
          # Convert entire github context to JSON
          GITHUB_JSON='${{ toJSON(github) }}'
          echo "$GITHUB_JSON" > github-context.json
          
          echo "JobPost workflow context saved"
          echo "Repository: $(echo "$GITHUB_JSON" | jq -r '.repository')"
          echo "Event: $(echo "$GITHUB_JSON" | jq -r '.event_name')"
      
      - name: Matrix configuration JSON
        strategy:
          matrix:
            config:
              - { env: 'dev', workers: 2 }
              - { env: 'prod', workers: 8 }
        run: |
          # Access matrix as JSON
          ENV='${{ matrix.config.env }}'
          WORKERS='${{ matrix.config.workers }}'
          
          echo "JobPost environment: $ENV"
          echo "Workers: $WORKERS"
    📦 JobPost JSON conversion: toJSON and fromJSON

    4. JobPost join and array functions

    name: JobPost Array Operations

on: [push]

jobs:
  array-operations:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Join JobPost test commands
        run: |
          # Join array of test paths
          TEST_PATHS="${{ join(github.event.commits.*.modified, ', ') }}"
          echo "Modified files: $TEST_PATHS"
      
      - name: Join JobPost environments
        env:
          # Create comma-separated list
          ENVIRONMENTS: ${{ join(fromJSON('["dev", "staging", "production"]'), ', ') }}
        run: |
          echo "JobPost environments: $ENVIRONMENTS"
      
      - name: Build JobPost tag list
        run: |
          # Join labels with separator
          TAGS="${{ join(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, ' | ') }}"
          echo "JobPost PR tags: $TAGS"
      
      - name: Create JobPost deployment command
        run: |
          # Join command arguments
          ARGS="${{ join(fromJSON('["--no-cache", "--verbose", "--production"]'), ' ') }}"
          echo "Deploy args: $ARGS"
          # python manage.py deploy $ARGS
      
      - name: Format JobPost notification with joined data
        run: |
          CHANGED_FILES="${{ join(github.event.commits[0].added, '\n  - ') }}"
          echo "📝 JobPost files added:"
          echo "  - $CHANGED_FILES"
    🔗 JobPost join function for array operations

    5. JobPost hashFiles for cache keys

    name: JobPost Hash Files

on: [push]

jobs:
  caching:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Cache JobPost dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v3
        with:
          path: |
            env/
            ~/.cache/pip
          # Generate cache key based on requirements file hash
          key: jobpost-deps-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('requirements.txt') }}
          restore-keys: |
            jobpost-deps-${{ runner.os }}-
      
      - name: Cache JobPost multiple files
        uses: actions/cache@v3
        with:
          path: node_modules/
          # Hash multiple files with glob pattern
          key: jobpost-npm-${{ hashFiles('package*.json') }}
      
      - name: Cache JobPost with nested patterns
        uses: actions/cache@v3
        with:
          path: .pytest_cache/
          # Hash all Python files for test cache
          key: jobpost-tests-${{ hashFiles('**/*.py') }}
      
      - name: Display JobPost file hashes
        run: |
          echo "🔑 JobPost Cache Keys:"
          echo "Requirements hash: ${{ hashFiles('requirements.txt') }}"
          echo "Python files hash: ${{ hashFiles('**/*.py') }}"
          echo "Config hash: ${{ hashFiles('anihpj/settings.py', 'manage.py') }}"
      
      - name: Conditional rebuild based on hash
        run: |
          CURRENT_HASH="${{ hashFiles('requirements.txt') }}"
          echo "Current requirements hash: $CURRENT_HASH"
          
          # Save hash for comparison in future runs
          echo "$CURRENT_HASH" > .requirements-hash
          echo "JobPost dependencies hash saved"
    🔐 JobPost hashFiles function for cache key generation

    Status check functions

    Docs: Status check functions

    You can use the following status check functions as expressions in if conditionals. A default status check of success() is applied unless you include one of these functions.

    Full description

    Status check functions allow you to control JobPost workflow execution based on the outcome of previous jobs and steps. These functions return boolean values and are essential for implementing error handling, cleanup tasks, and conditional deployments. The main status functions are success(), always(), cancelled(), and failure(). They are evaluated at different points in the workflow lifecycle and can be combined with logical operators for complex conditional logic. Understanding when each status function returns true is crucial for reliable workflow design.

    📌 Default Behavior:
    If you don't provide a status check function, the expression is automatically wrapped with success(). For example, if: env.MY_VAR is equivalent to if: success() && env.MY_VAR. This means steps skip by default if previous steps failed or were cancelled.

    Official Status Check Functions

    ✅ success
    success()

    Returns true when none of the previous steps have failed or been cancelled.

    Example: 
    steps:
  ...
  - name: The job has succeeded
    if: ${{ success() }}
    ...
    🔄 always
    always()

    Causes the step to always execute, and returns true, even when cancelled. The always expression is best used at the step level or on tasks that you expect to run even when a job is cancelled. For example, you can use always to send logs even when a job is cancelled.

    Example: 
    if: ${{ always() }}
    ⚠️ Avoid using always for critical failures
    Avoid using always for tasks that handle secrets or sensitive data, because attackers could run malicious code even when the workflow is cancelled. GitHub will cancel workflow jobs when runners lose communication with GitHub or when the runner's Runner.Worker process is forcefully terminated. In these cases, the job and steps with if: always() conditions will not run.
    🚫 cancelled
    cancelled()

    Returns true if the workflow was cancelled.

    Example: 
    if: ${{ cancelled() }}
    ❌ failure
    failure()

    Returns true when any previous step of a job fails. If you have a chain of dependent jobs, failure() returns true if any ancestor job fails.

    Example: 
    steps:
  ...
  - name: The job has failed
    if: ${{ failure() }}
    ...
    Example with conditions: 
    steps:
  ...
  - name: Failing step
    id: demo
    if: ${{ failure() && steps.demo.conclusion == 'failure' }}
    ...

    Examples

    1. JobPost success and failure handling

    name: JobPost Status Checks

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Run JobPost tests
        id: tests
        run: |
          echo "Running JobPost test suite"
          python manage.py test jobpost
      
      - name: Deploy on success
        # Only runs if all previous steps succeeded (default behavior)
        if: success()
        run: |
          echo "✅ JobPost tests passed"
          echo "Deploying JobPost to staging"
          python manage.py deploy --staging
      
      - name: Notify on failure
        # Runs only if any previous step failed
        if: failure()
        run: |
          echo "❌ JobPost tests failed"
          curl -X POST ${{ vars.WEBHOOK_URL }} \
               -d '{"status": "failed", "project": "JobPost"}'
      
      - name: Report test results
        # Runs whether tests passed or failed (but not if cancelled)
        if: success() || failure()
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost test report"
          echo "Status: ${{ job.status }}"
          python scripts/generate_report.py
    ✅ JobPost workflow with success and failure conditionals

    2. JobPost always runs for cleanup

    name: JobPost Cleanup

on: [push]

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup JobPost environment
        run: |
          echo "Setting up JobPost test environment"
          docker-compose up -d postgres redis
          sleep 5
      
      - name: Run JobPost migrations
        run: python manage.py migrate
      
      - name: Run JobPost tests
        run: python manage.py test jobpost
      
      - name: Cleanup test environment
        # Always runs, even if previous steps failed or workflow cancelled
        if: always()
        run: |
          echo "🧹 Cleaning up JobPost test environment"
          docker-compose down
          rm -rf test-data/
          echo "Cleanup complete"
      
      - name: Upload test logs
        # Runs on both success and failure, but not if cancelled
        if: success() || failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: jobpost-test-logs
          path: logs/
      
      - name: Archive test database
        # Only runs if tests actually executed (not cancelled early)
        if: "!cancelled()"
        run: |
          echo "📦 Archiving JobPost test database"
          pg_dump jobpost_test > test-db-backup.sql
    🧹 JobPost always() function ensures cleanup runs

    3. JobPost cancelled workflow handling

    name: JobPost Cancellation

on: [push]

jobs:
  long-running-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Start JobPost integration tests
        run: |
          echo "🚀 Starting JobPost integration tests (may take 30+ minutes)"
          python manage.py test --tag=integration
      
      - name: Report cancellation
        # Runs only if workflow was cancelled
        if: cancelled()
        run: |
          echo "⚠️ JobPost integration tests were cancelled"
          curl -X POST ${{ vars.WEBHOOK_URL }} \
               -d '{"status": "cancelled", "job": "integration-tests"}'
      
      - name: Cleanup resources
        # Runs if cancelled or failed, but not on success
        if: cancelled() || failure()
        run: |
          echo "🛑 JobPost workflow interrupted"
          echo "Cleaning up resources..."
          docker-compose down
          pkill -f "python manage.py"
      
      - name: Success notification
        # Only runs if not cancelled and all succeeded
        if: success() && !cancelled()
        run: |
          echo "✅ JobPost integration tests completed successfully"
      
      - name: Final cleanup
        # Runs no matter what - success, failure, or cancellation
        if: always()
        run: |
          echo "🔚 JobPost workflow ending"
          echo "Performing final cleanup"
          rm -rf /tmp/jobpost-*
    🛑 JobPost cancelled() function for interruption handling

    4. JobPost complex status combinations

    name: JobPost Complex Status

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Build JobPost
        id: build
        run: python setup.py build
      
      - name: Run tests
        id: tests
        run: python manage.py test jobpost
      
      - name: Deploy to staging
        # Deploy only if build and tests both succeeded
        if: success()
        run: echo "Deploying JobPost to staging"
      
      - name: Rollback on test failure
        # Rollback if tests failed but build succeeded
        if: failure() && steps.build.outcome == 'success'
        run: |
          echo "⚠️ JobPost tests failed, rolling back"
          python scripts/rollback.py --to-previous
      
      - name: Investigate build failure
        # Special handling if build itself failed
        if: failure() && steps.build.outcome == 'failure'
        run: |
          echo "❌ JobPost build failed"
          echo "Build logs:"
          cat build.log
      
      - name: Slack notification
        # Notify on failure or cancellation, but not success
        if: failure() || cancelled()
        run: |
          STATUS="${{ job.status }}"
          curl -X POST ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }} \
               -d "{\"text\": \"JobPost workflow $STATUS: ${{ github.ref_name }}\"}"
      
      - name: Archive artifacts
        # Archive on success or failure (not cancellation)
        if: "!cancelled()"
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: jobpost-build
          path: dist/
    🔀 JobPost complex status function combinations

    5. JobPost status-based deployment strategy

    name: JobPost Deployment Strategy

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      test_status: ${{ job.status }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run JobPost tests
        run: python manage.py test jobpost
  
  deploy-staging:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Deploy to staging only if tests succeeded
    if: success()
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost to staging
        run: |
          echo "✅ Tests passed - deploying JobPost to staging"
          python manage.py deploy --env=staging
  
  deploy-production:
    needs: [test, deploy-staging]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Deploy to production only if both previous jobs succeeded
    if: success() && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost to production
        run: |
          echo "🚀 Deploying JobPost to production"
          python manage.py deploy --env=production
  
  notify:
    needs: [test, deploy-staging, deploy-production]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Always notify, regardless of outcome
    if: always()
    steps:
      - name: Send JobPost deployment notification
        run: |
          echo "📧 JobPost Deployment Summary"
          echo "Test: ${{ needs.test.result }}"
          echo "Staging: ${{ needs.deploy-staging.result }}"
          echo "Production: ${{ needs.deploy-production.result }}"
          
          # Send to notification service
          python scripts/notify.py \
            --test="${{ needs.test.result }}" \
            --staging="${{ needs.deploy-staging.result }}" \
            --production="${{ needs.deploy-production.result }}"
  
  cleanup:
    needs: [test, deploy-staging, deploy-production]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Cleanup runs if any job failed or was cancelled
    if: failure() || cancelled()
    steps:
      - name: Cleanup failed JobPost deployment
        run: |
          echo "🧹 Cleaning up failed JobPost deployment"
          python scripts/cleanup_failed_deployment.py
    🎯 JobPost multi-job deployment with status-based strategy

    Object filters

    Docs: Object filters

    You can use the * syntax to apply a filter and select matching items in a collection.

    Full description

    Object filters provide powerful array and object manipulation capabilities within GitHub Actions expressions. The * (star) filter extracts properties from all objects in an array, creating a new array of those property values. This is particularly useful for JobPost workflows when processing multiple items like commits, pull requests, labels, or matrix configurations. Filters can be chained and combined with other expression functions like join() and contains() for complex data transformations. Understanding object filters enables cleaner, more maintainable workflow code.

    Official Object Filter Documentation

    ⭐ Star Filter Syntax:
    Use the * character to apply a filter and select matching items in a collection. This creates a new array containing the selected property from each object.
    📚 Official Example - Array Filter:
    For example, consider an array of objects named fruits:
    [
  { "name": "apple", "quantity": 1 },
  { "name": "orange", "quantity": 2 },
  { "name": "pear", "quantity": 1 }
]
    The filter fruits.*.name returns the array [ "apple", "orange", "pear" ].
    🥗 Official Example - Object Filter:
    You can also use the * syntax on an object. For example, suppose you have an object named vegetables:
    {
  "scallions": {
    "colors": ["green", "white", "red"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stalks"],
  },
  "beets": {
    "colors": ["purple", "red", "gold", "white", "pink"],
    "ediblePortions": ["roots", "stems", "leaves"],
  },
  "artichokes": {
    "colors": ["green", "purple", "red", "black"],
    "ediblePortions": ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"],
  },
}
    The filter vegetables.*.ediblePortions could evaluate to: 
    [
  ["roots", "stalks"],
  ["hearts", "stems", "leaves"],
  ["roots", "stems", "leaves"],
]
    ⚠️ Object Order Not Guaranteed
    Since objects don't preserve order, the order of the output cannot be guaranteed to be the same as the order of the input in the vegetables object. The resulting array might appear in any order.

    Examples

    1. JobPost commit message filtering

    name: JobPost Commit Filters

on: [push]

jobs:
  check-commits:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Extract all JobPost commit messages
        run: |
          # Get all commit messages as JSON array
          MESSAGES='${{ toJSON(github.event.commits.*.message) }}'
          echo "JobPost commit messages:"
          echo "$MESSAGES" | jq -r '.[]'
      
      - name: Check if any commit skips CI
        if: contains(join(github.event.commits.*.message, ' '), '[skip ci]')
        run: |
          echo "⏭️ Skipping JobPost CI - found [skip ci] in commits"
          exit 0
      
      - name: Extract JobPost commit authors
        run: |
          # Get all author names
          AUTHORS='${{ join(github.event.commits.*.author.name, ', ') }}'
          echo "JobPost commits by: $AUTHORS"
      
      - name: List all modified files
        run: |
          # Extract modified files from all commits
          echo "📝 JobPost files modified in this push:"
          MODIFIED='${{ toJSON(github.event.commits.*.modified) }}'
          echo "$MODIFIED" | jq -r '.[][] | select(. != null)'
      
      - name: Check for Python changes
        run: |
          # Check if any Python files were modified
          FILES='${{ join(github.event.commits.*.modified, ' ') }}'
          if echo "$FILES" | grep -q "\.py$"; then
            echo "🐍 Python files changed in JobPost"
            echo "Running Python tests..."
            python manage.py test jobpost
          else
            echo "No Python changes in JobPost"
          fi
    📋 JobPost commit data extraction using object filters

    2. JobPost pull request label filtering

    name: JobPost PR Labels

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, labeled, unlabeled]

jobs:
  check-labels:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: List all JobPost PR labels
        run: |
          # Extract all label names
          LABELS='${{ join(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, ', ') }}'
          echo "JobPost PR labels: $LABELS"
      
      - name: Check for JobPost enhancement label
        if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'enhancement')
        run: |
          echo "✨ JobPost enhancement PR detected"
          echo "Applying enhancement review checklist"
      
      - name: Check for JobPost bugfix label
        if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'bug')
        run: |
          echo "🐛 JobPost bugfix PR detected"
          echo "Running regression tests"
      
      - name: Require JobPost security review
        if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'security')
        run: |
          echo "🔒 JobPost security-related PR"
          echo "Security review required before merge"
          # Could add status check here
      
      - name: Auto-deploy JobPost preview
        if: |
          contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'preview') &&
          !contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'do-not-deploy')
        run: |
          echo "🚀 Deploying JobPost preview environment"
          PR_NUMBER="${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
          python manage.py deploy --preview="pr-$PR_NUMBER"
    🏷️ JobPost PR label filtering with object filters

    3. JobPost matrix value extraction

    name: JobPost Matrix Filters

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ['3.9', '3.10', '3.11', '3.12']
        database: ['postgresql', 'mysql']
        include:
          - python-version: '3.12'
            database: 'postgresql'
            experimental: true
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup Python ${{ matrix.python-version }}
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      
      - name: Display JobPost test matrix
        run: |
          echo "🧪 JobPost Test Matrix"
          echo "Python: ${{ matrix.python-version }}"
          echo "Database: ${{ matrix.database }}"
          echo "Experimental: ${{ matrix.experimental || false }}"
      
      - name: Run JobPost tests
        run: |
          echo "Testing JobPost with Python ${{ matrix.python-version }}"
          python manage.py test jobpost --database=${{ matrix.database }}
  
  summary:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: JobPost test summary
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost tested across multiple configurations"
          echo "Python versions: ${{ join(strategy.matrix.python-version, ', ') }}"
          echo "Databases: ${{ join(strategy.matrix.database, ', ') }}"
    🔢 JobPost matrix configuration with object filters

    4. JobPost file change detection

    name: JobPost File Changes

on: [push]

jobs:
  detect-changes:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 2
      
      - name: Detect JobPost Python changes
        id: changes
        run: |
          # Extract all modified files from commits
          MODIFIED='${{ toJSON(github.event.commits.*.modified) }}'
          ADDED='${{ toJSON(github.event.commits.*.added) }}'
          
          echo "Modified files:"
          echo "$MODIFIED" | jq -r '.[][] | select(. != null)'
          
          # Check for specific file types
          if echo "$MODIFIED" | grep -q "jobpost.*\.py"; then
            echo "jobpost_changed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi
          
          if echo "$MODIFIED" | grep -q "requirements\.txt"; then
            echo "deps_changed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi
          
          if echo "$MODIFIED" | grep -q "migrations/"; then
            echo "migrations_changed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi
      
      - name: Run JobPost app tests
        if: steps.changes.outputs.jobpost_changed == 'true'
        run: |
          echo "🧪 JobPost app code changed, running tests"
          python manage.py test jobpost
      
      - name: Update JobPost dependencies
        if: steps.changes.outputs.deps_changed == 'true'
        run: |
          echo "📦 JobPost dependencies changed"
          pip install -r requirements.txt
      
      - name: Run JobPost migrations
        if: steps.changes.outputs.migrations_changed == 'true'
        run: |
          echo "🔄 JobPost migrations detected"
          python manage.py migrate --check
      
      - name: List all JobPost authors
        run: |
          # Get unique list of commit authors
          AUTHORS='${{ toJSON(github.event.commits.*.author.name) }}'
          echo "👥 JobPost contributors in this push:"
          echo "$AUTHORS" | jq -r '.[] | select(. != null)' | sort -u
    🔍 JobPost file change detection with object filters

    5. JobPost complex filter combinations

    name: JobPost Complex Filters

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]

jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Extract JobPost commit metadata
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost PR Commit Analysis"
          echo ""
          
          # All commit messages
          MESSAGES='${{ join(github.event.commits.*.message, '\n  - ') }}'
          echo "Commit messages:"
          echo "  - $MESSAGES"
          echo ""
          
          # All authors
          AUTHORS='${{ join(github.event.commits.*.author.name, ', ') }}'
          echo "Authors: $AUTHORS"
          echo ""
          
          # All author emails
          EMAILS='${{ toJSON(github.event.commits.*.author.email) }}'
          echo "Author emails:"
          echo "$EMAILS" | jq -r '.[]'
          echo ""
      
      - name: Check JobPost PR labels and assignees
        run: |
          # Extract PR labels
          LABELS='${{ toJSON(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name) }}'
          echo "🏷️ JobPost PR Labels:"
          echo "$LABELS" | jq -r '.[]'
          echo ""
          
          # Extract assignees
          ASSIGNEES='${{ toJSON(github.event.pull_request.assignees.*.login) }}'
          echo "👤 JobPost PR Assignees:"
          echo "$ASSIGNEES" | jq -r '.[]'
          echo ""
      
      - name: Combined JobPost filter checks
        run: |
          # Check multiple conditions using filters
          LABELS='${{ join(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, ',') }}'
          MESSAGES='${{ join(github.event.commits.*.message, ' ') }}'
          
          # Check if breaking change
          if echo "$LABELS" | grep -q "breaking"; then
            echo "⚠️ JobPost breaking change detected"
          fi
          
          # Check if hotfix
          if echo "$LABELS" | grep -q "hotfix"; then
            echo "🔥 JobPost hotfix - expedited review"
          fi
          
          # Check commit messages for keywords
          if echo "$MESSAGES" | grep -qi "fix\|bug"; then
            echo "🐛 JobPost bugfix detected in commits"
          fi
          
          if echo "$MESSAGES" | grep -qi "feature\|add"; then
            echo "✨ JobPost new feature detected in commits"
          fi
      
      - name: Generate JobPost PR summary
        run: |
          # Create comprehensive PR summary using filters
          cat << EOF > pr-summary.md
          # JobPost PR Summary
          
          ## Metadata
          - **PR Number:** ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          - **Title:** ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}
          - **Author:** ${{ github.event.pull_request.user.login }}
          - **Labels:** ${{ join(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, ', ') }}
          
          ## Commits
          ${{ join(github.event.commits.*.message, '\n') }}
          
          ## Contributors
          ${{ join(github.event.commits.*.author.name, ', ') }}
          EOF
          
          cat pr-summary.md
    🎯 JobPost complex object filter combinations and data extraction

    Available contexts

    Docs: GitHub Actions contexts

    Find information about contexts available in GitHub Actions workflows, including available properties, access methods, and usage examples.

    Full description

    Contexts are a way to access information about workflow runs, runner environments, jobs, and steps in GitHub Actions. Each context is an object that contains properties you can access using expression syntax ${{ context.property }}. For JobPost workflows, contexts provide essential data like repository information, commit details, environment variables, job status, and more. Understanding which context to use and when is crucial for building dynamic, responsive workflows. Different contexts are available at different points in the workflow execution.

    Official Contexts Reference Table

    Context name Type Description
    github object Information about the workflow run. For more information, see github context.
    env object Contains variables set in a workflow, job, or step. For more information, see env context.
    vars object Contains variables set at the repository, organization, or environment levels. For more information, see vars context.
    job object Information about the currently running job. For more information, see job context.
    jobs object For reusable workflows only, contains outputs of jobs from the reusable workflow. For more information, see jobs context.
    steps object Information about the steps that have been run in the current job. For more information, see steps context.
    runner object Information about the runner that is running the current job. For more information, see runner context.
    secrets object Contains the names and values of secrets that are available to a workflow run. For more information, see secrets context.
    strategy object Information about the matrix execution strategy for the current job. For more information, see strategy context.
    matrix object Contains the matrix properties defined in the workflow that apply to the current job. For more information, see matrix context.
    needs object Contains the outputs of all jobs that are defined as a dependency of the current job. For more information, see needs context.
    inputs object Contains the inputs of a reusable or manually triggered workflow. For more information, see inputs context.

    Context Access Syntax

    📝 Two Ways to Access Context Information:

    As part of an expression, you can access context information using one of two syntaxes:

    • Index syntax: github['sha']
    • Property dereference syntax: github.sha

    In order to use property dereference syntax, the property name must start with a letter or _ and contain only alphanumeric characters, -, or _.

    If you attempt to dereference a nonexistent property, it will evaluate to an empty string.

    Determining when to use contexts

    Contexts vs Default Variables

    GitHub Actions includes a collection of variables called contexts and a similar collection of variables called default variables. These variables are intended for use at different points in the workflow:

    Default environment variables: These environment variables exist only on the runner that is executing your job. For more information, see Variables reference.

    Contexts: You can use most contexts at any point in your workflow, including when default variables would be unavailable. For example, you can use contexts with expressions to perform initial processing before the job is routed to a runner for execution; this allows you to use a context with the conditional if keyword to determine whether a step should run. Once the job is running, you can also retrieve context variables from the runner that is executing the job, such as runner.os. For details of where you can use various contexts within a workflow, see Context availability.

    Example demonstrating the difference: 
    name: CI
on: push
jobs:
  prod-check:
    if: ${{ github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: echo "Deploying to production server on branch $GITHUB_REF"
    In this example, the if statement checks the github.ref context to determine the current branch name; if the name is refs/heads/main, then the subsequent steps are executed. The if check is processed by GitHub Actions, and the job is only sent to the runner if the result is true. Once the job is sent to the runner, the step is executed and refers to the $GITHUB_REF variable from the runner.

    Context availability

    ⚠️ Context Availability Restrictions

    Different contexts are available throughout a workflow run. For example, the secrets context may only be used at certain places within a job.

    In addition, some functions may only be used in certain places. For example, the hashFiles function is not available everywhere.

    The following table lists the restrictions on where each context and special function can be used within a workflow. The listed contexts are only available for the given workflow key, and may not be used anywhere else. Unless listed below, a function can be used anywhere.
    Workflow key Context Special functions
    run-name github, inputs, vars None
    concurrency github, inputs, vars None
    env github, secrets, inputs, vars None
    jobs.<job_id>.concurrency github, needs, strategy, matrix, inputs, vars None
    jobs.<job_id>.container github, needs, strategy, matrix, vars, inputs None
    jobs.<job_id>.container.credentials github, needs, strategy, matrix, env, vars, secrets, inputs None
    jobs.<job_id>.container.env.<env_id> github, needs, strategy, matrix, job, runner, env, vars, secrets, inputs None
    jobs.<job_id>.if github, needs, vars, inputs always, cancelled, success, failure
    jobs.<job_id>.outputs.<output_id> github, needs, strategy, matrix, job, runner, env, vars, secrets, steps, inputs None
    jobs.<job_id>.runs-on github, needs, strategy, matrix, vars, inputs None
    jobs.<job_id>.steps.continue-on-error github, needs, strategy, matrix, job, runner, env, vars, secrets, steps, inputs hashFiles
    jobs.<job_id>.steps.if github, needs, strategy, matrix, job, runner, env, vars, steps, inputs always, cancelled, success, failure, hashFiles
    jobs.<job_id>.steps.run github, needs, strategy, matrix, job, runner, env, vars, secrets, steps, inputs hashFiles
    jobs.<job_id>.steps.with github, needs, strategy, matrix, job, runner, env, vars, secrets, steps, inputs hashFiles
    on.workflow_call.inputs.<inputs_id>.default github, inputs, vars None
    on.workflow_call.outputs.<output_id>.value github, jobs, vars, inputs None

    Example: printing context information to the log

    ⚠️ Security Warning
    When using the whole github context, be mindful that it includes sensitive information such as github.token. GitHub masks secrets when they are printed to the console, but you should be cautious when exporting or printing the context.
    Debugging with toJSON()

    You can print the contents of contexts to the log for debugging. The toJSON function is required to pretty-print JSON objects to the log.

    Official debugging example: 
    name: Context testing
on: push

jobs:
  dump_contexts_to_log:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Dump GitHub context
        env:
          GITHUB_CONTEXT: ${{ toJSON(github) }}
        run: echo "$GITHUB_CONTEXT"
      - name: Dump job context
        env:
          JOB_CONTEXT: ${{ toJSON(job) }}
        run: echo "$JOB_CONTEXT"
      - name: Dump steps context
        env:
          STEPS_CONTEXT: ${{ toJSON(steps) }}
        run: echo "$STEPS_CONTEXT"
      - name: Dump runner context
        env:
          RUNNER_CONTEXT: ${{ toJSON(runner) }}
        run: echo "$RUNNER_CONTEXT"
      - name: Dump strategy context
        env:
          STRATEGY_CONTEXT: ${{ toJSON(strategy) }}
        run: echo "$STRATEGY_CONTEXT"
      - name: Dump matrix context
        env:
          MATRIX_CONTEXT: ${{ toJSON(matrix) }}
        run: echo "$MATRIX_CONTEXT"

    JobPost Examples

    1. JobPost using multiple contexts

    name: JobPost Multi-Context

on: [push]

env:
  JOBPOST_VERSION: "2.0.0"

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Display JobPost contexts
        run: |
          echo "🔍 JobPost Context Information"
          echo ""
          echo "📦 GitHub Context:"
          echo "  Repository: ${{ github.repository }}"
          echo "  Branch: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
          echo "  Commit: ${{ github.sha }}"
          echo "  Actor: ${{ github.actor }}"
          echo ""
          echo "🌍 Environment:"
          echo "  JOBPOST_VERSION: ${{ env.JOBPOST_VERSION }}"
          echo ""
          echo "⚙️ Configuration:"
          echo "  API URL: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
          echo ""
          echo "🖥️ Runner:"
          echo "  OS: ${{ runner.os }}"
          echo "  Arch: ${{ runner.arch }}"
          echo ""
          echo "💼 Job:"
          echo "  Status: ${{ job.status }}"
    📋 JobPost workflow accessing multiple contexts

    2. JobPost context availability by location

    name: JobPost Context Availability

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        description: 'Deployment environment'
        required: true
        default: 'staging'

env:
  # ✅ Available: github, vars, inputs, secrets
  JOBPOST_API: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}
  DEPLOY_ENV: ${{ inputs.environment }}

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # ✅ Available in job-level if: github, vars, needs, inputs
    if: inputs.environment == 'production' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost
        # ✅ Available in steps: all contexts
        run: |
          echo "Deploying JobPost to ${{ inputs.environment }}"
          echo "API: ${{ vars.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
          echo "From: ${{ github.repository }}"
          echo "Runner: ${{ runner.os }}"
    🎯 JobPost context availability at different workflow levels

    3. JobPost context in conditionals

    name: JobPost Conditionals

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Use github context in job conditional
    if: github.event_name == 'push' || github.event.pull_request.draft == false
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Run JobPost tests
        # Use multiple contexts in step conditional
        if: runner.os == 'Linux' && vars.JOBPOST_TESTS_ENABLED == 'true'
        run: python manage.py test jobpost
      
      - name: Deploy on main branch
        # Combine contexts with logical operators
        if: |
          success() && 
          github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && 
          github.event_name == 'push'
        run: echo "Deploying JobPost"
    🔀 JobPost using contexts in conditional expressions

    4. JobPost matrix and needs contexts

    name: JobPost Matrix and Dependencies

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python: ['3.9', '3.10', '3.11', '3.12']
        database: ['postgresql', 'mysql']
    outputs:
      test_status: ${{ job.status }}
    steps:
      - name: Test JobPost
        run: |
          # Access matrix context
          echo "Testing JobPost with Python ${{ matrix.python }}"
          echo "Database: ${{ matrix.database }}"
  
  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Access needs context for job outputs
    if: needs.test.result == 'success'
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost
        run: |
          echo "Previous job status: ${{ needs.test.outputs.test_status }}"
          echo "Deploying JobPost"
    🔢 JobPost using matrix and needs contexts

    5. JobPost steps context for outputs

    name: JobPost Steps Context

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Build JobPost
        id: build
        run: |
          echo "Building JobPost"
          VERSION=$(python setup.py --version)
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "build_time=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Create JobPost package
        id: package
        run: |
          echo "version=${{ steps.build.outputs.version }}" | \
          python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel
          echo "package_name=jobpost-${{ steps.build.outputs.version }}.tar.gz" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Upload JobPost package
        run: |
          # Access outputs from previous steps
          echo "Uploading ${{ steps.package.outputs.package_name }}"
          echo "Built at: ${{ steps.build.outputs.build_time }}"
          echo "Version: ${{ steps.build.outputs.version }}"
      
      - name: Check step outcomes
        if: always()
        run: |
          echo "Build outcome: ${{ steps.build.outcome }}"
          echo "Package outcome: ${{ steps.package.outcome }}"
          echo "Build conclusion: ${{ steps.build.conclusion }}"
    📤 JobPost using steps context for outputs and status

    github context

    Docs: github context

    Access workflow run, repository, and event information using the github context.

    Full description

    The github context contains information about the workflow run and the event that triggered it. It's the most comprehensive context, providing access to repository details, commit information, pull request data, actor information, and event payloads. For JobPost workflows, the github context is essential for conditional logic, dynamic naming, notifications, and integrations. It's available in most parts of the workflow including job and step conditionals. The context properties vary depending on the event type that triggered the workflow.

    Syntax

    # Common github context properties
${{ github.repository }}         # owner/repo-name
${{ github.ref }}                 # Full git ref (refs/heads/main)
${{ github.ref_name }}            # Short ref name (main)
${{ github.sha }}                 # Commit SHA
${{ github.actor }}               # Username who triggered
${{ github.event_name }}          # Event type (push, pull_request, etc.)
${{ github.run_id }}              # Unique workflow run ID
${{ github.run_number }}          # Sequential run number
${{ github.workflow }}            # Workflow name
${{ github.event }}               # Full event payload

    Parameters description

    Repository properties:
    • github.repository - Repository owner and name (e.g., user/jobpost)
    • github.repository_owner - Repository owner username
    • github.repository_id - Unique repository ID
    Ref and commit properties:
    • github.ref - Fully-formed git ref (e.g., refs/heads/main)
    • github.ref_name - Short ref name (e.g., main)
    • github.sha - Commit SHA that triggered the workflow
    • github.head_ref - Head branch of pull request
    • github.base_ref - Base branch of pull request
    Actor and workflow properties:
    • github.actor - Username who triggered the workflow
    • github.triggering_actor - User who initiated the workflow
    • github.workflow - Name of the workflow
    • github.run_id - Unique ID for this workflow run
    • github.run_number - Sequential number for this workflow
    • github.run_attempt - Retry attempt number
    Event properties:
    • github.event_name - Name of the event (push, pull_request, etc.)
    • github.event - Full webhook event payload

    Return value

    The github context returns an object with string and nested object properties depending on the event type and workflow configuration.

    Examples

    1. JobPost repository and ref information

    name: JobPost GitHub Context

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  info:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Display JobPost repository info
        run: |
          echo "📦 JobPost Repository Information"
          echo "Repository: ${{ github.repository }}"
          echo "Owner: ${{ github.repository_owner }}"
          echo "Ref: ${{ github.ref }}"
          echo "Branch/Tag: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
          echo "Commit SHA: ${{ github.sha }}"
          echo "Short SHA: ${GITHUB_SHA::7}"
      
      - name: Check JobPost branch type
        run: |
          if [[ "${{ github.ref }}" == refs/heads/* ]]; then
            echo "JobPost branch push: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
          elif [[ "${{ github.ref }}" == refs/tags/* ]]; then
            echo "JobPost tag: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
          elif [[ "${{ github.ref }}" == refs/pull/* ]]; then
            echo "JobPost PR merge ref"
          fi
      
      - name: JobPost PR information
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        run: |
          echo "PR head: ${{ github.head_ref }}"
          echo "PR base: ${{ github.base_ref }}"
          echo "PR number: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
    📦 JobPost repository and ref information from github context

    2. JobPost workflow run information

    name: JobPost Run Info

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Display JobPost workflow info
        run: |
          echo "🔄 JobPost Workflow Run Information"
          echo "Workflow: ${{ github.workflow }}"
          echo "Run ID: ${{ github.run_id }}"
          echo "Run Number: ${{ github.run_number }}"
          echo "Run Attempt: ${{ github.run_attempt }}"
          echo "Actor: ${{ github.actor }}"
          echo "Triggering Actor: ${{ github.triggering_actor }}"
          echo "Event: ${{ github.event_name }}"
      
      - name: Create JobPost build identifier
        run: |
          BUILD_ID="jobpost-${{ github.run_number }}-${{ github.sha }}"
          echo "Build ID: $BUILD_ID"
          echo "BUILD_ID=$BUILD_ID" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Generate JobPost run URL
        run: |
          RUN_URL="https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }}"
          echo "Run URL: $RUN_URL"
          echo "RUN_URL=$RUN_URL" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: JobPost retry handling
        if: github.run_attempt > 1
        run: |
          echo "⚠️ JobPost workflow retry detected"
          echo "This is attempt #${{ github.run_attempt }}"
          echo "Checking for transient failures..."
    🔄 JobPost workflow run metadata from github context

    3. JobPost event-specific data

    name: JobPost Event Data

on:
  push:
  pull_request:
  issues:
    types: [opened, labeled]

jobs:
  process-event:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Handle JobPost push event
        if: github.event_name == 'push'
        run: |
          echo "📤 JobPost Push Event"
          echo "Pusher: ${{ github.event.pusher.name }}"
          echo "Commits: ${{ github.event.commits[0].message }}"
          echo "Compare: ${{ github.event.compare }}"
      
      - name: Handle JobPost PR event
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        run: |
          echo "🔀 JobPost Pull Request Event"
          echo "PR #${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
          echo "Title: ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.pull_request.user.login }}"
          echo "State: ${{ github.event.pull_request.state }}"
          echo "Draft: ${{ github.event.pull_request.draft }}"
      
      - name: Handle JobPost issue event
        if: github.event_name == 'issues'
        run: |
          echo "🐛 JobPost Issue Event"
          echo "Issue #${{ github.event.issue.number }}"
          echo "Title: ${{ github.event.issue.title }}"
          echo "Action: ${{ github.event.action }}"
          echo "Author: ${{ github.event.issue.user.login }}"
      
      - name: Access JobPost event payload
        run: |
          # Full event payload as JSON
          echo '${{ toJSON(github.event) }}' > event.json
          echo "Event payload saved to event.json"
    📋 JobPost event-specific data from github context

    4. JobPost conditional deployment logic

    name: JobPost Deployment

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  deploy-production:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Deploy to production only on main branch pushes
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost to production
        run: |
          echo "🚀 Deploying JobPost to production"
          echo "Commit: ${{ github.sha }}"
          echo "By: ${{ github.actor }}"
          python manage.py deploy --production
  
  deploy-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Deploy to staging on develop branch or non-draft PRs
    if: |
      (github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/develop') ||
      (github.event_name == 'pull_request' && github.event.pull_request.draft == false)
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost to staging
        run: |
          echo "🧪 Deploying JobPost to staging"
          if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" == "pull_request" ]; then
            echo "PR #${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            DEPLOY_URL="https://pr-${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}.staging.jobpost.com"
          else
            DEPLOY_URL="https://staging.jobpost.com"
          fi
          echo "Deploy URL: $DEPLOY_URL"
  
  deploy-preview:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Deploy preview for feature branches
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/heads/feature/')
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost preview
        run: |
          echo "👀 Deploying JobPost preview"
          echo "Feature: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
          PREVIEW_URL="https://${{ github.ref_name }}.preview.jobpost.com"
          echo "Preview URL: $PREVIEW_URL"
    🎯 JobPost conditional deployments using github context

    5. JobPost notifications and integrations

    name: JobPost Notifications

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  notify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Send JobPost Slack notification
        run: |
          # Build notification message with github context
          if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" == "push" ]; then
            MESSAGE="🚀 JobPost deployed to ${{ github.ref_name }}"
            MESSAGE="$MESSAGE\nCommit: ${{ github.sha }}"
            MESSAGE="$MESSAGE\nBy: ${{ github.actor }}"
            MESSAGE="$MESSAGE\nMessage: ${{ github.event.head_commit.message }}"
          elif [ "${{ github.event_name }}" == "pull_request" ]; then
            MESSAGE="🔀 JobPost PR #${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            MESSAGE="$MESSAGE\nTitle: ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }}"
            MESSAGE="$MESSAGE\nAuthor: ${{ github.event.pull_request.user.login }}"
            MESSAGE="$MESSAGE\n${{ github.event.pull_request.html_url }}"
          fi
          
          # Send to Slack webhook
          curl -X POST ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }} \
               -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
               -d "{\"text\": \"$MESSAGE\"}"
      
      - name: Create JobPost GitHub comment
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        run: |
          COMMENT="## JobPost CI Results\n"
          COMMENT="${COMMENT}✅ Build passed\n"
          COMMENT="${COMMENT}- Commit: \`${{ github.sha }}\`\n"
          COMMENT="${COMMENT}- Workflow: [${{ github.workflow }}]"
          COMMENT="${COMMENT}(https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}/actions/runs/${{ github.run_id }})"
          
          # Post comment using GitHub API
          curl -X POST \
               -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}" \
               -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
               https://api.github.com/repos/${{ github.repository }}/issues/${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}/comments \
               -d "{\"body\": \"$COMMENT\"}"
      
      - name: Update JobPost deployment status
        run: |
          # Create deployment status with github context
          python scripts/update_deployment.py \
            --repo="${{ github.repository }}" \
            --sha="${{ github.sha }}" \
            --ref="${{ github.ref_name }}" \
            --run-id="${{ github.run_id }}" \
            --actor="${{ github.actor }}"
    📢 JobPost notifications using github context data

    env context

    Docs: env context

    Access environment variables set at workflow, job, or step level using the env context.

    Full description

    The env context contains environment variables that have been set in a workflow, job, or step. You can set custom environment variables at different levels of the workflow, and they become accessible through the env context. For JobPost workflows, this is crucial for managing configuration values, API endpoints, deployment targets, and Django settings across different environments. Environment variables set with GITHUB_ENV are also accessible through the env context in subsequent steps. The env context is available in steps and can be used in conditionals.

    Syntax

    # Access environment variables
${{ env.VARIABLE_NAME }}

# Set environment variables at different levels
env:                          # Workflow level
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: anihpj.settings
  
jobs:
  build:
    env:                      # Job level
      DATABASE_NAME: jobpost_db
    steps:
      - name: Step with env
        env:                  # Step level
          DEBUG: true
        run: echo "${{ env.DEBUG }}"

    Parameters description

    Environment variable levels:
    • Workflow level - Available to all jobs and steps in the workflow
    • Job level - Available to all steps in that specific job
    • Step level - Available only in that specific step
    Precedence (highest to lowest):
    • Step-level environment variables override job-level
    • Job-level environment variables override workflow-level
    • Lower-level variables take precedence over higher-level ones
    Dynamic environment variables:
    • Can be created during workflow execution using echo "VAR=value" >> $GITHUB_ENV
    • Variables set in GITHUB_ENV are available in subsequent steps
    • Not available in the step where they are created

    Return value

    The env context returns string values for environment variable names. If an environment variable doesn't exist, it returns an empty string.

    Examples

    1. JobPost multi-level environment variables

    name: JobPost Environment Levels

on: [push]

# Workflow-level environment variables
env:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: anihpj.settings
  APP_NAME: jobpost
  LOG_LEVEL: INFO

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Job-level environment variables
    env:
      ENVIRONMENT: production
      DATABASE_NAME: jobpost_prod_db
      DEBUG: false
    
    steps:
      - name: Display JobPost workflow env vars
        run: |
          echo "📦 JobPost Environment Variables"
          echo "App: ${{ env.APP_NAME }}"
          echo "Settings: ${{ env.DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE }}"
          echo "Log Level: ${{ env.LOG_LEVEL }}"
      
      - name: Display JobPost job env vars
        run: |
          echo "Environment: ${{ env.ENVIRONMENT }}"
          echo "Database: ${{ env.DATABASE_NAME }}"
          echo "Debug: ${{ env.DEBUG }}"
      
      - name: JobPost step with override
        env:
          # Step-level override
          DEBUG: true
          LOG_LEVEL: DEBUG
        run: |
          echo "⚙️ JobPost Step Configuration"
          echo "Debug (overridden): ${{ env.DEBUG }}"
          echo "Log Level (overridden): ${{ env.LOG_LEVEL }}"
          echo "Environment (from job): ${{ env.ENVIRONMENT }}"
    📊 JobPost environment variable precedence levels

    2. JobPost dynamic environment variables

    name: JobPost Dynamic Env

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Generate JobPost build info
        run: |
          # Create dynamic environment variables
          BUILD_TIME=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
          BUILD_VERSION="v1.0.${{ github.run_number }}"
          DEPLOY_URL="https://jobpost-${{ github.run_number }}.example.com"
          
          # Save to GITHUB_ENV for subsequent steps
          echo "BUILD_TIME=$BUILD_TIME" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "BUILD_VERSION=$BUILD_VERSION" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "DEPLOY_URL=$DEPLOY_URL" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          
          echo "Generated JobPost build info"
      
      - name: Use JobPost build info
        run: |
          # Access dynamically created env vars
          echo "🏗️ JobPost Build Information"
          echo "Build Time: ${{ env.BUILD_TIME }}"
          echo "Build Version: ${{ env.BUILD_VERSION }}"
          echo "Deploy URL: ${{ env.DEPLOY_URL }}"
      
      - name: JobPost deployment with env vars
        run: |
          python manage.py deploy \
            --version="${{ env.BUILD_VERSION }}" \
            --url="${{ env.DEPLOY_URL }}" \
            --timestamp="${{ env.BUILD_TIME }}"
      
      - name: Create JobPost release tag
        run: |
          # Use env vars in git operations
          git tag "${{ env.BUILD_VERSION }}" \
                  -m "JobPost release ${{ env.BUILD_VERSION }} at ${{ env.BUILD_TIME }}"
    ⚡ JobPost dynamic environment variables across steps

    3. JobPost environment-specific configuration

    name: JobPost Multi-Environment

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop, staging]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Set JobPost environment variables
        run: |
          # Determine environment based on branch
          if [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/main" ]; then
            ENV="production"
            DB_HOST="jobpost-prod.db.example.com"
            API_URL="https://api.jobpost.com"
            WORKERS=4
          elif [ "${{ github.ref }}" == "refs/heads/staging" ]; then
            ENV="staging"
            DB_HOST="jobpost-staging.db.example.com"
            API_URL="https://api.staging.jobpost.com"
            WORKERS=2
          else
            ENV="development"
            DB_HOST="localhost"
            API_URL="http://localhost:8000"
            WORKERS=1
          fi
          
          # Save JobPost configuration to env
          echo "JOBPOST_ENV=$ENV" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "JOBPOST_DB_HOST=$DB_HOST" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "JOBPOST_API_URL=$API_URL" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "JOBPOST_WORKERS=$WORKERS" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Display JobPost environment config
        run: |
          echo "🌍 JobPost Environment: ${{ env.JOBPOST_ENV }}"
          echo "Database: ${{ env.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}"
          echo "API URL: ${{ env.JOBPOST_API_URL }}"
          echo "Workers: ${{ env.JOBPOST_WORKERS }}"
      
      - name: Deploy JobPost application
        run: |
          python manage.py deploy \
            --environment="${{ env.JOBPOST_ENV }}" \
            --database="${{ env.JOBPOST_DB_HOST }}" \
            --api-url="${{ env.JOBPOST_API_URL }}" \
            --workers="${{ env.JOBPOST_WORKERS }}"
    🌍 JobPost environment-specific configurations using env context

    4. JobPost conditional execution with env

    name: JobPost Conditional Steps

on: [push]

env:
  RUN_TESTS: true
  RUN_SECURITY_SCAN: true
  DEPLOY_ENABLED: false

jobs:
  ci-cd:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout JobPost code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Run JobPost tests
        if: env.RUN_TESTS == 'true'
        run: |
          echo "🧪 Running JobPost tests"
          python manage.py test jobpost
      
      - name: Run JobPost security scan
        if: env.RUN_SECURITY_SCAN == 'true'
        run: |
          echo "🔒 Running JobPost security scan"
          pip install bandit
          bandit -r jobpost/ anihpj/
      
      - name: Deploy JobPost
        if: env.DEPLOY_ENABLED == 'true'
        run: |
          echo "🚀 Deploying JobPost"
          python manage.py deploy
      
      - name: Set deployment status
        run: |
          if [ "${{ env.DEPLOY_ENABLED }}" == "true" ]; then
            STATUS="deployed"
          else
            STATUS="built-only"
          fi
          echo "DEPLOYMENT_STATUS=$STATUS" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Report JobPost status
        run: |
          echo "📋 JobPost CI/CD Summary"
          echo "Tests Run: ${{ env.RUN_TESTS }}"
          echo "Security Scan: ${{ env.RUN_SECURITY_SCAN }}"
          echo "Deployment: ${{ env.DEPLOYMENT_STATUS }}"
    🎯 JobPost conditional workflow execution using env context

    5. JobPost cross-step communication

    name: JobPost Communication

on: [push]

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Build JobPost application
        run: |
          echo "🏗️ Building JobPost"
          python manage.py collectstatic --noinput
          
          # Store build results
          BUILD_SUCCESS="true"
          BUILD_SIZE=$(du -sh staticfiles | cut -f1)
          STATIC_FILES=$(find staticfiles -type f | wc -l)
          
          echo "BUILD_SUCCESS=$BUILD_SUCCESS" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "BUILD_SIZE=$BUILD_SIZE" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "STATIC_FILES=$STATIC_FILES" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Test JobPost application
        if: env.BUILD_SUCCESS == 'true'
        run: |
          echo "🧪 Testing JobPost"
          python manage.py test
          
          # Store test results
          TEST_COUNT=42
          TEST_PASSED=40
          TEST_FAILED=2
          
          echo "TEST_COUNT=$TEST_COUNT" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "TEST_PASSED=$TEST_PASSED" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          echo "TEST_FAILED=$TEST_FAILED" >> $GITHUB_ENV
      
      - name: Generate JobPost report
        run: |
          echo "📊 JobPost Build & Test Report"
          echo "================================"
          echo "Build Status: ${{ env.BUILD_SUCCESS }}"
          echo "Static Size: ${{ env.BUILD_SIZE }}"
          echo "Static Files: ${{ env.STATIC_FILES }}"
          echo "Tests Run: ${{ env.TEST_COUNT }}"
          echo "Tests Passed: ${{ env.TEST_PASSED }}"
          echo "Tests Failed: ${{ env.TEST_FAILED }}"
          
          # Create summary report
          REPORT_FILE="jobpost-report-${{ github.run_number }}.txt"
          echo "REPORT_FILE=$REPORT_FILE" >> $GITHUB_ENV
          
          cat > $REPORT_FILE << EOF
          JobPost CI Report
          Build: ${{ env.BUILD_SUCCESS }}
          Static: ${{ env.BUILD_SIZE }} (${{ env.STATIC_FILES }} files)
          Tests: ${{ env.TEST_PASSED }}/${{ env.TEST_COUNT }} passed
          EOF
      
      - name: Upload JobPost report
        run: |
          echo "📤 Uploading report: ${{ env.REPORT_FILE }}"
          # Upload logic here
    🔄 JobPost cross-step data communication using env context

    vars context

    Docs: vars context

    Access configuration variables defined at repository, environment, or organization level using the vars context.

    Full description

    The vars context contains configuration variables that you set at the repository, environment, or organization level. Unlike environment variables, configuration variables are stored in GitHub's UI and can be reused across workflows without hardcoding values. For JobPost workflows, vars are ideal for non-sensitive configuration like API endpoints, feature flags, deployment targets, and application settings that vary across environments. Configuration variables follow a precedence order: environment-level variables override repository-level, which override organization-level variables.

    Syntax

    # Access configuration variables
${{ vars.VARIABLE_NAME }}

# Common usage in JobPost workflows
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: ${{ vars.DEPLOY_ENVIRONMENT }}
    steps:
      - name: Deploy
        run: |
          python manage.py deploy \
            --url="${{ vars.API_URL }}" \
            --region="${{ vars.AWS_REGION }}"

    Parameters description

    Configuration variable levels:
    • Organization-level - Available to all repositories in the organization
    • Repository-level - Available to all workflows in the repository
    • Environment-level - Available only when job specifies that environment
    Precedence (highest to lowest):
    • Environment-level variables (when environment is specified)
    • Repository-level variables
    • Organization-level variables
    Characteristics:
    • Non-sensitive values only (use secrets context for sensitive data)
    • Maximum 255 characters per variable value
    • Variable names must start with letter or underscore, contain only alphanumeric and underscores
    • Case-sensitive on all platforms

    Return value

    The vars context returns string values for configuration variable names. If a variable doesn't exist, it returns an empty string.

    Examples

    1. JobPost environment-specific configuration

    name: JobPost Deploy with Vars

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, staging, develop]

jobs:
  deploy-production:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    # Environment-level vars take precedence
    environment: production
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost to production
        run: |
          echo "🚀 Deploying JobPost to Production"
          echo "Environment: ${{ vars.ENVIRONMENT_NAME }}"
          echo "API URL: ${{ vars.API_URL }}"
          echo "Database: ${{ vars.DATABASE_HOST }}"
          echo "Region: ${{ vars.AWS_REGION }}"
          
          # Use vars in deployment
          python manage.py deploy \
            --environment="${{ vars.ENVIRONMENT_NAME }}" \
            --api-url="${{ vars.API_URL }}" \
            --database="${{ vars.DATABASE_HOST }}" \
            --region="${{ vars.AWS_REGION }}"
  
  deploy-staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/staging'
    steps:
      - name: Deploy JobPost to staging
        run: |
