  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Security scanner information
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🔒 JobPost Security Scanner"
        echo "==========================="
        echo "Action: JobPost Security Scanner"
        echo "Author: JobPost Security Team | anihpj Organization"
        echo ""
        echo "Scan Configuration:"
        echo "  Type: ${{ inputs.scan-type }}"
        echo "  Severity threshold: ${{ inputs.severity-threshold }}"
        echo ""
        echo "Scanning capabilities:"
        echo "  🔍 Dependency vulnerability scanning"
        echo "  🔍 Static code analysis (Bandit)"
        echo "  🔍 Secret detection (git-secrets)"
        echo "  🔍 Django security checks"
        echo ""
        echo "Maintained by: JobPost Security Team"
        echo "Organization: anihpj"
    🔒 JobPost security scanner with team and organization attribution

    5. JobPost action naming best practices

    # Example 1: Clear, action-focused name
# action.yml
name: 'Setup JobPost Environment'
author: 'JobPost DevOps'
description: 'Configure Python environment, install dependencies, and setup database for JobPost Django application'

---
# Example 2: Descriptive name with context
# action.yml
name: 'JobPost Django Linter & Formatter'
author: 'anihpj '
description: 'Run Black, isort, Flake8, and MyPy checks on JobPost codebase with configurable settings'

---
# Example 3: Version-specific action name
# action.yml
name: 'JobPost Django 5.x Compatibility Checker'
author: 'JobPost Compatibility Team'
description: 'Verify JobPost application compatibility with Django 5.x releases and identify deprecated API usage'

---
# Example 4: Integration-focused name
# action.yml
name: 'JobPost Slack Notifier'
author: 'JobPost Integrations Team'
description: 'Send customizable deployment and test notifications to JobPost team Slack channels'

---
# Example 5: Utility action name
# action.yml
name: 'JobPost Cache Manager'
author: 'anihpj Platform Team'
description: 'Intelligent cache management for JobPost workflows including pip, npm, and Docker layer caching'

# Usage example in workflow
---
name: Use JobPost Actions

on: [push]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Display action naming best practices
        run: |
          echo "📋 JobPost Action Naming Best Practices"
          echo "========================================"
          echo ""
          echo "Good action names:"
          echo "  ✓ 'Deploy JobPost Django App'"
          echo "  ✓ 'JobPost Database Manager'"
          echo "  ✓ 'Setup JobPost Environment'"
          echo "  ✓ 'JobPost Security Scanner'"
          echo ""
          echo "Name characteristics:"
          echo "  ✓ Clear purpose (Deploy, Setup, Scan, etc.)"
          echo "  ✓ Context included (JobPost, Django)"
          echo "  ✓ Action verb or noun"
          echo "  ✓ Under 50 characters"
          echo "  ✓ No special characters"
          echo ""
          echo "Author best practices:"
          echo "  ✓ Team name: 'JobPost DevOps Team'"
          echo "  ✓ Organization: 'anihpj'"
          echo "  ✓ With contact: 'Team '"
          echo "  ✓ With affiliation: 'Team | Organization'"
          echo ""
          echo "Avoid:"
          echo "  ✗ Generic names: 'Deploy Action'"
          echo "  ✗ Unclear purpose: 'JobPost Helper'"
          echo "  ✗ Too long: 'The Ultimate JobPost Django...'"
          echo "  ✗ Version in name: 'JobPost v1.2.3 Deployer'"
          echo ""
          echo "Benefits of good naming:"
          echo "  • Easy to discover in Marketplace"
          echo "  • Clear in workflow logs"
          echo "  • Professional appearance"
          echo "  • Better documentation"
    📋 JobPost action naming best practices and guidelines

    description

    Docs: Action metadata - description

    Provide a clear and concise description of your custom GitHub Action's purpose and functionality.

    Full description

    The description field is required in action metadata and provides a brief explanation of what the action does. This description appears in the GitHub Marketplace, the Actions tab, and when browsing action repositories. For the JobPost Django application, action descriptions should clearly explain the action's purpose, key features, and primary use cases. Good descriptions help users quickly understand if an action meets their needs and how it fits into their workflow. Keep descriptions concise (under 125 characters for Marketplace display) while conveying the action's value proposition.

    Syntax

    # action.yml
name: 'Action Name'
description: 'Brief, clear description of what the action does and its primary purpose'
# Recommended: 25-125 characters
# Required field for all actions

    Parameters description

    Description field guidelines:
    • Required - Every action must have a description field
    • Length - Aim for 25-125 characters for optimal display
    • Clarity - Clearly state what the action does
    • Keywords - Include relevant terms for searchability
    • Value proposition - Explain why someone would use this action
    • Avoid marketing - Focus on functionality, not sales language

    Return value

    The description field doesn't return a value; it provides metadata displayed in the GitHub UI. A well-written description improves action discoverability and helps users understand the action's purpose at a glance.

    1. JobPost deployment action description

    # action.yml
name: 'Deploy JobPost Django App'
author: 'JobPost DevOps Team'
description: 'Deploy Django application with database migrations, static collection, and health checks'

inputs:
  environment:
    description: 'Deployment environment (staging/production)'
    required: true
  run-migrations:
    description: 'Run database migrations during deployment'
    required: false
    default: 'true'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Display description benefits
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "📝 JobPost Action Description Analysis"
        echo "======================================"
        echo ""
        echo "Action: Deploy JobPost Django App"
        echo "Description: Deploy Django application with database"
        echo "             migrations, static collection, and health checks"
        echo ""
        echo "Description breakdown:"
        echo "  • 'Deploy Django application' - Core action"
        echo "  • 'with database migrations' - Key feature 1"
        echo "  • 'static collection' - Key feature 2"
        echo "  • 'and health checks' - Key feature 3"
        echo ""
        echo "Character count: 82 (within 125 limit)"
        echo ""
        echo "Why this description works:"
        echo "  ✓ Clear action verb (Deploy)"
        echo "  ✓ Specifies technology (Django)"
        echo "  ✓ Lists main features"
        echo "  ✓ Concise and scannable"
        echo "  ✓ Professional tone"
    📝 JobPost deployment action with effective description

    2. JobPost testing action description

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Test Suite Runner'
author: 'JobPost QA Team'
description: 'Run Django tests with coverage, parallel execution, and detailed reporting for JobPost application'

inputs:
  test-path:
    description: 'Path to tests (default: jobpost)'
    required: false
    default: 'jobpost'
  coverage-threshold:
    description: 'Minimum coverage percentage required'
    required: false
    default: '80'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Analyze test action description
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🧪 Test Action Description Analysis"
        echo "===================================="
        echo ""
        echo "Description: Run Django tests with coverage, parallel"
        echo "             execution, and detailed reporting for JobPost"
        echo ""
        echo "Key components:"
        echo "  1. 'Run Django tests' - Primary function"
        echo "  2. 'with coverage' - Feature 1"
        echo "  3. 'parallel execution' - Feature 2"
        echo "  4. 'detailed reporting' - Feature 3"
        echo "  5. 'for JobPost application' - Context"
        echo ""
        echo "Character count: 98 (optimal for Marketplace)"
        echo ""
        echo "Searchable keywords:"
        echo "  • Django"
        echo "  • tests"
        echo "  • coverage"
        echo "  • parallel"
        echo "  • JobPost"
        echo ""
        echo "User expectations set:"
        echo "  ✓ Will run Django tests"
        echo "  ✓ Includes coverage reporting"
        echo "  ✓ Supports parallel execution"
        echo "  ✓ Provides detailed output"
    🧪 JobPost testing action with feature-rich description

    3. JobPost database action with clear description

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Database Operations'
author: 'anihpj'
description: 'Manage PostgreSQL database: migrations, backups, seeding, and rollbacks for Django JobPost'

inputs:
  operation:
    description: 'Database operation (migrate/backup/seed/rollback)'
    required: true
  database-url:
    description: 'PostgreSQL connection URL'
    required: true

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Database action description analysis
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "💾 Database Action Description Analysis"
        echo "========================================"
        echo ""
        echo "Description: Manage PostgreSQL database: migrations,"
        echo "             backups, seeding, and rollbacks for Django JobPost"
        echo ""
        echo "Description structure:"
        echo "  • 'Manage PostgreSQL database' - Scope & technology"
        echo "  • 'migrations, backups, seeding, rollbacks' - Operations"
        echo "  • 'for Django JobPost' - Application context"
        echo ""
        echo "Character count: 92 (excellent length)"
        echo ""
        echo "Clear expectations:"
        echo "  ✓ Works with PostgreSQL specifically"
        echo "  ✓ Four main operations listed"
        echo "  ✓ Django-specific"
        echo "  ✓ JobPost application context"
        echo ""
        echo "Improvement over vague description:"
        echo "  ❌ Bad: 'Database helper for Django'"
        echo "  ✅ Good: Current description"
        echo ""
        echo "Why it's effective:"
        echo "  • Specifies database type (PostgreSQL)"
        echo "  • Lists exact operations"
        echo "  • Clear target (Django JobPost)"
        echo "  • No ambiguity"
    💾 JobPost database action with technology-specific description

    4. JobPost security scanner description

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Security Audit'
author: 'JobPost Security Team'
description: 'Scan for vulnerabilities: dependencies (Safety), code (Bandit), secrets, and Django security issues'

inputs:
  scan-level:
    description: 'Scan depth (quick/standard/thorough)'
    required: false
    default: 'standard'
  fail-on:
    description: 'Severity to fail build (medium/high/critical)'
    required: false
    default: 'high'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Security action description analysis
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🔒 Security Action Description Analysis"
        echo "======================================="
        echo ""
        echo "Description: Scan for vulnerabilities: dependencies (Safety),"
        echo "             code (Bandit), secrets, and Django security issues"
        echo ""
        echo "Description power:"
        echo "  • 'Scan for vulnerabilities' - Clear purpose"
        echo "  • 'dependencies (Safety)' - Tool named"
        echo "  • 'code (Bandit)' - Tool named"
        echo "  • 'secrets' - Important feature"
        echo "  • 'Django security issues' - Framework-specific"
        echo ""
        echo "Character count: 103 (within limit)"
        echo ""
        echo "Transparency benefits:"
        echo "  ✓ Names specific tools (Safety, Bandit)"
        echo "  ✓ Lists vulnerability types"
        echo "  ✓ Django-aware checks mentioned"
        echo "  ✓ Users know what to expect"
        echo ""
        echo "Technical credibility:"
        echo "  • Recognizable tool names"
        echo "  • Comprehensive coverage"
        echo "  • Framework-specific"
        echo "  • Professional approach"
        echo ""
        echo "Compare with alternatives:"
        echo "  ❌ Vague: 'Security scanner for Python'"
        echo "  ❌ Generic: 'Find security problems'"
        echo "  ✅ Current: Specific tools and coverage"
    🔒 JobPost security action with tool-specific description

    5. JobPost description best practices comparison

    # Example 1: TOO SHORT - Not descriptive enough
name: 'Deploy JobPost'
description: 'Deploys the app'
# ❌ Problem: Doesn't explain how or what features

---
# Example 2: TOO LONG - Exceeds recommended length
name: 'JobPost Deployer'
description: 'This comprehensive action will deploy your JobPost Django application to various environments including staging and production with full support for database migrations, static file collection, cache warming, health checks, rollback capabilities, and notification systems'
# ❌ Problem: 260 characters, hard to scan, too much detail

---
# Example 3: TOO VAGUE - Not specific enough
name: 'JobPost Helper'
description: 'Helps with JobPost workflows and makes things easier'
# ❌ Problem: Doesn't explain what it actually does

---
# Example 4: EXCELLENT - Clear, specific, right length
name: 'Deploy JobPost Django'
description: 'Deploy Django app with migrations, static files, and health verification'
# ✅ Great: 78 characters, clear features, specific

---
# Example 5: EXCELLENT - Feature-focused
name: 'JobPost Test Runner'
description: 'Run Django tests with coverage reporting, parallel execution, and JUnit XML output'
# ✅ Great: 89 characters, lists key features, clear tech

---
# Example 6: EXCELLENT - Tool transparency
name: 'JobPost Code Quality'
description: 'Analyze code with Black, isort, Flake8, and MyPy for Django best practices'
# ✅ Great: 83 characters, names tools, clear purpose

---
# Example 7: MARKETING LANGUAGE - Avoid this
name: 'Amazing JobPost Deployer'
description: 'The ultimate, revolutionary deployment solution that will transform your workflow!'
# ❌ Problem: Marketing hype, no technical details

---
# Demonstration workflow
name: Description Best Practices

on: [push]

jobs:
  analyze-descriptions:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Description best practices guide
        run: |
          echo "📚 JobPost Action Description Best Practices"
          echo "============================================="
          echo ""
          echo "✅ GOOD DESCRIPTIONS:"
          echo ""
          echo "1. Clear and specific"
          echo "   'Deploy Django app with migrations, static files, and health verification'"
          echo "   • States what it does"
          echo "   • Lists main features"
          echo "   • 78 characters"
          echo ""
          echo "2. Tool transparency"
          echo "   'Analyze code with Black, isort, Flake8, and MyPy for Django'"
          echo "   • Names specific tools"
          echo "   • Clear technology"
          echo "   • 68 characters"
          echo ""
          echo "3. Feature-focused"
          echo "   'Run tests with coverage, parallel execution, and detailed reports'"
          echo "   • Action verb"
          echo "   • Three key features"
          echo "   • 74 characters"
          echo ""
          echo "❌ AVOID:"
          echo ""
          echo "• Too short: 'Deploys the app' (16 chars)"
          echo "• Too long: Exceeds 125 characters"
          echo "• Too vague: 'Helps with workflows'"
          echo "• Marketing: 'Ultimate revolutionary solution'"
          echo "• No tech details: 'Makes deployment easy'"
          echo ""
          echo "📏 LENGTH GUIDELINES:"
          echo ""
          echo "• Minimum: 25 characters (be descriptive)"
          echo "• Ideal: 60-90 characters (detailed but scannable)"
          echo "• Maximum: 125 characters (Marketplace cutoff)"
          echo ""
          echo "🎯 KEY ELEMENTS TO INCLUDE:"
          echo ""
          echo "1. Action verb (Deploy, Run, Analyze, Scan, Manage)"
          echo "2. Technology/framework (Django, Python, PostgreSQL)"
          echo "3. Main features (1-3 key capabilities)"
          echo "4. Optional: Tool names for transparency"
          echo "5. Optional: Target application (JobPost)"
          echo ""
          echo "🔍 SEARCHABILITY:"
          echo ""
          echo "Include keywords users might search:"
          echo "  • Django, Python, PostgreSQL"
          echo "  • deploy, test, scan, analyze"
          echo "  • coverage, parallel, security"
          echo "  • migrations, static files"
          echo ""
          echo "📝 WRITING TIPS:"
          echo ""
          echo "• Start with action verb"
          echo "• Use active voice"
          echo "• Be specific, not generic"
          echo "• List concrete features"
          echo "• Avoid jargon and marketing"
          echo "• Test readability"
          echo ""
          echo "✨ QUALITY CHECKLIST:"
          echo ""
          echo "□ Action verb present?"
          echo "□ Technology specified?"
          echo "□ Main features listed?"
          echo "□ 25-125 character range?"
          echo "□ No marketing fluff?"
          echo "□ Easy to understand?"
          echo "□ Searchable keywords?"
    📚 Comprehensive guide to JobPost action description best practices

    inputs

    Docs: Action metadata - inputs

    Define input parameters that your action expects to receive during runtime.

    Full description

    Input parameters allow you to specify data that the action expects to use during runtime. GitHub stores input parameters as environment variables. For composite actions, inputs are accessible via the inputs context, while Docker and JavaScript actions receive them as environment variables with the INPUT_ naming convention (converted to uppercase with spaces replaced by underscores). For the JobPost application, inputs might include environment names, version numbers, database URLs, or configuration flags that customize action behavior for different deployment scenarios.

    Input Parameter Environment Variables

    Automatic Environment Variable Creation: When you specify an input, GitHub creates an environment variable for the input with the name INPUT_. The environment variable created converts input names to uppercase letters and replaces spaces with _ characters.

    Composite Actions: If the action is written using a composite, then it will not automatically get INPUT_. With composite actions you can use the inputs context to access action inputs.

    Docker Container Actions: To access the environment variable in a Docker container action, you must pass the input using the args keyword in the action metadata file.

    Syntax

    # action.yml
inputs:
  num-octocats:
    description: 'Number of Octocats'
    required: false
    default: '1'
  octocat-eye-color:
    description: 'Eye color of the Octocats'
    required: true

    Parameters description

    Input configuration options:
    • inputs. - Required. String identifier to associate with the input. Must be unique within the inputs object. Must start with a letter or _ and contain only alphanumeric characters, -, or _
    • inputs..description - Required. String description of the input parameter
    • inputs..required - Optional. Boolean to indicate whether the action requires the input parameter. Set to true when the parameter is required
    • inputs..default - Optional. String representing the default value. The default value is used when an input parameter isn't specified in a workflow file
    • inputs..deprecationMessage - Optional. If the input parameter is used, this string is logged as a warning message. You can use this warning to notify users that the input is deprecated and mention any alternatives
    ⚠️ Important Note:

    Actions using required: true will not automatically return an error if the input is not specified. Workflow files that use this action must use the with keyword to set an input value.

    Return value

    Inputs are made available to the action code as environment variables or through the inputs context, allowing the action to adapt its behavior based on the provided values.

    1. JobPost deployment inputs

    # action.yml
name: 'Deploy JobPost'
inputs:
  environment:
    description: 'Target environment (staging/production)'
    required: true
  python-version:
    description: 'Python version to use'
    required: false
    default: '3.11'
  skip-migrations:
    description: 'Skip database migrations'
    required: false
    default: 'false'
  django-settings:
    description: 'Django settings module'
    required: false
    default: 'anihpj.settings.production'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Display input values
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🚀 JobPost Deployment Inputs"
        echo "============================"
        echo "Environment: ${{ inputs.environment }}"
        echo "Python Version: ${{ inputs.python-version }}"
        echo "Skip Migrations: ${{ inputs.skip-migrations }}"
        echo "Django Settings: ${{ inputs.django-settings }}"
        echo ""
        echo "Environment variable equivalents:"
        echo "  INPUT_ENVIRONMENT=${{ inputs.environment }}"
        echo "  INPUT_PYTHON-VERSION=${{ inputs.python-version }}"
        echo "  INPUT_SKIP-MIGRATIONS=${{ inputs.skip-migrations }}"
        echo "  INPUT_DJANGO-SETTINGS=${{ inputs.django-settings }}"
    🚀 JobPost deployment action with required and optional inputs

    2. JobPost database inputs with defaults

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Database Manager'
inputs:
  operation:
    description: 'Operation to perform (migrate/backup/seed/rollback)'
    required: true
  database-url:
    description: 'PostgreSQL database URL'
    required: false
    default: 'postgresql://localhost:5432/jobpost'
  backup-path:
    description: 'Path to store database backups'
    required: false
    default: './backups'
  fixtures:
    description: 'Comma-separated list of fixture files to load'
    required: false
    default: 'skills,locations'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Process database inputs
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "💾 JobPost Database Operation Inputs"
        echo "====================================="
        echo "Operation: ${{ inputs.operation }}"
        echo "Database URL: ${{ inputs.database-url }}"
        echo "Backup Path: ${{ inputs.backup-path }}"
        echo "Fixtures: ${{ inputs.fixtures }}"
        echo ""
        echo "Default values in action:"
        echo "  • database-url has default (localhost)"
        echo "  • backup-path has default (./backups)"
        echo "  • fixtures has default (skills,locations)"
        echo "  • operation is REQUIRED (no default)"
    💾 JobPost database manager with default input values

    3. JobPost test inputs with deprecation

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Test Runner'
inputs:
  test-path:
    description: 'Path to test directory'
    required: false
    default: 'jobpost'
  coverage-threshold:
    description: 'Minimum coverage percentage'
    required: false
    default: '80'
  parallel:
    description: 'Run tests in parallel'
    required: false
    default: 'true'
  use-pytest:
    description: 'DEPRECATED: Use test-runner instead. Use pytest instead of unittest'
    required: false
    default: 'false'
    deprecationMessage: 'The use-pytest input is deprecated. Please use test-runner input with values: pytest, unittest, or nose2'
  test-runner:
    description: 'Test runner to use (pytest/unittest/nose2)'
    required: false
    default: 'pytest'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Check for deprecated inputs
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🧪 JobPost Test Runner Inputs"
        echo "============================="
        echo "Test Path: ${{ inputs.test-path }}"
        echo "Coverage Threshold: ${{ inputs.coverage-threshold }}%"
        echo "Parallel: ${{ inputs.parallel }}"
        echo "Test Runner: ${{ inputs.test-runner }}"
        echo ""
        if [ "${{ inputs.use-pytest }}" != "false" ]; then
          echo "⚠️  WARNING: use-pytest is deprecated!"
          echo "    Please use test-runner input instead"
          echo "    Example: test-runner: 'pytest'"
        fi
    🧪 JobPost test runner with deprecation warning for old input

    4. JobPost Docker action with input args

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Security Scanner'
inputs:
  scan-type:
    description: 'Type of security scan (dependencies/code/secrets)'
    required: true
  severity-threshold:
    description: 'Minimum severity level (low/medium/high/critical)'
    required: false
    default: 'medium'
  fail-on-error:
    description: 'Fail the action if vulnerabilities are found'
    required: false
    default: 'true'

runs:
  using: 'docker'
  image: 'Dockerfile'
  args:
    - ${{ inputs.scan-type }}
    - ${{ inputs.severity-threshold }}
    - ${{ inputs.fail-on-error }}

# Usage in workflow
---
name: Security Scan

on: [push]

jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: ./actions/security-scanner
        with:
          scan-type: 'dependencies'
          severity-threshold: 'high'
          fail-on-error: 'true'
      - name: Show input flow
        run: |
          echo "📊 Input Flow in Docker Actions"
          echo "================================"
          echo ""
          echo "1. Workflow provides inputs via 'with:'"
          echo "   scan-type: 'dependencies'"
          echo "   severity-threshold: 'high'"
          echo ""
          echo "2. Action metadata defines inputs"
          echo "   (description, required, default)"
          echo ""
          echo "3. Docker action receives via args:"
          echo "   args: [\${{ inputs.scan-type }}, ...]"
          echo ""
          echo "4. Container entrypoint receives:"
          echo "   ./scan.sh dependencies high true"
    🔒 JobPost Docker action passing inputs through args

    5. JobPost input best practices

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Deployment Best Practices Example'

# ✅ GOOD INPUT DESIGN
inputs:
  # Required inputs first
  environment:
    description: 'Deployment environment: staging, production, or development'
    required: true
    # No default for required inputs
  
  # Optional inputs with sensible defaults
  python-version:
    description: 'Python version (3.9, 3.10, 3.11, 3.12)'
    required: false
    default: '3.11'  # Latest stable version
  
  django-version:
    description: 'Django version to install'
    required: false
    default: '5.0'
  
  # Boolean inputs as strings
  run-migrations:
    description: 'Run database migrations (true/false)'
    required: false
    default: 'true'  # Safe default
  
  collect-static:
    description: 'Collect static files (true/false)'
    required: false
    default: 'true'
  
  # Descriptive input with examples
  allowed-hosts:
    description: 'Comma-separated list of allowed hosts (e.g., example.com,www.example.com)'
    required: false
    default: 'localhost,127.0.0.1'
  
  # Deprecated input with migration path
  use-old-deploy:
    description: 'DEPRECATED: Use deployment-strategy instead'
    required: false
    deprecationMessage: 'use-old-deploy is deprecated. Please migrate to deployment-strategy input with values: blue-green, rolling, or canary'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Input design best practices
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "✅ JobPost Input Design Best Practices"
        echo "======================================="
        echo ""
        echo "1. NAMING CONVENTIONS:"
        echo "   ✓ Use kebab-case: python-version"
        echo "   ✓ Be descriptive: severity-threshold"
        echo "   ✓ Start with letter or _"
        echo "   ✗ Avoid: pythonVersion, PYTHON_VERSION"
        echo ""
        echo "2. REQUIRED INPUTS:"
        echo "   ✓ Mark truly essential inputs"
        echo "   ✓ No default values"
        echo "   ✓ Clear descriptions"
        echo "   ✗ Don't overuse required: true"
        echo ""
        echo "3. DEFAULT VALUES:"
        echo "   ✓ Sensible defaults for optional inputs"
        echo "   ✓ Latest stable versions"
        echo "   ✓ Safe operation defaults"
        echo "   ✓ Match common use cases"
        echo ""
        echo "4. DESCRIPTIONS:"
        echo "   ✓ Explain purpose clearly"
        echo "   ✓ List valid values/examples"
        echo "   ✓ Include format hints"
        echo "   ✓ Mention defaults"
        echo ""
        echo "5. BOOLEAN INPUTS:"
        echo "   ✓ Use strings: 'true'/'false'"
        echo "   ✓ Not booleans: true/false"
        echo "   ✓ Clear yes/no meaning"
        echo ""
        echo "6. DEPRECATION:"
        echo "   ✓ Use deprecationMessage"
        echo "   ✓ Suggest alternatives"
        echo "   ✓ Maintain backward compatibility"
        echo "   ✓ Provide migration timeline"
    ✅ JobPost comprehensive input design best practices

    outputs

    Docs: Action metadata - outputs

    Define output parameters that your action sets for use by subsequent actions or jobs.

    Full description

    Output parameters allow you to declare data that an action sets. Actions that run later in a workflow can use the output data set in previously run actions. For example, if you had an action that performed the addition of two inputs (x + y = z), the action could output the sum (z) for other actions to use as an input. For the JobPost application, outputs might include deployment URLs, test coverage percentages, build artifact names, or database migration counts that subsequent workflow steps need to access.

    ⚠️ Output Size Limits:

    Outputs can be a maximum of 1 MB per job. The total of all outputs in a workflow run can be a maximum of 50 MB. Size is approximated based on UTF-16 encoding.

    Outputs for Different Action Types

    Docker Container and JavaScript Actions: Use outputs. and outputs..description

    Composite Actions: Additionally require the value token to map the output to a step output value using the steps context.

    Syntax

    # Docker/JavaScript actions
outputs:
  sum:
    description: 'The sum of the inputs'

# Composite actions
outputs:
  random-number:
    description: "Random number"
    value: ${{ steps.random-number-generator.outputs.random-id }}

    Parameters description

    Output configuration:
    • outputs. - Required. String identifier to associate with the output. Must be unique within the outputs object. Must start with a letter or _ and contain only alphanumeric characters, -, or _
    • outputs..description - Required. String description of the output parameter
    • outputs..value - Required for composite actions. The value that the output parameter will be mapped to. You can set this to a string or an expression with context. For example, you can use the steps context to set the value of an output to the output value of a step

    Return value

    Outputs are accessible in subsequent steps and jobs using the steps or jobs context, allowing workflow orchestration and data passing between action invocations.

    1. JobPost composite action with outputs

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Build Info'
description: 'Generate build information for JobPost deployment'

outputs:
  version:
    description: 'JobPost application version'
    value: ${{ steps.get-version.outputs.version }}
  commit-sha:
    description: 'Git commit SHA'
    value: ${{ steps.get-commit.outputs.sha }}
  build-time:
    description: 'Build timestamp'
    value: ${{ steps.get-time.outputs.timestamp }}
  python-version:
    description: 'Python version used'
    value: ${{ steps.get-python.outputs.version }}

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - id: get-version
      shell: bash
      run: echo "version=$(cat VERSION)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: get-commit
      shell: bash
      run: echo "sha=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: get-time
      shell: bash
      run: echo "timestamp=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: get-python
      shell: bash
      run: echo "version=$(python --version | cut -d' ' -f2)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

# Usage in workflow
---
name: Use JobPost Build Info

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      
      - id: build-info
        uses: ./actions/jobpost-build-info
      
      - name: Display outputs
        run: |
          echo "📦 JobPost Build Information"
          echo "============================"
          echo "Version: ${{ steps.build-info.outputs.version }}"
          echo "Commit: ${{ steps.build-info.outputs.commit-sha }}"
          echo "Build Time: ${{ steps.build-info.outputs.build-time }}"
          echo "Python: ${{ steps.build-info.outputs.python-version }}"
    📦 JobPost composite action with multiple outputs

    2. JobPost test results output

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Test Runner with Results'
outputs:
  test-count:
    description: 'Total number of tests executed'
    value: ${{ steps.run-tests.outputs.count }}
  passed-count:
    description: 'Number of passed tests'
    value: ${{ steps.run-tests.outputs.passed }}
  failed-count:
    description: 'Number of failed tests'
    value: ${{ steps.run-tests.outputs.failed }}
  coverage-percent:
    description: 'Code coverage percentage'
    value: ${{ steps.run-tests.outputs.coverage }}
  duration-seconds:
    description: 'Test execution duration in seconds'
    value: ${{ steps.run-tests.outputs.duration }}

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - id: run-tests
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🧪 Running JobPost tests..."
        python -m pytest jobpost/ \
          --cov=jobpost \
          --cov-report=term \
          --junit-xml=test-results.xml \
          -v
        
        # Extract test counts
        TOTAL=$(grep -oP 'tests="\\K[0-9]+' test-results.xml | head -1)
        FAILED=$(grep -oP 'failures="\\K[0-9]+' test-results.xml | head -1)
        PASSED=$((TOTAL - FAILED))
        COVERAGE=$(coverage report | tail -1 | awk '{print $4}' | sed 's/%//')
        DURATION=$(grep -oP 'time="\\K[0-9.]+' test-results.xml | head -1)
        
        # Set outputs
        echo "count=$TOTAL" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        echo "passed=$PASSED" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        echo "failed=$FAILED" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        echo "coverage=$COVERAGE" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        echo "duration=$DURATION" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

# Usage workflow
---
name: JobPost Tests with Output Usage

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      
      - id: tests
        uses: ./actions/jobpost-test-runner
      
      - name: Check test results
        run: |
          echo "🧪 JobPost Test Results"
          echo "======================="
          echo "Total Tests: ${{ steps.tests.outputs.test-count }}"
          echo "Passed: ${{ steps.tests.outputs.passed-count }}"
          echo "Failed: ${{ steps.tests.outputs.failed-count }}"
          echo "Coverage: ${{ steps.tests.outputs.coverage-percent }}%"
          echo "Duration: ${{ steps.tests.outputs.duration-seconds }}s"
          
          # Fail if coverage below threshold
          if [ "${{ steps.tests.outputs.coverage-percent }}" -lt "80" ]; then
            echo "❌ Coverage below 80% threshold!"
            exit 1
          fi
    🧪 JobPost test runner with detailed test result outputs

    3. JobPost deployment outputs

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Deployment'
outputs:
  deployment-url:
    description: 'URL of the deployed JobPost application'
    value: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.url }}
  deployment-id:
    description: 'Unique deployment identifier'
    value: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.id }}
  migration-count:
    description: 'Number of database migrations applied'
    value: ${{ steps.migrations.outputs.count }}
  static-files-count:
    description: 'Number of static files collected'
    value: ${{ steps.static.outputs.count }}
  health-check-status:
    description: 'Health check status (healthy/degraded/unhealthy)'
    value: ${{ steps.health.outputs.status }}

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - id: migrations
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "Running migrations..."
        COUNT=$(python manage.py showmigrations --plan | grep -c "\[X\]")
        echo "count=$COUNT" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: static
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "Collecting static files..."
        COUNT=$(python manage.py collectstatic --noinput -v 0 | grep -c "static")
        echo "count=$COUNT" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: deploy
      shell: bash
      run: |
        DEPLOY_ID="jobpost-$(date +%s)"
        URL="https://staging.jobpost.example.com"
        echo "id=$DEPLOY_ID" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        echo "url=$URL" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: health
      shell: bash
      run: |
        STATUS="healthy"
        echo "status=$STATUS" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

# Multi-job workflow using outputs
---
name: JobPost Deploy Pipeline

on: [push]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      url: ${{ steps.deploy-action.outputs.deployment-url }}
      id: ${{ steps.deploy-action.outputs.deployment-id }}
      status: ${{ steps.deploy-action.outputs.health-check-status }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - id: deploy-action
        uses: ./actions/jobpost-deployment
      
      - name: Show deployment info
        run: |
          echo "🚀 Deployment: ${{ steps.deploy-action.outputs.deployment-id }}"
          echo "URL: ${{ steps.deploy-action.outputs.deployment-url }}"
          echo "Migrations: ${{ steps.deploy-action.outputs.migration-count }}"
          echo "Static Files: ${{ steps.deploy-action.outputs.static-files-count }}"
  
  notify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: deploy
    steps:
      - name: Send notification
        run: |
          echo "📢 Notifying team about deployment"
          echo "URL: ${{ needs.deploy.outputs.url }}"
          echo "ID: ${{ needs.deploy.outputs.id }}"
          echo "Status: ${{ needs.deploy.outputs.status }}"
    🚀 JobPost deployment with outputs used across jobs

    4. JobPost output size considerations

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Analysis Results'
outputs:
  summary:
    description: 'Brief summary of analysis (recommended)'
    value: ${{ steps.analyze.outputs.summary }}
  report-url:
    description: 'URL to full report (better than large output)'
    value: ${{ steps.analyze.outputs.url }}
  stats:
    description: 'Key statistics in JSON format'
    value: ${{ steps.analyze.outputs.stats }}

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - id: analyze
      shell: bash
      run: |
        # ✅ GOOD: Compact summary
        SUMMARY="JobPost: 150 files, 15k lines, 85% coverage"
        echo "summary=$SUMMARY" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        
        # ✅ GOOD: Reference to full report
        URL="https://reports.example.com/jobpost-123"
        echo "url=$URL" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        
        # ✅ GOOD: Structured data as JSON
        STATS='{"files":150,"lines":15000,"coverage":85}'
        echo "stats=$STATS" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
        
        # ❌ BAD: Don't output entire reports
        # FULL_REPORT=$(cat large-report.txt)  # Could exceed 1MB
        # echo "full-report=$FULL_REPORT" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

# Usage demonstrating output best practices
---
name: JobPost Analysis

on: [push]

jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - id: analysis
        uses: ./actions/jobpost-analysis
      
      - name: Use outputs properly
        run: |
          echo "📊 Output Best Practices"
          echo "========================"
          echo ""
          echo "✅ COMPACT OUTPUTS:"
          echo "Summary: ${{ steps.analysis.outputs.summary }}"
          echo "Report URL: ${{ steps.analysis.outputs.report-url }}"
          echo "Stats: ${{ steps.analysis.outputs.stats }}"
          echo ""
          echo "📏 OUTPUT SIZE LIMITS:"
          echo "• Per job: 1 MB maximum"
          echo "• Per workflow: 50 MB maximum"
          echo "• Encoding: UTF-16 based"
          echo ""
          echo "💡 BEST PRACTICES:"
          echo "✓ Use URLs for large reports"
          echo "✓ Upload artifacts for big files"
          echo "✓ Keep outputs concise"
          echo "✓ Use JSON for structured data"
          echo "✗ Don't output full logs"
          echo "✗ Avoid binary data"
          echo "✗ Don't exceed size limits"
    📊 JobPost action outputs with size considerations

    5. JobPost output naming best practices

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Build and Test'

outputs:
  # ✅ GOOD OUTPUT NAMES
  build-version:
    description: 'Semantic version of the build'
    value: ${{ steps.build.outputs.version }}
  
  test-status:
    description: 'Overall test status (passed/failed)'
    value: ${{ steps.test.outputs.status }}
  
  coverage-percentage:
    description: 'Code coverage as a percentage (0-100)'
    value: ${{ steps.coverage.outputs.percent }}
  
  artifact-name:
    description: 'Name of the uploaded build artifact'
    value: ${{ steps.artifact.outputs.name }}
  
  deployment-ready:
    description: 'Whether build is ready for deployment (true/false)'
    value: ${{ steps.check.outputs.ready }}
  
  # ✅ GOOD: Descriptive names with context
  database-migration-status:
    description: 'Status of database migrations'
    value: ${{ steps.db.outputs.migration_status }}
  
  # ❌ AVOID: Vague names
  # result: ...  # Result of what?
  # status: ...  # Status of what?
  # value: ...   # What value?

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - id: build
      shell: bash
      run: echo "version=1.2.3" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: test
      shell: bash
      run: echo "status=passed" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: coverage
      shell: bash
      run: echo "percent=87" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: artifact
      shell: bash
      run: echo "name=jobpost-1.2.3" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: check
      shell: bash
      run: echo "ready=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    
    - id: db
      shell: bash
      run: echo "migration_status=current" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

# Usage showing good output naming
---
name: JobPost Build Pipeline

on: [push]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - id: build-test
        uses: ./actions/jobpost-build-test
      
      - name: Output naming best practices
        run: |
          echo "📝 Output Naming Best Practices"
          echo "================================"
          echo ""
          echo "✅ GOOD NAMES:"
          echo "• build-version: ${{ steps.build-test.outputs.build-version }}"
          echo "• test-status: ${{ steps.build-test.outputs.test-status }}"
          echo "• coverage-percentage: ${{ steps.build-test.outputs.coverage-percentage }}"
          echo "• artifact-name: ${{ steps.build-test.outputs.artifact-name }}"
          echo "• deployment-ready: ${{ steps.build-test.outputs.deployment-ready }}"
          echo ""
          echo "NAME GUIDELINES:"
          echo "✓ Use kebab-case"
          echo "✓ Be specific and descriptive"
          echo "✓ Include context (what-type)"
          echo "✓ Use full words, not abbreviations"
          echo "✓ Match description content"
          echo ""
          echo "✗ AVOID:"
          echo "  ✗ Generic: result, value, data"
          echo "  ✗ Cryptic: v, st, pct"
          echo "  ✗ Ambiguous: status, output, info"
    📝 JobPost output naming conventions and best practices

    runs

    Docs: Action metadata - runs

    Specify whether this is a JavaScript action, composite action, or Docker container action and how the action is executed.

    Full description

    The runs configuration is required and specifies the execution model for your GitHub Action. It determines whether your action runs as JavaScript (Node.js), as a composite action combining multiple steps, or as a Docker container. For the JobPost Django application, you might use JavaScript actions for simple utilities, composite actions for multi-step workflows combining shell scripts and other actions, or Docker containers for complex environments requiring specific dependencies like PostgreSQL, Redis, or Python packages.

    Action Types and Execution Models

    JavaScript Actions: Fast execution, runs directly on the runner. Use Node.js v20 or v24. Best for simple operations and API integrations.

    Composite Actions: Combine multiple steps and actions. Use shell commands and existing actions. Best for workflow orchestration and reusable patterns.

    Docker Container Actions: Run in isolated containers. Can use any language/runtime. Best for complex dependencies and consistent environments.

    Syntax

    # JavaScript action
runs:
  using: 'node20'  # or 'node24'
  main: 'index.js'

# Composite action
runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - run: echo "Step 1"
      shell: bash

# Docker action
runs:
  using: 'docker'
  image: 'Dockerfile'

    Parameters description

    Runs configuration options:
    • using - Required. Specifies the runtime: 'node20', 'node24', 'composite', or 'docker'
    • main - Required for JavaScript. The file that contains your action code
    • steps - Required for composite. The steps to run in the action
    • image - Required for Docker. The Docker image to use as the container
    • pre - Optional for JavaScript. Script to run before main action
    • post - Optional for JavaScript. Script to run after main action
    • pre-if/post-if - Optional. Conditions for pre/post execution

    Return value

    The runs configuration doesn't return a value; it defines how GitHub Actions executes your action code and manages the action lifecycle.

    1. JobPost JavaScript action structure

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost API Notifier'
description: 'Send notifications to JobPost API'
inputs:
  api-url:
    description: 'JobPost API endpoint URL'
    required: true
  message:
    description: 'Notification message'
    required: true

runs:
  using: 'node20'
  main: 'dist/index.js'
  pre: 'dist/setup.js'
  post: 'dist/cleanup.js'
  pre-if: runner.os == 'linux'
  post-if: always()

# JavaScript implementation (dist/index.js)
---
const core = require('@actions/core');
const http = require('@actions/http-client');

async function run() {
  try {
    const apiUrl = core.getInput('api-url', { required: true });
    const message = core.getInput('message', { required: true });
    
    const client = new http.HttpClient('jobpost-action');
    const response = await client.post(apiUrl, JSON.stringify({ message }));
    
    core.setOutput('status', response.message.statusCode);
    core.info(`✅ Notification sent to JobPost API`);
  } catch (error) {
    core.setFailed(error.message);
  }
}

run();

# Usage in workflow
---
name: Notify JobPost API

on: [push]

jobs:
  notify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: ./actions/jobpost-api-notifier
        with:
          api-url: 'https://api.jobpost.example.com/notify'
          message: 'Build completed for commit ${{ github.sha }}'
    📡 JobPost JavaScript action with pre/post hooks

    2. JobPost composite action structure

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Django Setup'
description: 'Setup Python, Django, and JobPost dependencies'
inputs:
  python-version:
    description: 'Python version'
    required: false
    default: '3.11'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Setup Python
      uses: actions/setup-python@v5
      with:
        python-version: ${{ inputs.python-version }}
    
    - name: Cache dependencies
      uses: actions/cache@v4
      with:
        path: ~/.cache/pip
        key: jobpost-${{ runner.os }}-pip-${{ hashFiles('requirements.txt') }}
    
    - name: Install dependencies
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "📦 Installing JobPost dependencies..."
        pip install --upgrade pip
        pip install -r requirements.txt
    
    - name: Verify Django installation
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "✅ Verifying Django setup..."
        python -c "import django; print(f'Django {django.get_version()} installed')"
        python manage.py check --deploy
    
    - name: Display JobPost environment
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🔍 JobPost Environment Info"
        echo "=========================="
        echo "Python: $(python --version)"
        echo "Django: $(python -c 'import django; print(django.get_version())')"
        echo "Pip packages: $(pip list | wc -l) packages"

# Usage in workflow
---
name: JobPost CI

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: ./actions/jobpost-django-setup
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - run: python manage.py test
    🐍 JobPost composite action for Django setup

    3. JobPost Docker action structure

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Database Operations'
description: 'PostgreSQL operations in isolated container'
inputs:
  operation:
    description: 'Operation: migrate, backup, restore, seed'
    required: true
  database-url:
    description: 'PostgreSQL connection URL'
    required: true

runs:
  using: 'docker'
  image: 'Dockerfile'
  args:
    - ${{ inputs.operation }}
    - ${{ inputs.database-url }}
  env:
    POSTGRES_VERSION: '15'
    DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: 'anihpj.settings.production'

# Dockerfile
---
FROM python:3.11-slim

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \\
    postgresql-client-15 \\
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

COPY requirements.txt /app/
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r /app/requirements.txt

COPY entrypoint.sh /entrypoint.sh
RUN chmod +x /entrypoint.sh

ENTRYPOINT ["/entrypoint.sh"]

# entrypoint.sh
---
#!/bin/bash
set -e

OPERATION=$1
DATABASE_URL=$2

echo "🐘 JobPost Database Operation: $OPERATION"
echo "========================================"

case $OPERATION in
  migrate)
    python manage.py migrate --noinput
    echo "✅ Migrations completed"
    ;;
  backup)
    pg_dump $DATABASE_URL > backup.sql
    echo "✅ Backup created"
    ;;
  seed)
    python manage.py loaddata skills locations
    echo "✅ Fixtures loaded"
    ;;
  *)
    echo "❌ Unknown operation: $OPERATION"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

# Usage in workflow
---
name: JobPost Database

on: [workflow_dispatch]

jobs:
  db-operations:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: ./actions/jobpost-db-operations
        with:
          operation: 'migrate'
          database-url: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
    🐘 JobPost Docker action for database operations

    4. JobPost action type comparison

    # JavaScript Action - Fast execution, Node.js only
---
name: 'JobPost Quick Checker'
runs:
  using: 'node20'
  main: 'index.js'

# Pros:
# ✓ Fast startup (no container build)
# ✓ Direct runner access
# ✓ GitHub Actions toolkit available
# ✓ Good for API calls, file operations

# Cons:
# ✗ Node.js only
# ✗ Limited to runner capabilities
# ✗ No isolated environment

# Best for: API integrations, simple utilities, fast operations

---
# Composite Action - Multi-step, flexible, reusable
---
name: 'JobPost Test Suite'
runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
    - run: pip install pytest
      shell: bash
    - run: pytest
      shell: bash

# Pros:
# ✓ Combine multiple actions
# ✓ Use shell commands
# ✓ No build time
# ✓ Flexible and maintainable

# Cons:
# ✗ Runs on host (no isolation)
# ✗ Depends on runner OS
# ✗ No custom dependencies

# Best for: Workflow orchestration, reusable patterns, multi-step processes

---
# Docker Action - Isolated, consistent, any language
---
name: 'JobPost Full Stack Deploy'
runs:
  using: 'docker'
  image: 'Dockerfile'

# Pros:
# ✓ Complete isolation
# ✓ Any language/runtime
# ✓ Consistent environment
# ✓ Custom dependencies

# Cons:
# ✗ Slower (build + pull time)
# ✗ More complex setup
# ✗ Larger resource usage

# Best for: Complex dependencies, specific environments, cross-platform consistency

# Recommendation for JobPost:
---
# Use JavaScript for: API notifications, GitHub API operations
# Use Composite for: Django setup, test running, deployment steps
# Use Docker for: Database operations, security scanning, full environment needs

# Usage demonstration
---
name: JobPost Action Types Demo

on: [push]

jobs:
  demonstrate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Action type selection guide
        run: |
          echo "🎯 JobPost Action Type Selection Guide"
          echo "======================================="
          echo ""
          echo "📡 JavaScript Actions:"
          echo "  Use when:"
          echo "    • Need fast execution"
          echo "    • Working with GitHub API"
          echo "    • Simple file operations"
          echo "    • No special dependencies"
          echo ""
          echo "  JobPost examples:"
          echo "    • API notifiers"
          echo "    • Issue labelers"
          echo "    • Release validators"
          echo ""
          echo "🔧 Composite Actions:"
          echo "  Use when:"
          echo "    • Combining multiple steps"
          echo "    • Reusing workflow patterns"
          echo "    • Standard tooling (pip, npm)"
          echo "    • Multi-language steps"
          echo ""
          echo "  JobPost examples:"
          echo "    • Django setup + test"
          echo "    • Lint + format + check"
          echo "    • Build + upload artifacts"
          echo ""
          echo "🐳 Docker Actions:"
          echo "  Use when:"
          echo "    • Need complete isolation"
          echo "    • Complex dependencies"
          echo "    • Specific OS/versions"
          echo "    • Cross-platform consistency"
          echo ""
          echo "  JobPost examples:"
          echo "    • Database operations"
          echo "    • Security scanning"
          echo "    • Full Django + PostgreSQL"
    🎯 JobPost action type comparison and selection guide

    5. JobPost pre and post hooks

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Test with Coverage'
description: 'Run tests with automatic coverage reporting'

runs:
  using: 'node20'
  main: 'dist/index.js'
  pre: 'dist/pre.js'
  post: 'dist/post.js'
  pre-if: runner.os == 'linux'
  post-if: always()

# dist/pre.js - Setup before main action
---
const core = require('@actions/core');
const exec = require('@actions/exec');

async function pre() {
  core.info('🔧 Pre-action: Setting up test environment...');
  
  // Install coverage tools
  await exec.exec('pip', ['install', 'coverage', 'pytest-cov']);
  
  // Create coverage config
  await exec.exec('bash', ['-c', 'echo "[run]\\nsource = jobpost" > .coveragerc']);
  
  core.info('✅ Pre-action complete');
}

pre();

# dist/index.js - Main action
---
const core = require('@actions/core');
const exec = require('@actions/exec');

async function run() {
  core.info('🧪 Running JobPost tests with coverage...');
  
  await exec.exec('pytest', [
    'jobpost/',
    '--cov=jobpost',
    '--cov-report=xml',
    '--cov-report=term'
  ]);
  
  core.info('✅ Tests complete');
}

run();

# dist/post.js - Cleanup and reporting after main action
---
const core = require('@actions/core');
const fs = require('fs');

async function post() {
  core.info('📊 Post-action: Processing coverage results...');
  
  // Read coverage report
  if (fs.existsSync('coverage.xml')) {
    const coverage = fs.readFileSync('coverage.xml', 'utf8');
    const match = coverage.match(/line-rate="([0-9.]+)"/);
    
    if (match) {
      const percent = (parseFloat(match[1]) * 100).toFixed(2);
      core.info(`Coverage: ${percent}%`);
      core.setOutput('coverage', percent);
    }
  }
  
  core.info('✅ Post-action complete');
}

post();

# Usage with conditional hooks
---
name: JobPost Test Pipeline

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'
      
      - id: test-action
        uses: ./actions/jobpost-test-coverage
      
      - name: Check coverage
        run: |
          echo "📊 Coverage Report"
          echo "=================="
          echo "Coverage: ${{ steps.test-action.outputs.coverage }}%"
          echo ""
          echo "Hook execution order:"
          echo "  1. pre.js (if pre-if condition met)"
          echo "  2. index.js (main action)"
          echo "  3. post.js (if post-if condition met)"
          echo ""
          echo "Conditional execution:"
          echo "  • pre-if: runner.os == 'linux'"
          echo "    (only runs on Linux runners)"
          echo "  • post-if: always()"
          echo "    (always runs, even on failure)"
    🔧 JobPost action with pre/post hooks and conditional execution

    branding

    Docs: Action metadata - branding

    Customize your action's appearance in GitHub Marketplace with a color and Feather icon.

    Full description

    The optional branding configuration allows you to personalize and distinguish your action in the GitHub Marketplace. You can choose a background color and a Feather icon (v4.28.0) to create a badge that appears next to your action name. For the JobPost Django application, thoughtful branding helps your custom actions stand out and convey their purpose at a glance—for example, using a blue "database" icon for database operations, a green "check-circle" for testing actions, or an orange "alert-triangle" for security scanning.

    Branding Configuration

    Color Options: white, black, yellow, blue, green, orange, red, purple, or gray-dark

    Icon Source: Feather icons v4.28.0 (https://feathericons.com)

    Omitted Icons: Brand icons and certain icons (coffee, columns, divide-*, frown, hexagon, key, meh, mouse-pointer, smile, tool, x-octagon) are not available

    Syntax

    branding:
  icon: 'award'
  color: 'green'

    Parameters description

    Branding options:
    • branding.color - Optional. Background color of the badge. Choose from: white, black, yellow, blue, green, orange, red, purple, gray-dark
    • branding.icon - Optional. Name of a Feather icon (v4.28.0) to display. Examples: database, check-circle, package, server, code, shield, lock, zap
    • Visual impact - Badges appear in GitHub Marketplace listings and action search results
    • Best practice - Choose icons and colors that represent your action's purpose
    ⚠️ Icon Restrictions:

    The following icons are omitted and cannot be used: coffee, columns, divide-circle, divide-square, divide, frown, hexagon, key, meh, mouse-pointer, smile, tool, x-octagon, plus all brand-specific icons.

    Return value

    Branding doesn't return values; it provides visual customization for your action's marketplace presence and helps users identify your action quickly.

    1. JobPost deployment action branding

    # action.yml
name: 'Deploy JobPost Django'
author: 'JobPost DevOps Team'
description: 'Deploy Django application with migrations and static collection'

branding:
  icon: 'upload-cloud'  # Represents deployment/upload
  color: 'blue'          # Professional, reliable

inputs:
  environment:
    description: 'Target environment'
    required: true

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Deploy JobPost
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🚀 Deploying JobPost to ${{ inputs.environment }}"
        echo ""
        echo "Branding:"
        echo "  Icon: upload-cloud (☁️)"
        echo "  Color: blue"
        echo "  Meaning: Cloud deployment, professional"
    🚀 JobPost deployment action with cloud upload branding

    2. JobPost database action branding

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Database Manager'
author: 'anihpj'
description: 'Manage PostgreSQL database operations'

branding:
  icon: 'database'  # Clear database representation
  color: 'purple'   # Distinct from other actions

inputs:
  operation:
    description: 'Database operation to perform'
    required: true

runs:
  using: 'docker'
  image: 'Dockerfile'

# Visual representation
---
name: Database Branding Demo

on: [push]

jobs:
  demo:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Show database branding
        run: |
          echo "💾 JobPost Database Manager Branding"
          echo "===================================="
          echo ""
          echo "Icon: database (🗄️)"
          echo "Color: purple"
          echo ""
          echo "Why this works:"
          echo "  ✓ Database icon = clear purpose"
          echo "  ✓ Purple = stands out from blue (deploy)"
          echo "  ✓ Professional appearance"
          echo "  ✓ Instantly recognizable"
    💾 JobPost database action with purple database icon

    3. JobPost security scanner branding

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Security Scanner'
author: 'JobPost Security Team'
description: 'Scan for vulnerabilities with Bandit, Safety, and pip-audit'

branding:
  icon: 'shield'   # Security/protection icon
  color: 'orange'  # Alert/warning color

inputs:
  scan-type:
    description: 'Type of security scan'
    required: true

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Security scan
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🔒 JobPost Security Scanner"
        echo "==========================="
        echo ""
        echo "Branding:"
        echo "  Icon: shield (🛡️)"
        echo "  Color: orange"
        echo ""
        echo "Design rationale:"
        echo "  • Shield = protection/security"
        echo "  • Orange = attention/caution"
        echo "  • Combination = security awareness"
        echo "  • Distinct from other JobPost actions"
    🔒 JobPost security scanner with orange shield branding

    4. JobPost test runner branding

    # action.yml
name: 'JobPost Test Suite'
author: 'JobPost QA Team'
description: 'Run Django tests with coverage and parallel execution'

branding:
  icon: 'check-circle'  # Success/validation icon
  color: 'green'        # Success color

inputs:
  test-path:
    description: 'Path to tests'
    required: false
    default: 'jobpost'

runs:
  using: 'composite'
  steps:
    - name: Run tests
      shell: bash
      run: |
        echo "🧪 JobPost Test Suite"
        echo "===================="
        echo ""
        echo "Branding:"
        echo "  Icon: check-circle (✅)"
        echo "  Color: green"
        echo ""
        echo "Symbolism:"
        echo "  • Check-circle = validation/testing"
        echo "  • Green = success/passing tests"
        echo "  • Positive, confident appearance"
        echo "  • Matches CI/CD success states"
    🧪 JobPost test action with green check-circle branding

    5. JobPost branding best practices

    # Comprehensive branding guide for JobPost actions

# ✅ GOOD BRANDING EXAMPLES
---
# Deployment Actions
name: 'Deploy Production'
branding:
  icon: 'upload-cloud'    # Deployment
  color: 'blue'           # Professional
# Alternative: 'package' + 'blue', 'server' + 'blue'

---
# Database Actions
name: 'Database Operations'
branding:
  icon: 'database'        # Database
  color: 'purple'         # Distinct
# Alternative: 'hard-drive' + 'purple', 'server' + 'purple'

---
# Testing Actions
name: 'Run Tests'
branding:
  icon: 'check-circle'    # Validation
  color: 'green'          # Success
# Alternative: 'check-square' + 'green', 'check' + 'green'

---
# Security Actions
name: 'Security Scan'
branding:
  icon: 'shield'          # Protection
  color: 'orange'         # Alert
# Alternative: 'lock' + 'red', 'alert-triangle' + 'orange'

---
# Build Actions
name: 'Build Application'
branding:
  icon: 'package'         # Build/package
  color: 'blue'           # Professional
# Alternative: 'box' + 'blue', 'archive' + 'blue'

---
# Linting/Quality Actions
name: 'Code Quality'
branding:
  icon: 'code'            # Code
  color: 'purple'         # Code-related
# Alternative: 'file-text' + 'purple', 'edit' + 'purple'

---
# Notification Actions
name: 'Send Notifications'
branding:
  icon: 'bell'            # Alert/notify
  color: 'yellow'         # Attention
# Alternative: 'message-circle' + 'yellow', 'mail' + 'yellow'

---
# Monitoring Actions
name: 'Health Check'
branding:
  icon: 'activity'        # Monitoring
  color: 'green'          # Health
# Alternative: 'heart' + 'green', 'eye' + 'blue'

---
# Cleanup Actions
name: 'Cleanup Resources'
branding:
  icon: 'trash-2'         # Cleanup
  color: 'red'            # Removal
# Alternative: 'x-circle' + 'red'

---
# Documentation Actions
name: 'Generate Docs'
branding:
  icon: 'book'            # Documentation
  color: 'blue'           # Information
# Alternative: 'file-text' + 'blue', 'book-open' + 'blue'

# ❌ AVOID THESE MISTAKES
---
# Don't use restricted icons
branding:
  icon: 'coffee'  # ❌ NOT AVAILABLE
  icon: 'smile'   # ❌ NOT AVAILABLE
  icon: 'key'     # ❌ NOT AVAILABLE

---
# Don't mismatch icon and purpose
branding:
  icon: 'heart'     # ❌ For database action?
  color: 'green'
  
---
# Don't use poor color contrasts
branding:
  icon: 'star'
  color: 'white'    # ❌ Poor visibility

# POPULAR ICON CHOICES FOR JOBPOST
---
name: JobPost Branding Guide

on: [workflow_dispatch]

jobs:
  guide:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Branding best practices
        run: |
          echo "🎨 JobPost Action Branding Guide"
          echo "================================="
          echo ""
          echo "AVAILABLE COLORS:"
          echo "  • white - Clean, minimal"
          echo "  • black - Bold, strong"
          echo "  • yellow - Warning, attention"
          echo "  • blue - Professional, default"
          echo "  • green - Success, health"
          echo "  • orange - Alert, caution"
          echo "  • red - Error, critical"
          echo "  • purple - Code, data"
          echo "  • gray-dark - Neutral, utility"
          echo ""
          echo "POPULAR ICONS:"
          echo "  • database - Database operations"
          echo "  • shield - Security"
          echo "  • check-circle - Testing"
          echo "  • upload-cloud - Deployment"
          echo "  • package - Build"
          echo "  • code - Code quality"
          echo "  • bell - Notifications"
          echo "  • lock - Authentication"
          echo "  • zap - Fast/performance"
          echo "  • activity - Monitoring"
          echo ""
          echo "DESIGN PRINCIPLES:"
          echo "  1. Match icon to action purpose"
          echo "  2. Use color to categorize"
          echo "  3. Be consistent across related actions"
          echo "  4. Consider marketplace visibility"
          echo "  5. Test icon clarity at small sizes"
          echo ""
          echo "JOBPOST ACTION CATEGORIES:"
          echo ""
          echo "📦 Infrastructure (blue):"
          echo "  • upload-cloud, server, package"
          echo ""
          echo "💾 Data (purple):"
          echo "  • database, hard-drive, archive"
          echo ""
          echo "🧪 Quality (green):"
          echo "  • check-circle, check-square, activity"
          echo ""
          echo "🔒 Security (orange/red):"
          echo "  • shield, lock, alert-triangle"
          echo ""
          echo "⚡ Performance (yellow):"
          echo "  • zap, trending-up, cpu"
          echo ""
          echo "📢 Communication (yellow):"
          echo "  • bell, message-circle, mail"
    🎨 Comprehensive JobPost action branding guide
